result.subtopic: Single-cell RNA-seq foundations and droplet-based methods: Drop-seq, 10x Chromium, inDrop, UMI count-data statistics, the biology of transcriptional heterogeneity, and what a modern scRNA-seq experiment produces
result.synthesis:
Droplet microfluidics converted single-cell transcriptomics from a boutique, plate-based assay into a genome-scale census technology. Three 2015-2017 papers define the platform lineage our HNF1A-MODY islet study will run on. Macosko et al. (Drop-seq, Cell 2015) and Klein et al. (inDrop, Cell 2015) appeared back-to-back and share the same core trick: co-encapsulate one cell and one barcoded bead in a nanoliter aqueous-in-oil droplet, lyse in situ, and capture polyadenylated mRNA on oligo-dT primers carrying a bead-specific cell barcode and a molecule-specific Unique Molecular Identifier (UMI). Drop-seq beads carry a 12-nt split-pool cell barcode + 8-nt UMI and use template-switching (SMART) reverse transcription; inDrop uses photocleavable hydrogel beads, a split-pool barcode, and CEL-seq linear (IVT) amplification, capturing >75% of input cells (better for small samples). Zheng et al. (10x Chromium, Nat. Commun. 2017) industrialized the approach with gel-bead-in-emulsion (GEM) chemistry (16-nt cell barcode, 10-12-nt UMI), ~50% capture in ~6 min, and profiled ~250,000 cells including the 68k-PBMC benchmark; 10x now dominates published islet and MODY datasets. The common output is a genes x cells sparse integer UMI count matrix (typically 20-30k genes, 10^3-10^5 cells, 90-95% zeros, 1-5k median UMIs/cell), plus the ambient-RNA and empty-droplet artifacts that droplet encapsulation introduces.

A second, statistically load-bearing thread concerns why cells differ in expression and how UMI counts behave. Biological heterogeneity is real (discrete cell types, continuous trajectories, cell-cycle and metabolic states, and stochastic transcriptional bursting that makes even isogenic cells transcriptomically distinct), which Wagner, Regev & Yosef (Nat. Biotechnol. 2016) frame as recovering the low-dimensional "vectors of cellular identity." Superimposed on this biology is a well-characterized measurement process. Kivioja et al. (Nat. Methods 2012) introduced UMIs precisely to convert amplification-biased read counts into near-absolute molecule counts, with an occupancy/saturation correction for UMI collisions. The field then converged, after a genuine controversy, on the negative binomial (Gamma-Poisson) as the correct noise model: Svensson (Nat. Biotechnol. 2020) showed with droplet negative-control (RNA-spike) experiments that droplet scRNA-seq is NOT zero-inflated - observed zeros are exactly what count statistics predict - and Townes et al. (Genome Biol. 2019) showed UMI counts follow multinomial sampling with no zero inflation. This dissolved the earlier zero-inflated (ZINB/dropout) framing and simplifies any downstream generative model.

The practical consequence, and the part most relevant to a new clustering method, is that the standard preprocessing pipeline is provably distorting. Townes et al. demonstrated that log(CPM+1) normalization and highly-variable-gene (HVG) selection inject spurious variance that survives into PCA and clustering, and proposed GLM-PCA (exponential-family PCA under a Poisson/multinomial likelihood) with deviance-based feature selection instead. Hafemeister & Satija (sctransform, Genome Biol. 2019) proposed regularized-NB-regression Pearson residuals - regressing each gene on log sequencing depth and pooling dispersion across genes of similar abundance - to remove depth confounding while stabilizing variance. Choudhary & Satija (Genome Biol. 2022) then evaluated 59 datasets and refined the guidance: Poisson suffices for very sparse data but clear overdispersion appears once genes have adequate depth, mandating NB (variance = mu + mu^2/theta); this shipped as sctransform v2. Heumos et al. (Nat. Rev. Genet. 2023) codify the current end-to-end best-practice workflow (QC -> ambient/doublet removal -> count-model normalization -> HVG -> PCA -> kNN graph -> Leiden -> annotation). The state of the art is thus: a correct NB/multinomial count model exists, but production clustering still collapses it onto Euclidean PCA at a single arbitrary resolution with no calibrated uncertainty - exactly the seam our information-geometric, multiscale, conformal method targets.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) No principled metric from the count model. Standard pipelines apply log1p/HVG then Euclidean PCA + Leiden; Townes and Choudhary/Satija show this injects depth- and abundance-driven spurious variance. There is an open opportunity to define the inter-cell distance directly from the NB/multinomial likelihood via the Fisher-Rao (information) metric, which is precisely the "information-geometric" foundation our method advertises - none of the foundational pipelines do this. 2) Single-resolution clustering. Leiden/Louvain require a hand-tuned resolution; there is no accepted multiscale treatment despite cell identity being genuinely hierarchical (type -> subtype -> state), which our multiscale/topological formulation can fill. 3) Depth confounding of geometry. Number of detected genes correlates with library size; a metric that is not explicitly depth-invariant (as Pearson residuals regress out log depth) will cluster by sequencing depth, a known failure mode especially damaging for rare populations. 4) Rare-population detection. Resolving small disease-relevant subsets (e.g., HNF1A-mutant beta-cell substates in MODY islets) is exactly where overdispersion, sparsity, and ambient RNA corrupt log-normalized Euclidean distance; a count-faithful metric plus conformal confidence directly addresses whether a putative rare cluster is real. 5) No calibrated uncertainty. Zeros-are-not-dropouts (Svensson) removes the excuse for ad hoc imputation, but cluster assignments are still reported without confidence - the conformal layer is a clear gap none of these papers close. 6) Ambient RNA and empty droplets are ubiquitous but handled by separate ad hoc tools (EmptyDrops, SoupX/DecontX); a method that models the ambient profile jointly with the clustering geometry is unaddressed.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt the negative-binomial (Gamma-Poisson) generative model for UMI counts with the quadratic mean-variance law Var(Y)=mu+mu^2/theta and NB zero probability P(Y=0)=(theta/(theta+mu))^theta (Svensson; Choudhary & Satija); do NOT use zero-inflated/ZINB models for droplet data - they are unsupported. Replace log(CPM+1) input with variance-stabilized residuals: (a) sctransform Pearson residuals z_ij=(x_ij-mu_ij)/sqrt(mu_ij+mu_ij^2/theta_i) with mu_ij=exp(beta0_i+beta1_i*log10 m_j), m_j = cell depth, dispersion pooled across genes of similar mean (Hafemeister & Satija); and/or (b) Townes deviance residuals with Poisson deviance D=2*sum[y*log(y/mu)-(y-mu)] and binomial-deviance feature selection instead of HVG. For the information-geometric core, derive the base inter-cell metric as the Fisher-Rao metric of the multinomial/NB likelihood: on the probability simplex the square-root embedding p -> sqrt(p) maps to the unit sphere, giving the closed-form geodesic (Fisher-Rao angle / Hellinger) distance d(p,q)=2*arccos(sum_i sqrt(p_i*q_i)); this is the exact Riemannian metric a multinomial count model induces and connects our "information geometry" claim to the actual data likelihood. Use GLM-PCA (exponential-family/multinomial latent factorization via Fisher scoring) rather than plain PCA for the low-dimensional embedding the clustering runs on (Townes). Apply UMI saturation/collision correction when depth is high: estimated molecules n = -N*ln(1 - k/N) with N=4^L possible UMIs and k observed distinct UMIs (Kivioja occupancy correction). For preprocessing that feeds the geometry, incorporate empty-droplet calling (EmptyDrops multinomial test against the ambient profile / barcode-rank knee) and ambient-RNA decontamination (SoupX/DecontX) so the manifold is not distorted by soup. Cite Drop-seq/inDrop/10x for the barcode+UMI data model (16-nt CB + 10-12-nt UMI for 10x) and use the 10x 68k-PBMC and pancreatic-islet atlases as ground-truth benchmarks for the clustering/conformal evaluation.

papers.citation: Macosko EZ, Basu A, Satija R, Nemesh J, Shekhar K, Goldman M, Tirosh I, Bialas AR, Kamitaki N, Martersteck EM, Trombetta JJ, Weitz DA, Sanes JR, Shalek AK, Regev A, McCarroll SA (2015). Highly Parallel Genome-wide Expression Profiling of Individual Cells Using Nanoliter Droplets. Cell 161(5):1202-1214.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced Drop-seq: co-encapsulation of single cells with split-pool barcoded beads in nanoliter droplets, tagging each transcript with a 12-nt cell barcode + 8-nt UMI via template-switching RT, enabling parallel digital expression profiling of tens of thousands of cells at low per-cell cost.
papers.relevance: One of the two foundational droplet methods that define the barcode+UMI count-matrix data model our clustering consumes; establishes the STAMP/UMI framework and the sparse count output we must model geometrically.
papers.quantitative_findings: Profiled 44,808 mouse retinal cells; resolved 39 transcriptionally distinct populations; drove reagent cost toward ~$0.07 per cell.
papers.approx_impact: Landmark; ~6,200 citations (Semantic Scholar) / ~9,000+ (Google Scholar).

papers.citation: Klein AM, Mazutis L, Akartuna I, Tallapragada N, Veres A, Li V, Peshkin L, Weitz DA, Kirschner MW (2015). Droplet Barcoding for Single-Cell Transcriptomics Applied to Embryonic Stem Cells. Cell 161(5):1187-1201.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced inDrop: photocleavable hydrogel barcoded beads + split-pool cell barcodes + UMIs with CEL-seq (IVT linear) amplification, a droplet platform optimized for high cell-capture efficiency and small samples.
papers.relevance: The second foundational droplet method named in our scope; its high-capture, low-input design and linear-amplification chemistry contrast with Drop-seq/10x template-switching and inform noise-model assumptions for rare-cell (MODY) detection.
papers.quantitative_findings: Captures/profiles >75% of input cells even in very small samples; demonstrated on mouse embryonic stem cell differentiation over scales of thousands-to-tens-of-thousands of cells.
papers.approx_impact: Foundational; ~2,900 citations (Semantic Scholar) / ~3,500 (Google Scholar).

papers.citation: Zheng GXY, Terry JM, Belgrader P, Ryvkin P, Bent ZW, Wilson R, Ziraldo SB, Wheeler TD, McDermott GP, Zhu J, ... Bielas JH (2017). Massively parallel digital transcriptional profiling of single cells. Nature Communications 8:14049.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced/validated the 10x Genomics Chromium GEM (gel-bead-in-emulsion) platform: 16-nt cell barcode + 10-12-nt UMI, ~50% capture in ~6 min, commoditizing droplet scRNA-seq at scale.
papers.relevance: The dominant platform for modern islet and MODY datasets; defines the exact count matrices and benchmark datasets (68k PBMCs) our method will be tested on, making it the reference for 'what a modern scRNA-seq experiment produces.'
papers.quantitative_findings: Collected transcriptomes from ~250,000 single cells across 29 samples; released the widely used 68k-PBMC benchmark; ~50% cell-capture efficiency.
papers.approx_impact: Field-defining; several thousand citations (~5,000+, Google Scholar).

papers.citation: Kivioja T, Vahararautio A, Karlsson K, Bonke M, Enge M, Linnarsson S, Taipale J (2012). Counting absolute numbers of molecules using unique molecular identifiers. Nature Methods 9(1):72-74.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced Unique Molecular Identifiers (UMIs): random barcodes attached before amplification so that distinct molecules are counted rather than reads, removing PCR amplification bias and enabling near-absolute molecule quantification.
papers.relevance: UMIs are the reason droplet scRNA-seq yields count (not intensity) data with tractable Poisson/NB statistics; the UMI collision/saturation correction is a preprocessing step we should apply at high depth before building the information-geometric metric.
papers.quantitative_findings: Occupancy correction: true molecules n = -N*ln(1 - k/N), with N=4^L possible UMIs and k observed distinct UMIs; demonstrated on genome-scale karyotyping and Drosophila mRNA-seq.
papers.approx_impact: Foundational methods paper; ~1,500+ citations.

papers.citation: Wagner A, Regev A, Yosef N (2016). Revealing the vectors of cellular identity with single-cell genomics. Nature Biotechnology 34(11):1145-1160.
papers.key_contribution: Conceptual framework casting cellular identity as recoverable low-dimensional 'vectors' (discrete types, continuous states, spatial/temporal axes) from single-cell data, and the computational challenges of doing so.
papers.relevance: Provides the biological rationale for why cells differ in expression and what a clustering method should recover; motivates multiscale/continuous structure (type -> subtype -> state) that our multiscale, topologically-aware clustering targets.
papers.quantitative_findings: Review/perspective; synthesizes early atlas-scale studies rather than reporting a single dataset result.
papers.approx_impact: Highly cited conceptual review; ~1,000+ citations.

papers.citation: Townes FW, Hicks SC, Aryee MJ, Irizarry RA (2019). Feature selection and dimension reduction for single-cell RNA-Seq based on a multinomial model. Genome Biology 20:295.
papers.key_contribution: Showed UMI counts follow multinomial sampling with no zero inflation; proved log-CPM normalization and HVG selection create false variability; proposed GLM-PCA (exponential-family PCA) and deviance-based feature selection.
papers.relevance: Directly load-bearing for our embedding and metric choices: replaces log1p/PCA with a likelihood-consistent factorization and deviance residuals, the count-faithful input our information-geometric clustering should run on.
papers.quantitative_findings: GLM-PCA + deviance feature selection outperformed log-CPM/HVG/PCA on ground-truth clustering benchmarks; Poisson deviance D=2*sum[y*log(y/mu)-(y-mu)].
papers.approx_impact: Influential methods paper; ~700+ citations.

papers.citation: Svensson V (2020). Droplet scRNA-seq is not zero-inflated. Nature Biotechnology 38(2):147-150.
papers.key_contribution: Used droplet negative-control (homogeneous RNA) experiments to show observed zeros match plain count statistics; droplet data are well-described by the negative binomial (quadratic mean-variance) with no excess dropout.
papers.relevance: Settles the noise-model question in our favor: we can build the manifold on NB/multinomial geometry without zero-inflation or imputation, simplifying the statistical model underlying the Fisher-Rao metric.
papers.quantitative_findings: Droplet data adequately modeled by NB; only plate-based data needed zero inflation; NB variance = mu + mu^2/theta with quadratic mean-variance relationship.
papers.approx_impact: Widely cited correction to the 'dropout' narrative; ~330+ citations.

papers.citation: Choudhary S, Satija R (2022). Comparison and evaluation of statistical error models for scRNA-seq. Genome Biology 23:27.
papers.key_contribution: Benchmarked Poisson vs negative-binomial error models across 59 datasets; found Poisson adequate only for very sparse data but clear overdispersion once genes have sufficient depth, mandating NB; shipped as sctransform v2 (regularized-NB Pearson residuals).
papers.relevance: Recent (2021-2026) high-impact guidance fixing the exact normalization/variance-stabilization step that feeds our geometry; justifies a depth-regularized NB Pearson-residual input and warns against over-parameterized per-gene dispersion.
papers.quantitative_findings: Across 59 datasets NB overdispersion is depth-dependent; sctransform v2 improved variable-gene selection and differential-expression downstream performance; NB variance = mu + mu^2/theta with pooled theta.
papers.approx_impact: Influential recent methods paper; ~400+ citations and growing.

result.subtopic: Statistical count models for scRNA-seq normalization: the correct noise model for UMI counts (negative binomial vs Poisson vs zero-inflated, overdispersion, Pearson/deviance residuals, sctransform, GLM-PCA)

papers.citation: Grün D, Kester L, van Oudenaarden A (2014). Validation of noise models for single-cell transcriptomics. Nature Methods 11(6):637-640. DOI:10.1038/nmeth.2930
papers.key_contribution: The foundational empirical decomposition of scRNA-seq technical noise. Using ERCC spike-ins and single-molecule FISH ground truth, decomposes total variability into (i) Poisson sampling noise (shot noise, CV^2 = 1/mu) and (ii) global cell-to-cell variation in capture/sequencing efficiency, and shows the combination yields a negative-binomial (gamma-Poisson) count distribution. Establishes the mean-variance relationship Var = mu + mu^2/theta empirically and validates it against absolute molecule counts.
papers.relevance: This is the empirical justification for using the NB likelihood (not Gaussian-on-log) as the measurement model. For our information-geometric metric we need the true Fisher information of the per-cell count distribution; Grun supplies the validated variance function Var(mu) = mu + mu^2/theta that defines that metric. Also the smFISH-validated framing is the template for stress-testing that HNF1A-driven low-expression regulatory shifts are signal, not sampling noise.
papers.quantitative_findings: Technical noise dominated by Poisson counting at low counts; global efficiency variation adds a multiplicative gamma component. Spike-in derived overdispersion; ES cell expression variability shown to be culture-condition dependent after subtracting the validated technical noise floor.
papers.approx_impact: ~728 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); foundational classic.

papers.citation: Hafemeister C, Satija R (2019). Normalization and variance stabilization of single-cell RNA-seq data using regularized negative binomial regression. Genome Biology 20:296. DOI:10.1186/s13059-019-1874-1
papers.key_contribution: sctransform (v1). Fits a per-gene NB GLM log(E[x_cg]) = beta_0g + beta_1g * log10(m_c), where m_c is the cell's total UMI count (sequencing depth), then REGULARIZES the three parameters {beta_0, beta_1, theta} by kernel-smoothing them against log10 mean expression to avoid per-gene overfitting. The output normalized value is the Pearson residual z_cg = (x_cg - mu_cg)/sqrt(mu_cg + mu_cg^2/theta_g), clipped to +/-sqrt(N). Eliminates the pseudocount, log-transform, and heuristic HVG selection.
papers.relevance: The reference implementation of residual-based normalization and the direct competitor/baseline for our normalization front-end. The regularized mean-variance surface is exactly the local geometry our multiscale method must respect. Its documented failure mode (overamplifying rare high-count genes, requiring ad hoc clipping) is a concrete gap our conformal-confidence and Fisher-Rao distance are positioned to fix.
papers.quantitative_findings: Shows unconstrained per-gene NB overfits (spurious theta at low mean); regularization stabilizes parameters. Pearson residuals remove depth as the dominant PC and improve downstream clustering vs log-CPM across multiple 10x datasets.
papers.approx_impact: ~3,790 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the dominant residual-normalization method, integrated in Seurat.

papers.citation: Townes FW, Hicks SC, Aryee MJ, Irizarry RA (2019). Feature selection and dimension reduction for single-cell RNA-Seq based on a multinomial model. Genome Biology 20:295. DOI:10.1186/s13059-019-1861-6
papers.key_contribution: Casts UMI counts as multinomial sampling (per cell, n_c draws over genes with probabilities pi_cg), approximated by independent Poisson or NB. Introduces (i) DEVIANCE-based feature selection (binomial/multinomial deviance replaces highly-variable-gene selection on log-normalized data) and (ii) GLM-PCA, a low-rank factorization of the natural parameters under a Poisson/NB likelihood (log mu_cg = offset + sum_l U_cl V_gl, fit by Fisher scoring), plus fast null-residual (Pearson/deviance) approximations. Argues log-CPM + HVG injects false variance.
papers.relevance: The most information-geometric of the classics: GLM-PCA optimizes the exponential-family likelihood directly rather than Euclidean distance on transformed values, which is precisely the philosophy of our Fisher-Rao/natural-parameter clustering. Deviance is a divergence (2x log-likelihood-ratio), so deviance feature selection and deviance residuals are the discrete analogues of the geodesic distances we want to build on.
papers.quantitative_findings: Negative-control (technical) data shown to follow multinomial with NO zero inflation. Deviance feature selection + GLM-PCA outperform log-CPM/HVG/PCA on ground-truth clustering benchmarks (higher adjusted Rand / silhouette on labeled mixtures).
papers.approx_impact: ~454 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the canonical multinomial/GLM-PCA reference; implemented in scry/glmpca.

papers.citation: Svensson V (2020). Droplet scRNA-seq is not zero-inflated. Nature Biotechnology 38:147-150. DOI:10.1038/s41587-019-0379-5
papers.key_contribution: Empirically settles the zero-inflation question for UMI/droplet data. Fits negative-control droplet datasets (cells replaced by homogeneous RNA, so only technical noise remains) and shows the observed fraction of zeros per gene is fully consistent with a plain NB (gamma-Poisson) model, with essentially zero extra zero-inflation parameter. Excess zeros are a consequence of low mean x low capture, not a distinct technical dropout process.
papers.relevance: Directly justifies dropping the ZI component: for our HNF1A UMI islet data we should model counts as NB/Poisson, not ZINB. This shrinks the parameter space of the noise model that our conformal calibration must account for, and rules out treating zeros as missing-not-at-random when computing cell-cell distances.
papers.quantitative_findings: Across negative-control and real 10x datasets, fitted zero-inflation ~0; the mean-zero relationship follows the NB expectation P(0)=(theta/(theta+mu))^theta. Zero-inflation was detectable only in non-UMI full-length (SMART-seq)-style data.
papers.approx_impact: ~310 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the standard citation for 'UMI counts are not zero-inflated', with a published exchange (Cao/Kim vs Svensson) in Nat Biotechnol 2021.

papers.citation: Sarkar A, Stephens M (2021). Separating measurement and expression models clarifies confusion in single-cell RNA sequencing analysis. Nature Genetics 53:770-777. DOI:10.1038/s41588-021-00873-4
papers.key_contribution: Provides the clean generative framework: a Poisson (or binomial) MEASUREMENT model x_cg | lambda_cg ~ Poisson(s_c * lambda_cg) composed with a separate EXPRESSION model for the latent relative abundance lambda_cg (e.g., point-gamma, gamma, or nonparametric). Marginalizing a gamma expression model over Poisson measurement yields NB; a point-gamma yields ZINB. Shows apparent 'zero inflation' and 'dropout' are artefacts of conflating the two models, and that a Poisson measurement model is the right, minimal starting point.
papers.relevance: This is the theoretical scaffold for a principled information geometry: it isolates the biological latent state (what we want to cluster) from the measurement channel (what conformal prediction must calibrate against). It licenses deconvolving the measurement noise before/while defining cell-cell divergences, and tells us when NB vs ZINB is warranted per gene via a formal likelihood comparison.
papers.quantitative_findings: Fits nonparametric expression distributions per gene across many datasets; a gamma (NB) expression model suffices for most genes, with limited genes needing a point-gamma (zero-inflation) component; unimodal/nonparametric fits rarely improve over gamma for UMI data.
papers.approx_impact: ~213 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the definitive 'measurement vs expression' formalization.

papers.citation: Lause J, Berens P, Kobak D (2021). Analytic Pearson residuals for normalization of single-cell RNA-seq UMI data. Genome Biology 22:258. DOI:10.1186/s13059-021-02451-7
papers.key_contribution: Derives a CLOSED-FORM alternative to sctransform. Uses the offset (independence) model mu_cg = (row_g * col_c)/total, i.e. expected count = (gene total)(cell total)/(grand total), with a SINGLE FIXED overdispersion theta (~100 for typical 10x data). Analytic Pearson residual r_cg = (x_cg - mu_cg)/sqrt(mu_cg + mu_cg^2/theta), clipped to +/-sqrt(n). Shows sctransform v1's per-gene depth-dependent intercept and per-gene theta estimation were statistically unnecessary and biased at low counts.
papers.relevance: Gives us a fast, hyperparameter-light, analytic normalization to seed the pipeline, and exposes theta as essentially a single global knob for droplet UMI data. Its critique of per-gene theta instability motivates why a geometry-aware, pooled treatment of overdispersion (or a conformal wrapper) is preferable to gene-by-gene point estimates.
papers.quantitative_findings: Recommends theta=100 for UMI data; with theta -> infinity residuals reduce to Poisson (multinomial) residuals. Reproduces GLM-PCA-like embeddings at a fraction of the cost; clipping to sqrt(n) controls rare-gene overamplification.
papers.approx_impact: ~176 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the standard analytic Pearson-residual reference (berenslab/umi-normalization).

papers.citation: Choudhary S, Satija R (2022). Comparison and evaluation of statistical error models for scRNA-seq. Genome Biology 23:27. DOI:10.1186/s13059-021-02584-9
papers.key_contribution: Systematic benchmark of error models (Poisson, NB with free vs regularized theta, offset/fixed-slope models) and the basis for sctransform v2. Diagnoses that v1 over-estimated overdispersion at low mean; v2 fixes the slope to 1 (a pure sequencing-depth offset), estimates theta with glmGamPoi, and regularizes/excludes unstable theta, yielding better-calibrated residuals and cross-dataset differential expression with fewer false positives under depth differences.
papers.relevance: Defines the current best practice for estimating the overdispersion surface theta(mu) that our metric depends on, and provides the calibration diagnostics (uniformity of residual variance vs mean) we should reuse to validate the count model before topological/conformal analysis on the islet data.
papers.quantitative_findings: Fixed-slope offset NB with regularized theta stabilizes residual variance across the mean range; reduces spurious DE across datasets with large depth disparities; theta estimation via glmGamPoi is both faster and less biased than v1's Poisson-GLM heuristic.
papers.approx_impact: ~485 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the reference error-model comparison and sctransform v2 source.

papers.citation: Ahlmann-Eltze C, Huber W (2023). Comparison of transformations for single-cell RNA-seq data. Nature Methods 20:665-672. DOI:10.1038/s41592-023-01814-1
papers.key_contribution: Large benchmark of four transformation families: (1) delta-method / variance-stabilizing transforms (shifted log log(y/s + y0), acosh/asinh VST), (2) model residuals (Pearson/sctransform), (3) latent-expression estimates, (4) GLM-PCA/factor analysis, evaluated by k-NN graph recovery against ground truth. Headline: a simple shifted-log with size factors followed by PCA is as good as or better than the sophisticated residual/latent methods, and Pearson residuals can distort rare cell types via outlier overamplification.
papers.relevance: The essential adversarial reality check: any sophisticated normalization (ours included) must beat log1p+PCA on downstream neighbor-graph fidelity. Its critique of Pearson-residual overamplification of rare populations is the strongest single motivation for our conformal-confidence and Fisher-Rao approach, which aim to preserve rare HNF1A-perturbed states rigorously rather than clip them.
papers.quantitative_findings: Shifted-log with per-cell size factors and optimized pseudocount y0 matches top methods on k-NN overlap; monotone acosh/asinh VST for NB behaves like log at high counts and sqrt at low counts; Pearson residuals showed instability/over-weighting of low-abundance genes and outliers. glmGamPoi used for scalable NB fits.
papers.approx_impact: ~117 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026); the current benchmark that recentered the field on simple VSTs.

result.synthesis:
The field has converged on a clear generative answer to 'what is the correct noise model for UMI counts,' while leaving the downstream geometry unresolved. The measurement channel is Poisson (multinomial across genes within a cell): observed count x_cg given a latent relative abundance lambda_cg is Poisson(s_c * lambda_cg), where s_c is a per-cell size factor (total UMIs). Cell-to-cell and biological variation in lambda_cg is modeled as gamma-distributed, and marginalizing gamma-over-Poisson gives the negative binomial with the empirically validated variance function Var = mu + mu^2/theta (Grun 2014; Sarkar and Stephens 2021). The zero-inflation debate is effectively settled for droplet UMI data: Svensson (2020) and Townes (2019) show negative-control data contain no excess zeros beyond NB expectation, and Sarkar and Stephens (2021) explain that apparent 'dropout' is just Poisson measurement of a continuous, often near-zero expression state. ZINB is warranted only for a minority of genes or for full-length (non-UMI) protocols. Practically, this means for HNF1A islet 10x data we should use NB/Poisson, not ZINB, and treat zeros as informative low counts rather than missing data.

On normalization, the community has moved decisively away from log(CPM + 1). The core objection (Lun 2018; Townes 2019; Hafemeister and Satija 2019) is that a pseudocount plus log distorts the mean-variance relationship, fails to stabilize variance across the enormous dynamic range of counts, makes the transformed value a biased proxy for log-fold-change when sequencing depth varies, and lets sequencing depth dominate the leading principal components. Two principled replacements dominate: (i) residual-based normalization, where the normalized value is the Pearson residual (x - mu)/sqrt(mu + mu^2/theta) from an NB GLM against sequencing depth (sctransform v1/v2: Hafemeister and Satija 2019; Choudhary and Satija 2022) or its closed-form offset-model version with a single global theta approximately 100 (analytic Pearson residuals: Lause et al 2021); and (ii) likelihood-based dimension reduction (GLM-PCA and deviance residuals/feature selection: Townes 2019), which fits the exponential-family natural parameters directly instead of Euclidean-transforming counts.

The most important recent development is a partial walk-back. Ahlmann-Eltze and Huber (2023) benchmarked the whole zoo against k-nearest-neighbor recovery and found that a simple shifted-log or acosh variance-stabilizing transform with proper size factors, followed by PCA, matches or beats the sophisticated residual and latent-variable methods, while Pearson residuals can actively harm rare-population recovery by overamplifying low-abundance genes and outliers (the same reason sctransform clips residuals to +/-sqrt(N)). The state of the art is therefore a tension: everyone agrees the generative model is NB-with-a-Poisson-core and no zero inflation, but the best way to turn that model into a Euclidean feature space for clustering is unsettled, and every method reduces the rich per-cell count likelihood to a single point estimate (a residual or a latent factor) before any distance is computed. That reduction is exactly where an information-geometric, uncertainty-aware method has room to contribute.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) Point estimates discard uncertainty. Every method above (Pearson/deviance residuals, GLM-PCA scores, shifted-log values) collapses the per-cell NB likelihood to a single number per gene, then runs Euclidean/PCA/kNN downstream. Nobody propagates the count-level measurement uncertainty (which is heteroskedastic and depth-dependent) into the distance metric or the clustering. This is the natural entry point for conformal confidence: calibrate cluster-assignment and neighbor-graph edges against the NB sampling variability, giving per-cell prediction sets with finite-sample coverage guarantees. 2) The metric is Euclidean but the model is not. The count family has a nontrivial Fisher information metric; residualization is only a first-order (delta-method) linearization of the true geometry, which is why it over/under-weights genes at the extremes of the mean range and mishandles rare cells (Ahlmann-Eltze and Huber 2023). A genuinely information-geometric (Fisher-Rao / alpha-divergence / Hellinger-on-the-simplex) cell-cell distance derived directly from the NB/multinomial likelihood has not been built into a multiscale clustering pipeline. 3) No multiscale treatment of overdispersion. theta(mu) sets the effective 'resolution' at which two cells are distinguishable, and it varies by an order of magnitude across the expression range; existing methods pick one residual scale. A multiscale/topological method can honor a mean-dependent metric across scales rather than fixing a single residual variance. 4) Rare-cell fidelity vs statistical stability. Pearson-residual clipping and fixed-theta are admitted hacks (Lause 2021; Ahlmann-Eltze 2023); a conformally calibrated, geometry-aware distance could preserve rare HNF1A-perturbed beta-cell states without the false-positive overamplification, and could flag low-confidence rare clusters instead of silently clipping them. 5) Weak signal regime. HNF1A MODY produces subtle shifts in low-to-moderately expressed transcription-factor and metabolic genes, precisely the count regime where log-normalization is worst and where residual overamplification is most dangerous; deviance-based selection plus an uncertainty-calibrated metric is the principled way to decide whether a subtle shift is signal or sampling noise (the modern analogue of Grun's smFISH validation).
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt as the front-end noise model the offset (size-factor) NB: x_cg ~ NB(mean = s_c * mu_g, dispersion theta_g), with s_c = (total counts of cell c)/(mean total), estimated mu_g from column-marginals, and theta from glmGamPoi (Ahlmann-Eltze and Huber's Gamma-Poisson fitter, Bioinformatics 2020) with sctransform-v2-style pooling/regularization of theta(mu) (Choudhary and Satija 2022). Justify dropping zero-inflation via Svensson (2020)/Sarkar and Stephens (2021); fit ZINB only per-gene where a likelihood-ratio test demands it. Concrete quantities to build on: (a) NB mean-variance function Var = mu + mu^2/theta, which defines the diagonal Fisher metric g_gg(mu) = 1/(mu + mu^2/theta) for a per-cell information-geometric distance -- use this instead of Euclidean-on-log. (b) Analytic Pearson residual r_cg = (x_cg - mu_cg)/sqrt(mu_cg + mu_cg^2/theta) with mu_cg = row_g*col_c/total and theta approximately 100 (Lause 2021) as a fast baseline embedding. (c) Deviance residuals and binomial/multinomial deviance D_g = 2*sum_c [x log(x/mu) - (x - mu)] for likelihood-based feature selection (Townes 2019), replacing HVG-on-log; deviance IS a divergence, so it aligns with the information-geometric objective. (d) The NB variance-stabilizing transform derived from the delta method, f(x) = 2*sqrt(theta)*asinh(sqrt(x/theta)) = 2*sqrt(theta)*log(sqrt(x/theta) + sqrt(1 + x/theta)), which interpolates between 2*sqrt(x) (Anscombe/Poisson, theta->inf) and log at high counts; equivalently the shifted-log log(x/s + y0) with optimized pseudocount (Ahlmann-Eltze and Huber 2023) as the map that renders NB counts approximately isometric to Euclidean space -- this is the bridge between our Fisher-Rao metric and a practical PCA/topology input. (e) GLM-PCA (Fisher-scoring low-rank fit of log mu_cg = offset + U_c . V_g under NB) as the likelihood-native embedding to compare our metric against and possibly to warm-start it. For the conformal layer: use the NB (or Poisson) predictive distribution per cell to generate nonconformity scores (e.g., deviance or Pearson residual magnitude, or negative log-likelihood of a cell under a candidate cluster's fitted NB model) and calibrate split-conformal/Mondrian prediction sets so that cluster membership and rare-cell calls carry finite-sample coverage. Always benchmark against the Ahlmann-Eltze and Huber protocol (k-NN overlap / graph recovery vs ground truth) and against log1p+PCA as the must-beat baseline, and validate the overdispersion fit with residual-variance-vs-mean uniformity diagnostics from Choudhary and Satija.

result.subtopic: Deep generative / autoencoder models for scRNA-seq: decoder architecture and count likelihoods (NB / ZINB), and use of the latent space for clustering

papers.citation: Lopez, R., Regier, J., Cole, M. B., Jordan, M. I. & Yosef, N. (2018). Deep generative modeling for single-cell transcriptomics (scVI). Nature Methods, 15(12), 1053-1058. DOI: 10.1038/s41592-018-0229-2.
papers.key_contribution: The canonical VAE for scRNA-seq. Generative model: latent biological state z_n ~ Normal(0, I); cell-specific library size l_n ~ LogNormal(l_mu, l_sigma^2) (batch-conditioned). A neural decoder f_w(z_n, s_n) maps to the (G-1)-simplex via a softmax, giving normalized expression frequencies rho_n; the NB mean is mu_ng = l_n * rho_ng. Counts follow a zero-inflated negative binomial x_ng ~ ZINB(mu_ng, theta_g, pi_ng), with gene-specific inverse dispersion theta_g and a second decoder head f_h producing dropout logits pi_ng (NB decoder is an option). This is realized as a Gamma-Poisson hierarchy (w ~ Gamma(theta, theta/mu), y ~ Poisson(w)) plus a Bernoulli zero-inflation gate. Trained by amortized/auto-encoding variational Bayes with factorized posterior q(z_n|x_n)q(l_n|x_n) and the reparameterization trick.
papers.relevance: Defines the decoder + count-likelihood template our paper must build on and beat. The softmax-simplex mean, gene-specific dispersion, and separate library-size latent are exactly the emission-model components whose Fisher information we would pull back to make the latent space information-geometric rather than naively Euclidean. Its posterior mean z-bar is the standard input to kNN+Leiden clustering, the pipeline we augment with multiscale/topological structure and conformal confidence.
papers.quantitative_findings: Latent dimension typically ~10; scales approximately linearly in the number of cells (demonstrated on the ~1.3M-cell mouse brain dataset). Benchmarked on imputation, batch correction, differential expression, and held-out log-likelihood, where it matched or exceeded prior tools; provides a Bayesian-hypothesis DE test from the posterior.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,970 citations (SciSpace/Semantic Scholar snapshot); substantially higher on Google Scholar (several thousand). Among the most-cited scRNA-seq methods papers.

papers.citation: Eraslan, G., Simon, L. M., Mircea, M., Mueller, N. S. & Theis, F. J. (2019). Single-cell RNA-seq denoising using a deep count autoencoder (DCA). Nature Communications, 10, 390. DOI: 10.1038/s41467-018-07931-2.
papers.key_contribution: A deterministic (non-variational) autoencoder whose decoder emits the three ZINB parameters per gene through separate output heads: mean mu (exp activation, scaled by per-cell size factors), inverse dispersion theta (softplus/exp), and dropout probability pi (sigmoid). The training loss is the negative ZINB log-likelihood, NB(x;mu,theta) = Gamma(x+theta)/(Gamma(theta) x!) * (theta/(theta+mu))^theta * (mu/(theta+mu))^x, gated by ZINB(x;mu,theta,pi) = pi*delta_0(x) + (1-pi)*NB(x;mu,theta). Establishes that a count-aware reconstruction loss, rather than MSE on log-normalized data, is what captures overdispersion, sparsity, and nonlinear gene-gene dependencies.
papers.relevance: The cleanest statement of the ZINB decoder/likelihood in isolation, decoupled from the VAE prior. It is the direct ablation baseline for our likelihood choice and shows the reconstruction term whose Fisher-Rao geometry we exploit. Its bottleneck is used for downstream clustering, making it a natural point of comparison for latent-space cluster quality.
papers.quantitative_findings: Scales linearly to millions of cells (runtime shown on ~1.3M cells). Improved recovery of biological signal on simulated and real data versus MAGIC, SAVER, and scImpute (higher correlation with reference/bulk, cleaner cell-type separation, improved DE and pseudotime); denoising strengthened cell-type-specific expression structure.
papers.approx_impact: ~611 citations (Scinapse/Semantic Scholar); higher (~1,000+) on Google Scholar.

papers.citation: Risso, D., Perraudeau, F., Gribkova, S., Dudoit, S. & Vert, J.-P. (2018). A general and flexible method for signal extraction from single-cell RNA-seq data (ZINB-WaVE). Nature Communications, 9, 284. DOI: 10.1038/s41467-017-02554-5.
papers.key_contribution: The pre-deep-learning ancestor of the ZINB decoder: a zero-inflated negative binomial factor model in which log(mu) and the zero-inflation logits are linear functions of known sample-level covariates X, known gene-level covariates V, and unknown low-dimensional latent factors W (the cell embedding). Estimation is by penalized maximum likelihood. Formalizes modeling counts directly (no prior log-normalization) while accounting for dropout, overdispersion, and library size.
papers.relevance: Grounds the likelihood our neural decoders inherit and provides the linear-decoder limit that LDVAE and interpretable variants recover. The W factor matrix is exactly the latent used for clustering/pseudotime, so it anchors the 'latent space used for clustering' lineage and motivates asking what geometry W should carry.
papers.quantitative_findings: Yielded more stable, accurate low-dimensional representations than PCA and ZIFA without a normalization step; improved downstream clustering and lineage inference; the fitted W integrates cleanly into standard clustering workflows. Also proposed observation weights to unlock bulk RNA-seq DE tools for zero-inflated single-cell data.
papers.approx_impact: Foundational and heavily cited (Nature Communications 2018; on the order of ~900+ citations). Exact count not independently re-verified here.

papers.citation: Xu, C., Lopez, R., Mehlman, E., Regier, J., Jordan, M. I. & Yosef, N. (2021). Probabilistic harmonization and annotation of single-cell transcriptomics data with deep generative models (scANVI). Molecular Systems Biology, 17(1), e9620. DOI: 10.15252/msb.20209620.
papers.key_contribution: A semi-supervised extension of scVI that adds a discrete cell-type latent c and a hierarchical prior z ~ p(z|c), so partial label information shapes the latent geometry. Retains the ZINB/NB decoder but the ELBO gains a classification term, letting the same generative model do integration, annotation/label transfer, and DE jointly.
papers.relevance: The template for injecting known islet cell identities and HNF1A-MODY prior knowledge into an otherwise unsupervised latent, directly informing how our confidence-calibrated clustering can be steered by (and evaluated against) curated labels. Its label-aware latent is a natural comparator for our conformal cluster-assignment guarantees.
papers.quantitative_findings: Improved label-transfer accuracy and dataset harmonization over unsupervised scVI and over marker-based transfer; robust when only a fraction of cells are labeled; produces a single generative model usable directly for downstream DE.
papers.approx_impact: Widely used integration/annotation method (several hundred citations; exact count not re-verified here). Distributed as a core model in scvi-tools.

papers.citation: Gayoso, A., Steier, Z., Lopez, R., Regier, J., Nazor, K. L., Streets, A. & Yosef, N. (2021). Joint probabilistic modeling of single-cell multi-omic data with totalVI. Nature Methods, 18(3), 272-282. DOI: 10.1038/s41592-020-01050-x.
papers.key_contribution: Extends the scVI decoder to CITE-seq by adding a second likelihood head for surface-protein counts: RNA uses the NB decoder, while proteins use a negative-binomial mixture that explicitly models a background/ambient component versus true signal (a two-component per-protein mixture), plus batch effects. A shared latent z generates both modalities, giving denoised, batch-corrected joint embeddings.
papers.relevance: Demonstrates the decoder-per-modality pattern and principled background modeling we can borrow if islet profiling includes protein/multimodal readouts, and shows how a shared latent supports joint clustering. The protein-background mixture is a concrete example of enriching the emission model beyond plain NB, relevant to our likelihood design.
papers.quantitative_findings: Outperformed naive concatenation and modality-specific baselines on integration and denoising; recovered protein background as an interpretable latent factor; enabled cross-CITE-seq-dataset integration and a matched multi-omic DE test.
papers.approx_impact: ~386 citations (Semantic Scholar); higher on Google Scholar.

papers.citation: Gayoso, A., Lopez, R., Xing, G., ... Streets, A., Regier, J., Jordan, M. I. & Yosef, N. (2022). A Python library for probabilistic analysis of single-cell omics data (scvi-tools). Nature Biotechnology, 40(2), 163-166. DOI: 10.1038/s41587-021-01206-w.
papers.key_contribution: The engineering unification of the whole family (scVI, scANVI, totalVI, LDVAE, peakVI, DestVI, etc.) on a common PyTorch/Pyro + AnnData stack with a standardized fit/get_latent_representation API and Scanpy/Seurat/Bioconductor interoperability. Codifies the canonical downstream recipe: extract the posterior-mean latent, build a kNN graph, and run Leiden/Louvain community detection for clustering.
papers.relevance: Defines the de facto baseline pipeline and interface our method will be benchmarked against and ideally plug into. It fixes the concrete latent-to-clusters procedure (kNN + Leiden) that our multiscale/topological + conformal layer replaces or augments, and provides the reference implementation for fair comparison.
papers.quantitative_findings: Ships a dozen-plus probabilistic models behind one API; adopted as the scverse standard for dimensionality reduction, clustering, annotation, DE, and multimodal analysis; enables reuse of pretrained reference models.
papers.approx_impact: High-impact software paper (many hundreds of citations and heavy real-world usage as the scverse deep-learning backbone).

papers.citation: Tian, T., Wan, J., Song, Q. & Wei, Z. (2019). Clustering single-cell RNA-seq data with a model-based deep learning approach (scDeepCluster). Nature Machine Intelligence, 1(4), 191-198. DOI: 10.1038/s42256-019-0037-0.
papers.key_contribution: The clearest 'latent-for-clustering' model: a ZINB denoising autoencoder (DCA-style decoder) whose embedding is jointly optimized with a Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) objective. It combines the negative ZINB log-likelihood reconstruction loss with a KL-divergence clustering loss between soft assignments q_ij (Student-t kernel over embedding-to-centroid distances) and a sharpened target distribution p_ij, so representation learning and cluster assignment are trained end-to-end rather than in two disjoint steps.
papers.relevance: The most direct prior art for coupling a count-likelihood decoder to an explicit clustering objective, and thus the key baseline for our topologically-aware, confidence-calibrated clustering. Its Student-t soft-assignment kernel and Euclidean centroid geometry are precisely what an information-geometric (Fisher-Rao) distance and conformal p-values would replace/augment.
papers.quantitative_findings: Reported higher ARI and NMI than PCA+k-means, DCA+k-means, DEC, CIDR, and MPSSC across simulated and real datasets; robust to increasing dropout rate and scalable to large cell numbers; end-to-end training improved separation over decoupled embed-then-cluster pipelines.
papers.approx_impact: Influential deep-clustering method for scRNA-seq (several hundred citations; spawned many autoencoder+GNN clustering follow-ups).

papers.citation: Svensson, V. (2020). Droplet scRNA-seq is not zero-inflated. Nature Biotechnology, 38(2), 147-150. DOI: 10.1038/s41587-019-0379-5.
papers.key_contribution: A careful empirical argument that, for UMI-based droplet data, the zero-inflation term in ZINB is largely unnecessary: observed zero counts in negative-control and real droplet data are consistent with a plain negative binomial (Gamma-Poisson) sampling model, so the pi*delta_0 gate mostly fits sampling zeros rather than a distinct biological/technical dropout process. Reframes the decoder likelihood choice as NB-by-default, ZINB-only-when-justified.
papers.relevance: Directly governs our decoder's likelihood specification and is central to the paper's gap analysis: it justifies defaulting to NB (matching modern scVI defaults) and testing zero-inflation as an ablation rather than assuming it. The debate it triggered (UMI-vs-non-UMI, Genome Biology 2022 reconciliations) is exactly where a calibrated/conformal treatment of likelihood adequacy adds value.
papers.quantitative_findings: Across droplet negative-control and biological datasets, NB fits the count distribution with little residual zero-inflation; apparent excess zeros are explained by low mean expression under Gamma-Poisson sampling. Non-UMI/full-length protocols (e.g., Smart-seq2) retain more genuine zero-inflation, delimiting where ZINB still helps.
papers.approx_impact: ~327 citations (SciSpace); widely cited as the reference point for the NB-vs-ZINB decoder debate.

result.synthesis:
The dominant paradigm for scRNA-seq representation learning is a count-generative model in which a low-dimensional latent captures biological state and a neural decoder maps it back to raw UMI counts under a negative binomial (NB) or zero-inflated NB (ZINB) likelihood. The lineage is tight and well-defined: ZINB-WaVE (Risso 2018) introduced the ZINB factor model with a linear decoder and penalized-ML estimation; DCA (Eraslan 2019) replaced the linear map with a deep autoencoder whose three output heads emit the ZINB parameters (mean, dispersion, dropout) and trained on the exact ZINB log-likelihood; and scVI (Lopez 2018) wrapped this in a VAE, adding a Normal(0, I) prior on the biological latent z, a separate LogNormal library-size latent l, a softmax decoder that outputs normalized frequencies rho on the (G-1)-simplex (so mu = l*rho), gene-specific inverse dispersion theta_g, and amortized variational inference via a factorized posterior q(z|x)q(l|x). scANVI, totalVI, and the scvi-tools framework then generalized this decoder: semi-supervised label-aware priors (scANVI), per-modality likelihoods with explicit protein-background mixtures (totalVI), and a unified library with a standardized latent-extraction API (scvi-tools). The near-universal downstream recipe is to take the posterior-mean latent, build a kNN graph, and run Leiden/Louvain community detection; scDeepCluster (Tian 2019) is the notable exception that folds a Deep Embedded Clustering objective (Student-t soft assignments vs a sharpened target, optimized jointly with the ZINB reconstruction) directly into training.

Two technical debates define the state of the art around the decoder and likelihood. First is the choice of emission distribution: Svensson (2020) showed that UMI droplet data are adequately described by NB without zero-inflation, and modern scVI defaults reflect this (NB, with ZINB and Poisson as options); zero-inflation remains defensible mainly for non-UMI/full-length protocols. Second is the parameterization of the mean and dispersion — the softmax-simplex mean with a cell-specific library-size factor and gene-specific (or cell-gene) dispersion is what makes these models count-faithful and is the component whose local curvature (the Fisher information of the NB emission) encodes a natural geometry on the latent. Practically, methods are evaluated with clustering/integration metrics: adjusted Rand index (ARI) and normalized mutual information (NMI) against reference labels, average silhouette width (ASW), and batch metrics such as kBET, iLISI/cLISI, now standardized in scIB-style benchmarks.

Critically for our proposal, this entire family treats the latent as a flat Euclidean space: distances for kNN graphs, Leiden clustering, and even scDeepCluster's Student-t kernel are computed with the ordinary L2 metric, ignoring that the decoder plus NB likelihood induce a non-Euclidean (Riemannian/Fisher-Rao) geometry on z. None of these methods provide multiscale/topological structure over the latent or finite-sample confidence guarantees on cluster membership; uncertainty, where present (scVI's posterior), is model-based and uncalibrated. That is precisely the opening for an information-geometric, multiscale, topologically-aware, conformally-calibrated method built on top of the well-validated NB/ZINB decoder.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) Euclidean-latent assumption. Every method above computes kNN/clustering distances with L2 on z, but the NB/ZINB decoder induces a Riemannian pullback metric G(z) = J(z)^T F(mu(z)) J(z), where J is the decoder Jacobian and F is the Fisher information of the emission. No scRNA-seq VAE uses this Fisher-Rao geometry for clustering — a direct information-geometric contribution. 2) No calibrated confidence. scVI yields a posterior but no finite-sample coverage on cluster assignments; conformal prediction over latent distances or decoder likelihood residuals would give distribution-free guarantees, which is novel here. 3) Single-scale clustering. Leiden-on-kNN and DEC produce one partition at one resolution; there is no principled multiscale/topological (persistent-homology) summary of latent structure, which matters for nested islet cell-type/subtype hierarchies. 4) Likelihood adequacy is assumed, not tested. Given the NB-vs-ZINB debate (Svensson 2020; UMI-or-not; Genome Biology 2022), a method that selects/validates the decoder likelihood per dataset (and reports miscalibration) is an open niche. 5) Rare-subpopulation collapse. VAE posterior over-smoothing and KL over-regularization can wash out rare states — a real risk for detecting HNF1A-MODY-specific islet perturbations, motivating geometry- and confidence-aware clustering that protects small clusters. 6) Interpretability of the decoder. Nonlinear decoders obscure which gene programs drive clusters; a linear/hybrid decoder (LDVAE-style) or Jacobian-based attribution linking latent axes to HNF1A target genes is an actionable add-on.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt the validated backbone and then layer our contributions. (a) Decoder + likelihood: use the scVI-style NB decoder as default — softmax decoder f_w(z,s) -> rho on the simplex, mean mu_ng = l_n * rho_ng with cell-specific library size l_n (either a LogNormal latent or observed size factors), gene-specific inverse dispersion theta_g, realized as the Gamma-Poisson mixture; keep ZINB (extra sigmoid dropout head pi_ng) and Poisson as switchable ablations, and report which the data support (per Svensson 2020). Train by amortized VI maximizing the ELBO L = E_q[log p(x|z,l)] - KL(q(z|x)||N(0,I)) - KL(q(l|x)||LogNormal) with reparameterization and KL warmup. (b) Information geometry: compute the Fisher information of the NB emission (for NB in mean-dispersion form, the per-gene Fisher term in mu is 1/(mu + mu^2/theta) = 1/(mu(1+mu/theta))), assemble F(mu) = diag of these, and pull it back through the decoder Jacobian to get the latent Riemannian metric G(z) = J^T F J; use geodesic / Fisher-Rao distances (or a symmetrized-KL / Hellinger surrogate between decoded NB distributions) in place of L2 for the kNN graph. (c) Multiscale/topological clustering: build the graph under G(z) and run Leiden (Traag, Waltman & van Eck 2019) across a resolution sweep, plus persistent-homology / persistence-diagram summaries to extract scale-stable clusters and nested structure. (d) Conformal confidence: calibrate cluster membership with split/inductive conformal prediction using a nonconformity score from the geometry (e.g., Fisher-Rao distance-to-centroid or negative decoder log-likelihood), yielding prediction sets with finite-sample coverage and flagging uncertain/rare cells. (e) Comparators: scDeepCluster's joint DEC+ZINB objective (KL between Student-t soft assignments q_ij and sharpened p_ij) as the end-to-end clustering baseline. (f) Prior injection: scANVI-style semi-supervised term to seed known islet identities and HNF1A-MODY labels; totalVI-style per-modality heads if protein/multimodal data are used. (g) Interpretability: an LDVAE linear-decoder variant (Svensson 2020, Bioinformatics) for gene-loading attribution of latent factors. (h) Evaluation: ARI, NMI, ASW for clustering quality; kBET and iLISI/cLISI for batch mixing; held-out NB/ZINB log-likelihood for decoder fit; and empirical coverage vs nominal alpha to validate the conformal guarantees — ideally within an scIB-style benchmark harness.

result.subtopic: Information geometry and the Fisher-Rao metric: from Rao (1945) and Čencov's uniqueness theorem through Amari's natural gradient to 2019-2025 machine-learning use (Fisher-Rao norms, computable Fisher-Rao distances between Gaussians, and information-geometric dimensionality reduction)

papers.citation: Rao, C. R. (1945). Information and the Accuracy Attainable in the Estimation of Statistical Parameters. Bulletin of the Calcutta Mathematical Society, 37, 81-91. (Reprinted in Breakthroughs in Statistics, Springer, 1992.)
papers.key_contribution: Founding paper of information geometry. Rao equipped the space of probability distributions {p(x;theta)} with the Fisher information matrix as a Riemannian metric tensor, g_ij(theta) = E[ (d/dtheta_i log p)(d/dtheta_j log p) ] = -E[ d^2/dtheta_i dtheta_j log p ], and defined the geodesic distance (the 'Rao distance') d_R(theta_0,theta_1) = inf over curves of the integral of sqrt( theta'(t)^T G(theta(t)) theta'(t) ) dt. The same paper introduced the Cramer-Rao lower bound Var(hat theta) >= I(theta)^{-1} and Rao-Blackwellization.
papers.relevance: This is the metric our method is built on. Treating each cell's (or cluster's) expression profile as a parameter of a statistical model (e.g. a multinomial over genes or a Gaussian in a latent space) makes the Fisher-Rao distance the natural, reparameterization-invariant dissimilarity for scRNA-seq clustering, replacing ad hoc Euclidean/cosine distances on log-normalized counts.
papers.quantitative_findings: Metric is d-dimensional for a d-parameter family; for the multinomial/categorical simplex the Fisher-Rao geometry is the positive orthant of the sphere under the sqrt-embedding p -> 2*sqrt(p), giving the closed-form geodesic distance d_R = 2*arccos( sum_i sqrt(p_i q_i) ) (twice the Bhattacharyya angle). One of the most consequential papers in 20th-century statistics; the Cramer-Rao bound alone is cited in tens of thousands of works.
papers.approx_impact: Foundational; effectively uncountable downstream citations through Cramer-Rao, Rao-Blackwell, and Fisher-Rao geometry.

papers.citation: Čencov (Chentsov), N. N. (1982). Statistical Decision Rules and Optimal Inference. Translations of Mathematical Monographs, Vol. 53, American Mathematical Society, Providence, RI. (Russian original: Nauka, Moscow, 1972.)
papers.key_contribution: Proved the uniqueness (categoricity) theorem: on the manifold of probability distributions over a finite sample space, the Fisher information metric is, up to an overall positive scalar, the ONLY Riemannian metric that is invariant (monotone / non-increasing) under Markov morphisms, i.e. under congruent embeddings and sufficient statistics. Also introduced the invariant family of alpha-connections and the third-order Amari-Chentsov tensor.
papers.relevance: This is the theoretical justification for why a clustering paper should use Fisher-Rao rather than any other Riemannian metric: it is the unique geometry consistent with the data-processing / sufficiency principle. Cell-type structure recovered under Fisher-Rao is invariant to sufficient reparameterizations of the counts (e.g. changing the gene-panel encoding), a property we can advertise as a correctness guarantee.
papers.quantitative_findings: Uniqueness is exact up to a single multiplicative constant. On the n-point probability simplex Delta^{n-1} the invariant metric is fixed to the Fisher metric; any monotone metric must be a scalar multiple of it.
papers.approx_impact: Foundational; the cornerstone invariance result of information geometry, cited across the entire field.

papers.citation: Amari, S. (1998). Natural Gradient Works Efficiently in Learning. Neural Computation, 10(2), 251-276.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced the natural gradient: the steepest-descent direction of a loss L(theta) with respect to the Fisher-Rao metric is grad_natural L = G(theta)^{-1} grad L(theta), where G is the Fisher information matrix. This update is invariant to smooth reparameterization and is asymptotically Fisher-efficient (attains the Cramer-Rao bound). The paper also gives adaptive/online natural-gradient learning and connects to the geometry of neural-network and blind-source-separation manifolds.
papers.relevance: Supplies the optimizer for any objective we define on the statistical manifold (e.g. maximizing between-cluster Fisher-Rao separation, or fitting a variational / latent embedding). Natural-gradient (and its practical K-FAC / diagonal-Fisher approximations) lets us optimize on the curved cluster manifold rather than in raw parameter space, improving conditioning and giving reparameterization invariance for free.
papers.quantitative_findings: Approximately 3,100 citations on SciSpace and several thousand more on Google Scholar; a seminal optimization paper. Establishes asymptotic optimality: natural-gradient online learning matches the accuracy of the (batch) maximum-likelihood estimator to leading order.
papers.approx_impact: Very high impact (multiple thousands of citations); the origin of natural-gradient / second-order-informed deep-learning optimizers.

papers.citation: Amari, S. and Nagaoka, H. (2000). Methods of Information Geometry. Translations of Mathematical Monographs, Vol. 191, American Mathematical Society / Oxford University Press. (Translated from the 1993 Japanese original.)
papers.key_contribution: The canonical monograph formalizing dually-flat statistical manifolds. Establishes the one-parameter family of alpha-connections and the crucial duality between the exponential (e-, alpha=+1) and mixture (m-, alpha=-1) connections with respect to the Fisher metric; the dual (theta, eta) coordinates linked by the Legendre transform of the log-partition function psi(theta); the canonical divergence, which equals the Bregman divergence generated by psi and coincides with the KL divergence for exponential families; and the generalized Pythagorean theorem and dual projection theorems.
papers.relevance: Provides the exact machinery for exponential-family and Gaussian cluster models: KL = Bregman(psi) gives an analytic dissimilarity, the Pythagorean/projection theorems let us do information-geometric 'centroid' and mixture decompositions (natural for a soft/multiscale clustering with a mixture model), and the e/m dual geodesics give principled interpolation paths between cell states.
papers.quantitative_findings: For any exponential family the Fisher metric is the Hessian of the log-partition function, G(theta) = Hessian psi(theta); the induced Bregman divergence B_psi(theta_1 : theta_2) equals KL(p_2 || p_1). The generalized Pythagorean relation D(P:Q)+D(Q:R)=D(P:R) holds when the m-geodesic PQ meets the e-geodesic QR orthogonally. Among the most-cited references in the field (thousands of citations).
papers.approx_impact: Very high impact; the standard textbook reference defining modern information geometry.

papers.citation: Ay, N., Jost, J., Lê, H. V., and Schwachhöfer, L. (2015). Information geometry and sufficient statistics. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 162(1-2), 327-364. (Expanded in their 2017 Springer monograph 'Information Geometry'.)
papers.key_contribution: Generalized Čencov's uniqueness theorem from finite sample spaces to arbitrary (including infinite / continuous, non-parametric) sample spaces. Shows that the Fisher metric and the Amari-Chentsov 3-tensor are, up to constants, the unique 2- and 3-tensors on the space of probability measures that are invariant / monotone under sufficient statistics (Markov morphisms), using a rigorous manifold-of-measures framework.
papers.relevance: Bridges the classical finite-alphabet theory to the continuous latent spaces we actually cluster in (Gaussian or nonparametric densities over an embedding). It guarantees that Fisher-Rao invariance still holds in the continuous regime where scRNA-seq embeddings live, and it names the Amari-Chentsov tensor as the natural higher-order (curvature/skewness) descriptor we can exploit for a topologically-aware, multiscale method.
papers.quantitative_findings: Extends invariance to infinite sample spaces; the characterization is exact up to two constants (one for the Fisher metric, one for the Amari-Chentsov tensor). Highly cited within mathematical information geometry (several hundred citations).
papers.approx_impact: High impact within the theory community; the definitive modern statement of the invariance/uniqueness principle.

papers.citation: Liang, T., Poggio, T., Rakhlin, A., and Stokes, J. (2019). Fisher-Rao Metric, Geometry, and Complexity of Neural Networks. Proceedings of the 22nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS), PMLR 89:888-896. (arXiv:1711.01530.)
papers.key_contribution: Defined the Fisher-Rao norm of network parameters, ||theta||_fr^2 = theta^T I(theta) theta (a quadratic form in the Fisher information matrix), and showed it is an invariant, geometry-aware capacity/complexity measure that is provably invariant to node-wise / layer-wise rescaling of ReLU networks and serves as an 'umbrella' lower-bounding several existing norm-based generalization measures (spectral, path, group norms).
papers.relevance: A modern, high-impact template for turning the Fisher-Rao metric into a scalar summary statistic with invariance guarantees - exactly the pattern we want for a confidence/complexity score on learned cluster representations. Its rescaling-invariance argument transfers directly to justifying that our cluster-separation statistic is unaffected by nuisance reparameterizations of the embedding network.
papers.quantitative_findings: Proves ||theta||_fr is invariant under the positive-rescaling symmetry group of ReLU nets and derives norm-comparison inequalities linking it to Rademacher-complexity-style generalization bounds; empirically tracks the generalization gap. Several hundred citations; a well-known reference for geometry-based generalization measures.
papers.approx_impact: High impact in ML theory (a few hundred citations); frequently cited in generalization / complexity-measure literature.

papers.citation: Nielsen, F. (2023). A Simple Approximation Method for the Fisher-Rao Distance between Multivariate Normal Distributions. Entropy, 25(4), 654.
papers.key_contribution: Gives a practical, provably-bounded algorithm to compute the Fisher-Rao distance between multivariate Gaussians (no closed form exists in general). It discretizes a curve joining the two Gaussians and approximates each short segment's Fisher-Rao length by the square root of the Jeffreys (symmetrized KL) divergence, sqrt(J(p_i, p_{i+1})); it also uses Calvo-Oller's isometric embedding of the Gaussian manifold into the SPD cone of (d+1)x(d+1) matrices to obtain guaranteed bounds.
papers.relevance: This is the concrete numerical tool that makes our method runnable: modeling each cell cluster as a Gaussian in a latent/PCA space, we need actual Fisher-Rao distances between those Gaussians for clustering, linkage, and the conformal-confidence step. The sqrt-Jeffreys segment rule and the Calvo-Oller SPD bound are directly implementable and cheap.
papers.quantitative_findings: Segment length approximated as sqrt(J) where J(p,q) = KL(p||q)+KL(q||p); accuracy improves with discretization density and the method sandwiches the true geodesic distance between lower/upper bounds via the Calvo-Oller embedding. Published in Entropy 25(4):654 (2023); part of a rapidly cited recent series by Nielsen on computable Fisher-Rao distances (see also arXiv:2403.10089, 2024, and 'On closed-form expressions for the Fisher-Rao distance', Information Geometry, 2024).
papers.approx_impact: Growing impact; the current go-to practical reference for Fisher-Rao distances between Gaussians.

papers.citation: Herrera-Esposito, D. and Burge, J. (2025). Supervised Quadratic Feature Analysis: An Information Geometry Approach to Dimensionality Reduction. arXiv:2502.00168 (OpenReview / preprint).
papers.key_contribution: Introduces SQFA, a supervised linear dimensionality-reduction method that learns a projection W maximizing the pairwise Fisher-Rao distances between class-conditional distributions (assumed Gaussian), i.e. it optimizes second-order (mean AND covariance) discriminability on the SPD manifold using the affine-invariant / Fisher-Rao geometry. A variant, SQFA-H, maximizes the Hellinger distance instead and further improves accuracy; features are designed to support quadratic (QDA) classifiers.
papers.relevance: The single most on-point recent paper: it is precisely 'use Fisher-Rao distances between class-conditional Gaussians to find a discriminative low-dimensional representation.' For our scRNA-seq method this is the supervised/known-label analogue (e.g. separating HNF1A-MODY islet states); we can adopt its Fisher-Rao-maximizing objective as the geometry-aware embedding/clustering criterion and its SPD-manifold optimization recipe.
papers.quantitative_findings: Evaluated on three real-world datasets; SQFA features are competitive with features maximizing more standard dissimilarity measures under QDA, and the Hellinger variant (SQFA-H) outperforms the Fisher-Rao variant and baselines. Open-source Python/PyTorch implementation (github.com/dherrera1911/sqfa, burgelab/SQFA). Very recent (2025), so citation count is still small but the method is directly transferable.
papers.approx_impact: New (2025), low citation count so far, but uniquely aligned with our approach; strong candidate for a direct baseline/ablation.

result.synthesis:
Information geometry rests on a single, remarkably rigid idea: the space of probability distributions is a Riemannian manifold whose metric is the Fisher information. Rao (1945) first equipped a parametric family {p(x;theta)} with the Fisher metric g_ij(theta)=E[d_i log p d_j log p] and the resulting geodesic 'Rao distance,' in the same paper that gave us the Cramer-Rao bound and Rao-Blackwellization. Why this metric and not another was settled by Čencov (1982), whose categoricity theorem proves the Fisher metric is the unique Riemannian metric (up to scale) invariant under sufficient statistics / Markov morphisms; Ay, Jost, Lê and Schwachhöfer (2015) later lifted this uniqueness - together with the Amari-Chentsov 3-tensor - to arbitrary and infinite sample spaces. Amari and Nagaoka (2000) then built the working machinery: dually-flat manifolds with the paired exponential (e-) and mixture (m-) connections, dual (theta, eta) coordinates via the Legendre transform of the log-partition function psi, and the fact that for exponential families the Fisher metric is exactly the Hessian of psi and the canonical (Bregman) divergence is the KL divergence - which delivers the generalized Pythagorean theorem and dual projections. This is the mathematically settled core: an invariant metric (Fisher-Rao), an invariant higher-order tensor (Amari-Chentsov), and an analytic divergence structure (Bregman/KL) for the exponential families that dominate applied modeling.

The machine-learning payoff of this geometry has two faces. First, as an optimizer: Amari's (1998) natural gradient, G(theta)^{-1} grad L, is the reparameterization-invariant, asymptotically Fisher-efficient steepest-descent direction on the statistical manifold, and it underlies modern second-order deep-learning methods (K-FAC and diagonal/blockwise Fisher approximations). Second, as a representation and complexity tool: Liang, Poggio, Rakhlin and Stokes (2019) turned the metric into the Fisher-Rao norm theta^T I(theta) theta, an invariant capacity measure that unifies norm-based generalization bounds and is provably immune to the rescaling symmetries of ReLU networks. The through-line is invariance - to reparameterization, to rescaling, to sufficient statistics - which is precisely the property a clustering method wants when raw features (gene counts, embeddings) are defined only up to nuisance transformations.

The most consequential recent development for an applied paper like ours is that Fisher-Rao distances have become computable and usable at scale. Because no closed form exists for general multivariate Gaussians, Nielsen (2023) gives a bounded, discretize-the-geodesic approximation using square-root-Jeffreys segment lengths plus the Calvo-Oller isometric embedding of the Gaussian manifold into the SPD cone (with follow-ups in 2024 tightening bounds and cataloguing closed forms). Building on exactly this, Herrera-Esposito and Burge (2025, SQFA) demonstrate the full loop for real data: learn a linear embedding that maximizes Fisher-Rao distances between class-conditional Gaussians on the SPD manifold to obtain discriminative features. In parallel, the single-cell field has begun importing these ideas (e.g. information-geometric quantification of cell-phenotype-transition manifolds, and geometry/topology-aware scRNA-seq distances), but a principled, invariance-grounded, multiscale Fisher-Rao clustering with statistical confidence has not yet been assembled - which is the opening our method targets.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) No invariance-grounded clustering pipeline for scRNA-seq. The field uses Euclidean/cosine/correlation distances on log-normalized counts (Seurat/Scanpy) or graph geodesics (PHATE, diffusion maps) that lack the Čencov invariance guarantee. Modeling clusters as exponential-family or Gaussian distributions and clustering under the Fisher-Rao distance would give a metric that is provably invariant to sufficient reparameterizations of the counts - a genuine theoretical selling point. 2) Multiscale structure is under-exploited. The Amari-Chentsov tensor (third-order, skewness/curvature) and the e/m dual-geodesic decomposition give a natural notion of scale and of interpolation paths between cell states, but no method uses them for hierarchical/multiscale clustering. 3) Fisher-Rao distances have only just become computable for Gaussians (Nielsen 2023/2024); nobody has yet paired that machinery with conformal prediction to produce calibrated, distribution-free cluster-membership confidence - the exact combination our paper proposes. 4) Rare-state sensitivity: monogenic-diabetes phenotypes (HNF1A-MODY islet cells) are subtle, low-count shifts; the Fisher-Rao/natural-gradient framework is specifically the geometry in which small distributional differences are measured in intrinsically comparable ('discriminability') units, so it should out-resolve Euclidean methods on exactly this regime. 5) SQFA (2025) proves the supervised Fisher-Rao-maximization objective works on real data but is linear, Gaussian, single-scale, unsupervised-incapable, and has no uncertainty quantification - each of which is a concrete axis our method can extend. 6) Curvature-aware statistics: existing scRNA-seq geometry work (Ricci curvature, path metrics) is topological but not information-geometric; unifying topological features with the Fisher-Rao metric and its curvature is an open, publishable direction.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt/adapt, with the actual equations: (A) Fisher-Rao metric as the base geometry: g_ij(theta)=E[d_i log p d_j log p]=-E[d_i d_j log p]. Model each cluster (or cell neighborhood) as (i) a categorical/multinomial over genes, giving the closed-form spherical geodesic d_R(p,q)=2*arccos(sum_i sqrt(p_i q_i)) via the sqrt-embedding p->2*sqrt(p); or (ii) a Gaussian N(mu,Sigma) in a PCA/latent space. (B) For Gaussian cluster models, compute Fisher-Rao distances with Nielsen's (2023) bounded approximation: discretize the connecting curve and sum segment lengths ~ sqrt(J(p_i,p_{i+1})) with Jeffreys J=KL(p||q)+KL(q||p), and use the Calvo-Oller SPD-cone embedding (Gaussian -> (d+1)x(d+1) SPD matrix) for guaranteed lower/upper bounds; fall back to affine-invariant SPD distance for covariance-only structure. (C) Use Amari-Nagaoka dually-flat structure for exponential-family clusters: Fisher metric = Hessian psi(theta); Bregman divergence B_psi = KL; use the generalized Pythagorean theorem and dual e-/m-projections to define information-geometric centroids and a principled hierarchical/multiscale merge criterion (right-triangle divergence decomposition D(P:R)=D(P:Q)+D(Q:R)). (D) Optimize any embedding/clustering objective with Amari's natural gradient G^{-1} grad L (K-FAC / diagonal-Fisher approximation for scalability) so training is reparameterization-invariant. (E) Borrow SQFA's (2025) objective directly: learn a projection W that maximizes summed pairwise Fisher-Rao (or Hellinger, per SQFA-H) distances between class/cluster-conditional Gaussians on the SPD manifold - use it both as our supervised embedding step (HNF1A vs control islet states) and as an ablation baseline. (F) Define a Fisher-Rao-norm-style invariant summary statistic (Liang et al. 2019), ||theta||_fr^2 = theta^T I(theta) theta, as a rescaling-invariant cluster-separation / confidence score. (G) Cite Čencov (1982) and Ay et al. (2015) as the formal invariance justification, and consider the Amari-Chentsov 3-tensor as a curvature/skewness feature feeding the topological, multiscale layer. Practical note: exploit that KL/Jeffreys, Bhattacharyya angle, and Fisher-Rao all coincide-to-leading-order locally (d_R^2 ~ 2*KL for nearby distributions), so KL/Bregman can serve as a cheap surrogate at fine scales and true Fisher-Rao at coarse scales - a built-in multiscale ladder.

result.subtopic: Clustering algorithms for scRNA-seq: graph/community-detection and consensus methods, 2021–2026 benchmarks, and the evaluation metrics (ARI/NMI/silhouette) that fail on rare populations

papers.citation: Traag, V. A., Waltman, L., & van Eck, N. J. (2019). From Louvain to Leiden: guaranteeing well-connected communities. Scientific Reports, 9, 5233. DOI:10.1038/s41598-019-41695-z.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces the Leiden algorithm, now the default community-detection clustering backend in Scanpy and Seurat. Adds a refinement phase to the Louvain local-move/aggregate loop and proves that returned communities are guaranteed connected and, under iteration, converge to a partition that is locally optimally assigned (gamma-separated and gamma-connected). Supports both modularity/Reichardt-Bornholdt (RBConfiguration) and the Constant Potts Model (CPM) quality functions, each with an explicit resolution parameter gamma.
papers.relevance: Leiden is the algorithm our method must beat or subsume.
