Its resolution parameter gamma is exactly the knob we would sweep to make clustering 'multiscale', and the CPM objective H = sum_c [e_c - gamma*C(n_c,2)] is resolution-limit-free, unlike modularity Q = (1/2m) sum_ij [A_ij - k_i k_j/(2m)] delta(c_i,c_j) which suffers the Fortunato-Barthelemy resolution limit and can merge small (rare) communities. Understanding CPM vs modularity is central to arguing that an information-geometric/multiscale objective resolves rare islet subpopulations Louvain/Leiden fuse away.
papers.quantitative_findings: On empirical networks Louvain produced up to 25% badly connected and up to 16% disconnected communities; Leiden reduces both to 0% by construction and is also faster (reported several-fold speedups on large graphs). Approx citation impact: >10,000 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: Very high; >10,000 citations, de facto standard clustering algorithm in single-cell pipelines.

papers.citation: Levine, J. H., Simonds, E. F., Bendall, S. C., Davis, K. L., Amir, E. D., Tadmor, M. D., ... Pe'er, D., & Nolan, G. P. (2015). Data-Driven Phenotypic Dissection of AML Reveals Progenitor-like Cells that Correlate with Prognosis. Cell, 162(1), 184-197. DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2015.05.047.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces PhenoGraph, the template for graph-based single-cell clustering: build a k-nearest-neighbor graph in expression space, reweight edges by the Jaccard overlap of shared neighbors (a shared-nearest-neighbor / SNN graph), then partition by Louvain modularity maximization. This kNN-graph -> Jaccard/SNN -> community-detection pipeline is the exact skeleton later adopted by Seurat's FindClusters and by Scanpy.
papers.relevance: Defines the graph-construction stage we would modify. The critical, rarely-questioned choice is the distance used for the kNN graph (Euclidean on PCA). Replacing that with an information-geometric (Fisher-Rao / Aitchison) distance on the negative-binomial count manifold is a natural differentiator of our method, and PhenoGraph is the canonical baseline to show the graph edges change.
papers.quantitative_findings: PhenoGraph resolved phenotypes in 30-dimensional mass-cytometry data across a pediatric AML cohort and isolated a progenitor-like signature predictive of survival in independent cohorts. Approx citation impact: ~2,000 (Semantic Scholar) to ~3,500 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: High; ~2,000-3,500 citations; originator of the kNN+Jaccard+Louvain single-cell clustering paradigm.

papers.citation: Kiselev, V. Y., Kirschner, K., Schaub, M. T., Andrews, T., Yiu, A., Chandra, T., ... & Hemberg, M. (2017). SC3: consensus clustering of single-cell RNA-seq data. Nature Methods, 14, 483-486. DOI:10.1038/nmeth.4236.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces SC3, the leading consensus/ensemble clustering method for scRNA-seq. Computes multiple cell-cell distance matrices (Euclidean, Pearson, Spearman), transforms each via PCA or the graph Laplacian, runs k-means over a range of retained dimensions d, then aggregates all solutions into a binary consensus matrix (cluster-based similarity partitioning, CSPA) that is hierarchically clustered. Trades runtime for accuracy and robustness.
papers.relevance: Demonstrates that stability across many base clusterings, not a single partition, is what makes cluster calls trustworthy. This is conceptually adjacent to the confidence guarantees we want to formalize with conformal prediction, and to multiscale aggregation. SC3 is a standard non-graph baseline in every benchmark, including Yin 2025.
papers.quantitative_findings: SC3 reported the highest median ARI across the reference scRNA-seq datasets available in 2017 and could recover subclones from neoplastic transcriptomes; its O(n^2)-O(n^3) consensus step limits it to roughly a few thousand cells without the hybrid SVM extension. Approx citation impact: ~1,100 (Semantic Scholar) to ~2,000 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: High; ~1,100-2,000 citations; canonical consensus-clustering baseline.

papers.citation: Kiselev, V. Y., Andrews, T. S., & Hemberg, M. (2019). Challenges in unsupervised clustering of single-cell RNA-seq data. Nature Reviews Genetics, 20, 273-282. DOI:10.1038/s41576-018-0088-9.
papers.key_contribution: Authoritative review that codifies the standard evaluation protocol and its failure modes. Names ARI (adjusted for chance) and NMI as the standard external metrics against ground-truth labels, silhouette width as the standard internal metric, and discusses the resolution/cluster-number selection problem, the curse of dimensionality, dropouts, and the difficulty of detecting rare cell types.
papers.relevance: This is the paper to cite for 'what is standard and what fails.' It explicitly frames rare-population detection and the arbitrariness of resolution/k selection as open problems, directly motivating our multiscale, confidence-calibrated design. Provides the vocabulary reviewers expect.
papers.quantitative_findings: Synthesizes dozens of methods and notes ARI/NMI are dominated by abundant clusters and that no method reliably recovers rare populations (populations below roughly 1-2% of cells are routinely missed). Approx citation impact: >1,000 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: High; >1,000 citations; standard reference for clustering evaluation practice and pitfalls.

papers.citation: Yin, Y.-H., Wang, F., Li, W., Liu, Q., Zhou, S., Zhou, M., Jiang, Z., Yu, D.-J., & Wang, G. (2025). Comparative benchmarking of single-cell clustering algorithms for transcriptomic and proteomic data. Genome Biology, 26, article 10.1186/s13059-025-03719-y.
papers.key_contribution: Systematic 2025 benchmark of 28 clustering algorithms (15 classical machine-learning, 6 community-detection, 7 deep-learning) on 10 paired transcriptomic+proteomic (CITE-seq) datasets plus 30 simulated datasets, scored on clustering accuracy, peak memory, and runtime, and analyzing the effect of highly variable gene (HVG) selection and cell-type granularity.
papers.relevance: This is the current benchmark our method will be judged against; we must position on its axes and datasets. It quantifies the accuracy/scalability Pareto front and, importantly, shows performance is modality- and granularity-dependent, exactly the fine-granularity/rare-type regime our method targets.
papers.quantitative_findings: Best overall accuracy: scAIDE, scDCC, and FlowSOM (FlowSOM also most robust). Memory-efficient: scDCC, scDeepCluster. Fastest: TSCAN, SHARP, MarkovHC. Community-detection methods (Louvain/Leiden family) gave the best balanced accuracy-vs-cost trade-off. >300,000 cells, >50 cell types, 5 tissues. Newly published (2025), citations still accruing.
papers.approx_impact: Emerging; the flagship 2025 clustering benchmark, low citation count so far but high field visibility (Genome Biology).

papers.citation: Luecken, M. D., Buttner, M., Chaichoompu, K., Danese, A., Interlandi, M., Mueller, M. F., ... & Theis, F. J. (2022). Benchmarking atlas-level data integration in single-cell genomics. Nature Methods, 19, 41-50. DOI:10.1038/s41592-021-01336-8.
papers.key_contribution: Defines the scIB metric framework, now the community standard for evaluating single-cell embeddings/clusterings. Fourteen metrics split into bio-conservation (NMI, ARI, cell-type ASW, isolated-label F1, isolated-label ASW, cLISI, cell-cycle and HVG conservation, trajectory conservation) and batch-removal (kBET, iLISI/graph-iLISI, graph connectivity, PCR, batch ASW), combined as an overall score = 0.6*bio + 0.4*batch.
papers.relevance: Supplies the exact metric battery and the isolated-label (rare-population) metrics we should report to be credible. Its explicit isolated-label F1/ASW metrics are the closest existing attempt to score rare-type recovery, giving us a principled, reviewer-accepted evaluation harness and a target to improve on.
papers.quantitative_findings: Evaluated 68 method/preprocessing combinations on 85 batches from 23 studies, >1.2 million cells, 13 integration tasks. Found no universal winner; method ranking depended on task and metric, and preprocessing (HVG count, scaling) materially changed rankings. Approx citation impact: >1,500 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: Very high; >1,500 citations; the standard scIB benchmarking/metrics package.

papers.citation: Lopez-De-Castro, M., Garcia-Galindo, A., Gonzalez-Gomariz, J., & Armananzas, R. (2025). Conformal inference for reliable single cell RNA-seq annotation. Bioinformatics, 41(10), btaf521. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btaf521.
papers.key_contribution: First application of split conformal prediction to single-cell annotation: returns, for each query cell, a prediction set of plausible cell-type labels with a distribution-free marginal coverage guarantee P(Y in C(X)) >= 1 - alpha under exchangeability, and uses set size as a calibrated uncertainty measure plus an anomaly/OOD detector for previously unseen cell types.
papers.relevance: Directly instantiates the 'conformal confidence' component of our paper on the annotation side; we extend it from supervised annotation to unsupervised/multiscale cluster assignment, and to class-conditional (Mondrian) conformal so that rare islet populations (e.g., HNF1A-perturbed beta cells) get their own coverage guarantee rather than being swamped by marginal coverage.
papers.quantitative_findings: Reported well-calibrated prediction sets maintaining empirical coverage at the nominal 1-alpha level across significance levels and effective OOD identification of held-out cell types. Newly published (2025), citations still accruing.
papers.approx_impact: Emerging; niche but directly on-theme; the reference point for conformal methods in scRNA-seq.

papers.citation: Rizvi, A. H., Camara, P. G., Kandror, E. K., Roberts, T. J., Schieren, I., Maniatis, T., & Rabadan, R. (2017). Single-cell topological RNA-seq analysis reveals insights into cellular differentiation and development. Nature Biotechnology, 35, 551-560. DOI:10.1038/nbt.3854.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces scTDA, a topological-data-analysis framework for scRNA-seq built on the Mapper algorithm: uses a filter function and overlapping cover to build a simplicial/graph representation of the data's shape, capturing continuous and circular trajectories and transient states without imposing a fixed number of clusters.
papers.relevance: The foundational reference for the 'topologically-aware' component. Mapper covers and persistent homology give a principled, resolution-parameterized (cover overlap/gain) multiscale summary that preserves continuum structure Leiden shatters, and topological features (small loops/flares) are a natural way to flag rare transient populations that hard partitioning misses.
papers.quantitative_findings: Applied to mouse embryonic stem cell differentiation into motor neurons; Mapper recovered the differentiation topology and transient states model-free/unsupervised where partition-based clustering artificially fragments the trajectory. Approx citation impact: ~400-500 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: Moderate; ~400-500 citations; the anchor paper for topology-based single-cell analysis.

result.synthesis:
The scRNA-seq clustering field has consolidated around a single dominant paradigm: embed cells (PCA on log-normalized, HVG-selected counts), build a k-nearest-neighbor graph, reweight edges by shared-neighbor/Jaccard overlap (the SNN graph), and partition by community detection. Levine et al. (2015, PhenoGraph) established this skeleton; Blondel et al. (2008, Louvain, ~60k citations) supplied the modularity-maximization engine Q = (1/2m) sum_ij [A_ij - k_i k_j/(2m)] delta(c_i,c_j); and Traag et al. (2019, Leiden) fixed Louvain's disconnected-community pathology and became the default in Scanpy and Seurat. Leiden also exposes the two levers our method exploits: a resolution parameter gamma and a choice between modularity and the resolution-limit-free Constant Potts Model. The main competing family is consensus/ensemble clustering, exemplified by Kiselev et al. (2017, SC3), which aggregates many k-means solutions into a consensus matrix; deep-embedding methods (scDeepCluster, scDCC, scAIDE) are the newest entrants. Kiselev, Andrews & Hemberg (2019, Nature Reviews Genetics) is the canonical statement of the evaluation protocol: external agreement with labels via the chance-adjusted Rand index ARI = (RI - E[RI])/(max RI - E[RI]) and normalized mutual information NMI(U,V) = I(U;V)/mean(H(U),H(V)), and internal cohesion via the silhouette s(i) = (b(i)-a(i))/max(a(i),b(i)).

The 2021-2026 literature is dominated by benchmarking rather than new algorithms. Yin et al. (2025, Genome Biology) is the flagship, comparing 28 algorithms across classical, community-detection, and deep-learning families on >300k cells; it finds no universal winner (scAIDE/scDCC/FlowSOM lead on accuracy, community-detection methods win the accuracy-vs-cost trade-off) and shows results depend on HVG selection and cell-type granularity. Luecken et al. (2022, scIB) supplies the community-standard 14-metric battery, combined as 0.6*bio + 0.4*batch, and crucially includes isolated-label F1/ASW metrics that begin to address rare populations. A pointed 2025 Nature Biotechnology analysis ("Shortcomings of silhouette in single-cell integration benchmarking") shows silhouette's convex-cluster/Euclidean assumptions are systematically violated in single-cell data, undermining a metric still in wide use.

The consistent, well-documented failure mode is rare-population recovery. ARI and NMI are pair-counting and information-theoretic aggregates dominated by abundant clusters: a rare population of a few dozen cells contributes O(k^2) pairs out of O(n^2), so missing it costs almost nothing in ARI, and resolution-limited modularity actively merges small communities into larger neighbors. Silhouette assumes convex, roughly equal-variance clusters that rare/transient states violate. Consequently, standard benchmarks can rank a method highly while it systematically erases exactly the small, clinically important subpopulation (e.g., an HNF1A-perturbed beta-cell state in MODY) that motivates the analysis. Two orthogonal threads point at fixes: topological methods (Rizvi et al. 2017, scTDA/Mapper) preserve continuum and transient structure that hard partitioning shatters, and conformal prediction (Lopez-De-Castro et al. 2025) attaches distribution-free coverage guarantees to per-cell annotations. No existing method unifies multiscale community detection, a geometry-aware distance, topological structure, and calibrated per-cell confidence, which is precisely the whitespace our paper occupies.
result.gaps_opportunities:
1) No calibrated confidence on cluster assignments. Every mainstream method (Leiden, PhenoGraph, SC3, deep embedders) emits hard labels with no statistical guarantee; SC3's consensus and conformal annotation (Lopez-De-Castro 2025) are the closest, but conformal has never been fused with unsupervised multiscale clustering. Opportunity: split/Mondrian (class-conditional) conformal giving each cell a prediction set with per-cell-type coverage 1-alpha, so rare islet states get their own guarantee instead of being hidden under marginal coverage.
2) Metrics fail on rare populations. ARI/NMI are abundance-weighted and silhouette's assumptions are violated (Nature Biotech 2025); benchmarks (Yin 2025, Luecken 2022) rank on aggregates. Opportunity: report macro-averaged and per-rare-cluster F1, isolated-label F1/ASW, and a rare-type sensitivity curve, and design an objective whose loss is not abundance-dominated.
3) The distance/geometry is unquestioned. Nearly all pipelines use Euclidean distance on PCA of log-normalized counts, ignoring that scRNA-seq counts are (zero-inflated) negative-binomial and, after normalization, compositional. Opportunity: an information-geometric (Fisher-Rao / Aitchison) distance on the count/simplex manifold that changes which cells are neighbors, directly attacking the graph-construction stage PhenoGraph/Seurat inherit.
4) Resolution/scale selection is heuristic. gamma (Leiden) and k are chosen ad hoc, and the modularity resolution limit fuses small communities. Opportunity: a principled multiscale scheme (Markov-stability-style scanning of gamma with CPM, MarkovHC-style hierarchy) that selects stable scales and exposes rare clusters that appear only at fine resolution.
5) Partitioning shatters continua and misses transient states. Mapper/scTDA (Rizvi 2017) preserves topology but is not integrated with confidence or scalable community detection. Opportunity: a topologically-aware graph/cover (persistent-homology-informed) so trajectories are not fragmented and small loops/flares flag rare transient populations.
6) Disease stress-tests are underused. Benchmarks use healthy atlases; a monogenic-diabetes (HNF1A MODY) islet stress-test where the target subpopulation is small and clinically defined is a differentiated, reviewer-compelling evaluation of rare-population sensitivity with calibrated confidence.
result.methods_we_should_use:
Adopt and adapt, with explicit equations:
1) Community-detection core with a resolution-limit-free objective. Use Leiden (Traag 2019) with the Constant Potts Model H = sum_c [e_c - gamma * C(n_c,2)] rather than modularity Q = (1/2m) sum_ij [A_ij - k_i k_j/(2m)] delta(c_i,c_j), because CPM avoids the resolution limit that merges rare clusters. Keep Leiden's connectivity guarantee.
2) Multiscale via Markov stability. Scan gamma (equivalently Markov time t) and select scales where partitions are stable (variation-of-information plateaus / MarkovHC-style hierarchy); report the whole hierarchy, not one resolution. This operationalizes 'multiscale.'
3) Information-geometric distance for graph construction. Model counts as negative-binomial/ZINB and use the Fisher-Rao metric g_ij(theta) = E[d_i log p * d_j log p]; for count/multinomial models the Fisher-Rao geodesic reduces to the Hellinger/arc-length distance d(p,q) = arccos(sum sqrt(p_k q_k)) (the sqrt-transform onto the unit sphere). Alternatively use Aitchison geometry (centered-log-ratio transform) for the compositional nature of normalized counts. Build the kNN/SNN graph under this metric instead of Euclidean-on-PCA.
4) Topologically-aware layer. Overlay a Mapper cover (Rizvi 2017) or persistent-homology filtration on the same graph so continuous trajectories are preserved and small persistent features (loops/flares) surface rare transient states; use cover overlap/gain as an additional scale parameter tied to (2).
5) Conformal confidence. Wrap the pipeline in split conformal with a nonconformity score s(x,y) (e.g., 1 - soft cluster/annotation probability); prediction set C_alpha(x) = { y : s(x,y) <= q_hat }, where q_hat is the ceil((n+1)(1-alpha))/n empirical quantile of calibration scores, giving marginal coverage P(Y in C(X)) >= 1-alpha. Use Mondrian/class-conditional conformal (separate q_hat per cluster) so rare populations get their own coverage guarantee; report set size as per-cell uncertainty and set-based OOD flags (Lopez-De-Castro 2025).
6) Evaluation battery. Score with ARI = [sum_ij C(n_ij,2) - (sum_i C(a_i,2) sum_j C(b_j,2))/C(n,2)] / [ (1/2)(sum_i C(a_i,2)+sum_j C(b_j,2)) - (sum_i C(a_i,2) sum_j C(b_j,2))/C(n,2) ], AMI/NMI, and the scIB isolated-label F1/ASW, cLISI, and graph connectivity (Luecken 2022); add macro-F1 and per-rare-cluster F1 and a rare-type detection-rate curve; de-emphasize or avoid raw silhouette given its documented assumption violations (Nature Biotech 2025), or replace it with graph-based cLISI/graph connectivity.

result.subtopic: Riemannian geometry of deep generative models and pullback metrics: decoder-Jacobian geometry, the stochastic/expected metric of a VAE, geodesics and magnification factors in latent space, and Fisher-Rao (information-geometric) pullback for non-Gaussian decoders (2014-2026)
result.synthesis:
The field rests on one deceptively simple idea: a deep generative model's decoder g: Z(R^d) -> X(R^D) immerses a low-dimensional latent space into data space, so the latent coordinates are curvilinear and Euclidean distances/interpolations in Z are meaningless. The fix is to endow Z with the pullback of the ambient (Euclidean) metric through the decoder, M(z) = J_g(z)^T J_g(z), where J_g = dg/dz is the decoder Jacobian. Curve length is L(gamma)=int sqrt(gamma' ^T M(gamma) gamma') dt, geodesics minimize the energy int gamma'^T M gamma' dt (via the geodesic ODE with Christoffel symbols or discretized energy minimization / graph shortest-paths), and the local volume element / magnification factor is MF(z)=sqrt(det M(z)). This lineage is old: Bishop-Svensen-Williams introduced the magnification factor for GTM (1997-98), and Tosi et al. (2014) first defined a probabilistic (expected) metric E[J^T J] under a GP-LVM posterior. Arvanitidis, Hansen & Hauberg's Latent Space Oddity (ICLR 2018, ~330 cites) is the field-defining paper for deep models: it derives the *stochastic* Riemannian metric of a VAE whose decoder has mean mu(z) and variance sigma^2(z), with expected metric E[M(z)] = J_mu^T J_mu + J_sigma^T J_sigma, and shows that a well-calibrated (extrapolating) variance network makes the metric blow up off the data support, so geodesics are forced to stay on the manifold. Parallel to it, Shao-Kumar-Fletcher (2018) and Chen et al. (2018) built the practical machinery: geodesic solvers, parallel transport, curvature estimation, and graph-based approximations, with the empirical finding that learned image manifolds are surprisingly close to flat.

The second wave generalizes what is pulled back and fixes what is broken. Geometrically Enriched Latent Spaces (Arvanitidis, Hauberg & Scholkopf, AISTATS 2021) treats the *ambient* space as a Riemannian manifold with metric A(x) (e.g. large in low-density regions) and pulls back M(z)=J_g^T A(g(z)) J_g, letting domain knowledge/density steer geodesics. Pulling Back Information Geometry (Arvanitidis, Gonzalez-Duque, Pouplin, Kalatzis & Hauberg, AISTATS 2022) is the most directly relevant to an information-geometric method: it replaces the Euclidean ambient metric with the Fisher-Rao metric of the decoder's output *distribution family*, giving M(z)=J_f(z)^T I(f(z)) J_f(z) where f(z) are the decoder's distribution parameters and I is the Fisher information matrix. This makes geometry well-defined for Bernoulli, categorical, Poisson, and negative-binomial decoders (recovering Latent Space Oddity for the Gaussian case) which is exactly the regime scRNA-seq lives in (over-dispersed counts, ZINB/NB decoders as in scVI). Kalatzis et al. (ICML 2020) close the loop on the prior by replacing N(0,I) with a Riemannian Brownian-motion prior whose transition density respects the pullback geometry.

The current frontier is identifiability and uncertainty of the *learned* geometry. Hauberg's Only Bayes Should Learn a Manifold (2018) and Syrota-Moreno-Munoz-Hauberg's Decoder Ensembling (ICML GRaM 2024) show the pullback metric of a single decoder is essentially arbitrary: the latent space can be smoothly reparameterized without changing the model density, so a point estimate of M(z) is not identifiable. The remedy is Bayesian marginalization / decoder ensembles, using the *expected* metric bar-M(z)=(1/K) sum_k J_{g_k}^T J_{g_k}, which yields stable, reproducible geodesics. Recent 2023-2025 work (Park et al., NeurIPS 2023; Lobashev et al. Hessian Geometry, ICML 2025) is porting the same pullback/Fisher-information machinery to diffusion models and revealing rich (even fractal, phase-transition) latent geometry, confirming that decoder-Jacobian geometry is now a mainstream lens on generative models.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) No information-geometric pullback tailored to single-cell counts. The Fisher-Rao pullback (Arvanitidis 2022) exists in principle but has never been instantiated for NB/ZINB scRNA-seq decoders (scVI-style) at scale; for a NB decoder with mean mu and dispersion theta the Fisher information is closed-form, so M(z)=J_f^T I_NB(f) J_f is directly computable and biologically correct (variance-mean over-dispersion is baked into the metric, not assumed away by a Gaussian). This is a clean, novel contribution. 2) Identifiability + statistical guarantees are unsolved together. Ensembles restore reproducibility of the geometry but nobody has coupled the expected/Bayesian pullback metric to *conformal* guarantees on geodesic distances or cluster assignments; our conformal-confidence angle can be the first to give distribution-free coverage for metric-based cluster membership, using the metric-induced local volume MF(z)=sqrt(det M(z)) or off-manifold variance blow-up as a nonconformity score. 3) No genuinely multiscale metric. All prior work computes a single metric tensor; there is no principled family M_t(z) indexed by a diffusion/heat-kernel scale t that would let clustering resolve coarse cell types and fine subpopulations (e.g. HNF1A+ vs healthy beta cells) in one framework. 4) No topological awareness. Pullback geometry captures curvature and geodesic distance but not homology; combining Fisher-Rao geodesic distances with persistent homology / TDA on the learned manifold is open and is the natural home for a topologically-aware clustering claim. 5) Scalability. BVP geodesic solvers and dense graph shortest-paths are O(N^2)-ish and do not scale to 10^5-10^6 cells; a scalable geodesic/metric-graph approximation is needed and publishable. 6) Rare-cell / disease stress test. Existing evaluations are on images (MNIST/faces); a rigorous benchmark on a monogenic-diabetes islet dataset with a known perturbed subpopulation (HNF1A MODY) is missing and gives biological validation Nature/NeurIPS reviewers want.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt/adapt these concrete objects. (a) Base pullback metric: M(z)=J_g(z)^T J_g(z) with J_g the decoder Jacobian, computed by reverse-mode autodiff; magnification factor MF(z)=sqrt(det M(z)) as a multiscale volume/density descriptor (Latent Space Oddity; Bishop GTM). (b) Information-geometric upgrade (our core): Fisher-Rao pullback M(z)=J_f(z)^T I(f(z)) J_f(z), with f(z) the decoder distribution parameters and I the Fisher information; instantiate I for a negative-binomial/ZINB decoder so the metric is the correct information geometry for scRNA-seq counts (Arvanitidis et al. 2022). This is what makes the paper legitimately 'information-geometric.' (c) Stochastic/variance-aware metric: E[M]=J_mu^T J_mu + J_sigma^T J_sigma with an extrapolating variance network (RBF/precision head) so the metric diverges off the data support and geodesics hug the manifold; reuse the off-manifold variance as a natural nonconformity score for conformal sets (Latent Space Oddity). (d) Identifiable geometry via decoder ensembles / Bayesian marginalization: expected metric bar-M(z)=(1/K) sum_k J_{g_k}^T J_{g_k} (or GP-posterior expectation E[J^T J] as in Tosi 2014) to defeat the reparameterization non-identifiability; essential for reproducible clustering and honest confidence (Syrota et al. 2024; Hauberg 2018). (e) Geodesic computation: minimize discrete curve energy E(gamma)=sum_i (Delta gamma_i)^T M(z_i) (Delta gamma_i) or solve the geodesic ODE gamma'' = -Gamma(gamma)[gamma',gamma'] with Christoffel symbols from M; for scale, use a k-NN metric graph with edge weights = local Riemannian length and Dijkstra/Floyd shortest paths as the geodesic-distance matrix feeding clustering (Chen et al. 2018; Shao et al. 2018). (f) Geometry-respecting prior/sampling: Riemannian Brownian-motion prior (single scale sigma) instead of N(0,I) to avoid latent 'holes' and to define the multiscale family via the heat kernel exp(-t Delta_M) (Kalatzis et al. 2020) - this doubles as our diffusion-scale index t for multiscale clustering. (g) Ambient re-weighting: geometrically-enriched pullback M=J_g^T A(g(z)) J_g with A large in low-density ambient regions to push cluster boundaries into sparse regions (Arvanitidis et al. 2021). Pipeline: train NB/ZINB VAE ensemble -> expected Fisher-Rao pullback metric -> heat-kernel multiscale geodesic distances -> topological/persistent-homology-aware clustering -> conformal calibration of cluster membership, stress-tested on HNF1A MODY islets.

papers.citation: Arvanitidis, G., Hansen, L. K., & Hauberg, S. (2018). Latent Space Oddity: on the Curvature of Deep Generative Models. In International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2018). arXiv:1710.11379.
papers.key_contribution: Field-defining derivation of the stochastic Riemannian (pullback) metric of a VAE: for a stochastic decoder with mean mu(z) and std sigma(z), the expected metric is E[M(z)] = J_mu^T J_mu + J_sigma^T J_sigma. Shows an extrapolating variance network makes the metric diverge off-manifold so geodesics stay on the data support; introduces improved distances, interpolants, clustering and sampling in latent space.
papers.relevance: This is the technical backbone of our metric. The mean+variance expected metric and the off-manifold variance blow-up give us both the geodesic distances for clustering and a ready-made nonconformity signal for conformal confidence. Magnification factor sqrt(det M(z)) is our multiscale density descriptor.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~330 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Demonstrates that standard VAE variance estimates are poor and that a proposed inverse-RBF/precision variance network fixes them, yielding qualitatively better geodesics and latent clustering on MNIST and related data.
papers.approx_impact: ~330 citations; the most-cited paper of this subfield and its canonical reference.

papers.citation: Arvanitidis, G., Gonzalez-Duque, M., Pouplin, A., Kalatzis, D., & Hauberg, S. (2022). Pulling back information geometry. In Proceedings of the 25th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2022), PMLR 151:4872-4894. arXiv:2106.05367.
papers.key_contribution: Generalizes the pullback beyond Gaussian decoders by pulling back the Fisher-Rao metric of the decoder's output distribution family: M(z) = J_f(z)^T I(f(z)) J_f(z), with f(z) the decoder parameters and I the Fisher information matrix. Enables meaningful 'black-box' latent geometries for arbitrary (Bernoulli, categorical, count) likelihoods; recovers Latent Space Oddity for Gaussians.
papers.relevance: This is the paper that makes our method legitimately information-geometric and directly applicable to scRNA-seq: instantiating I for a negative-binomial/ZINB (scVI-style) count decoder yields the correct Fisher-Rao metric for over-dispersed single-cell counts - a novel, unclaimed instantiation.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~28 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Shows well-behaved geodesics and interpolations for non-Gaussian decoders where prior Gaussian-only theory fails.
papers.approx_impact: ~28 citations; the key enabling result for non-Gaussian (count) decoder geometry.

papers.citation: Shao, H., Kumar, A., & Fletcher, P. T. (2018). The Riemannian Geometry of Deep Generative Models. In IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW 2018), pp. 315-323. arXiv:1711.08014.
papers.key_contribution: Provides efficient algorithms for the core Riemannian operations on decoder-induced manifolds: computing geodesic curves (intrinsic distance), parallel transport of tangent vectors, and curvature estimation via the decoder Jacobian. Empirically finds learned image manifolds are close to zero curvature, so latent straight lines approximate geodesics.
papers.relevance: Supplies the numerical geodesic and parallel-transport machinery we need to turn the metric into distances for clustering, and the curvature diagnostics to test whether our islet latent manifold is flat or strongly curved around the MODY subpopulation.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~207 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Demonstrates geodesics and parallel transport on VAE/GAN image manifolds with near-zero measured curvature.
papers.approx_impact: ~207 citations; a primary algorithmic reference for geodesic/parallel-transport computation.

papers.citation: Chen, N., Klushyn, A., Kurle, R., Jiang, X., Bayer, J., & van der Smagt, P. (2018). Metrics for Deep Generative Models. In Proceedings of the 21st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2018), PMLR 84:1540-1550. arXiv:1711.01204.
papers.key_contribution: Defines Riemannian distance in deep generative models as the shortest path under the decoder-induced pullback metric and gives practical approximations, including graph-based (discretized) geodesic computation and a principled Riemannian distance for interpolation and visual inspection.
papers.relevance: Its graph/energy-minimization geodesic approximation is the scalable route we need for 10^5-10^6 cells: build a k-NN metric graph with Riemannian edge lengths and run shortest paths to get the geodesic distance matrix that feeds multiscale/topological clustering.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~124 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Shows Riemannian interpolation outperforms linear latent interpolation and yields interpretable geodesics.
papers.approx_impact: ~124 citations; standard reference for practical geodesic distance computation.

papers.citation: Tosi, A., Hauberg, S., Vellido, A., & Lawrence, N. D. (2014). Metrics for Probabilistic Geometries. In Proceedings of the 30th Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2014), pp. 800-808. arXiv:1411.7432.
papers.key_contribution: First rigorous probabilistic pullback metric: treats the GP-LVM as a random field and defines the *expected* metric tensor E[J^T J] under the GP posterior over mappings, with algorithms to compute expected metrics and geodesics. Foundational bridge from Bishop's GTM magnification factor to modern stochastic pullback geometry.
papers.relevance: The expectation-over-mappings idea is exactly what we need for identifiability: our decoder-ensemble expected metric is the deep-learning analogue of Tosi's GP-posterior expected metric, giving reproducible geodesic distances for stable clustering and honest confidence.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~67 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Establishes computable expected metric tensors and geodesic interpolation for probabilistic latent-variable models.
papers.approx_impact: ~67 citations; the foundational pre-deep-learning reference for probabilistic/expected pullback metrics.

papers.citation: Arvanitidis, G., Hauberg, S., & Scholkopf, B. (2021). Geometrically Enriched Latent Spaces. In Proceedings of the 24th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS 2021), PMLR 130:631-639.
papers.key_contribution: Treats the ambient data space as itself a Riemannian manifold with metric A(x) (encoding domain knowledge or density), and pulls it back through the decoder: M(z) = J_g(z)^T A(g(z)) J_g(z). Geodesics then follow the learned manifold *and* respect ambient structure (e.g. avoiding low-density regions).
papers.relevance: Gives us a principled hook to inject biological priors and density into the metric: choosing A large in low-density/low-confidence ambient regions pushes cluster boundaries into sparse regions and can bias geodesics away from doublets/ambient-RNA artifacts, sharpening rare-cell (MODY) separation.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~68 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Improves interpretability and functionality of representations for both stochastic and deterministic generators.
papers.approx_impact: ~68 citations; the reference for domain-knowledge/ambient-metric enrichment of pullback geometry.

papers.citation: Kalatzis, D., Eklund, D., Arvanitidis, G., & Hauberg, S. (2020). Variational Autoencoders with Riemannian Brownian Motion Priors. In Proceedings of the 37th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2020), PMLR 119:5020-5034. arXiv:2002.05227.
papers.key_contribution: Replaces the N(0,I) latent prior with a Riemannian Brownian-motion prior whose transition (heat-kernel) density respects the pullback metric, adding model capacity with a single extra scalar (the diffusion time/scale) and eliminating latent 'holes'/aggregated-posterior mismatch.
papers.relevance: The heat-kernel/Brownian construction gives us the diffusion-scale index t for a genuinely *multiscale* family of metrics and geodesic distances - the missing ingredient for resolving coarse cell types and fine MODY subpopulations within one model.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~59 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Shows the geometry-aware prior increases capacity and improves latent density modeling over the standard Gaussian prior.
papers.approx_impact: ~59 citations; principal reference for geometry-respecting priors and heat-kernel scale.

papers.citation: Syrota, S., Moreno-Munoz, P., & Hauberg, S. (2024). Decoder ensembling for learned latent geometries. ICML 2024 Workshop on Geometry-grounded Representation Learning and Generative Modeling (GRaM), PMLR 251. arXiv:2408.07507.
papers.key_contribution: Directly attacks the non-identifiability of learned geometry: because a latent space can be smoothly reparameterized without changing the model density, a single decoder's pullback metric is essentially arbitrary. Proposes ensembles of decoders and computes geodesics on the *expected* manifold bar-M(z) = (1/K) sum_k J_{g_k}^T J_{g_k}, giving reliable, reproducible latent geometry.
papers.relevance: This is the linchpin for our reproducibility and confidence claims: only an expected/Bayesian metric yields stable geodesic distances and cluster assignments across retrainings - a prerequisite for meaningful conformal coverage guarantees on cluster membership.
papers.quantitative_findings: Recent workshop paper (2024); shows single-decoder geodesics are unstable/arbitrary while ensemble expected-metric geodesics are consistent and reliable across models.
papers.approx_impact: Emerging/frontier work; the current reference for identifiable, ensemble-based pullback geometry (builds on Hauberg 2018, 'Only Bayes Should Learn a Manifold').

result.subtopic: Diffusion maps, random walks, and graph construction in single-cell RNA-seq: from Coifman–Lafon diffusion geometry and random-walk timescales to DPT, PAGA, UMAP/kNN graphs, and multiscale/information-geometric embeddings

papers.citation: Coifman, R.R. & Lafon, S. (2006). Diffusion Maps. Applied and Computational Harmonic Analysis, 21(1), 5–30. DOI:10.1016/j.acha.2006.04.006.
papers.key_contribution: The foundational paper of diffusion geometry. Defines the family of anisotropic Markov diffusion operators on data and the diffusion-distance embedding. From a symmetric affinity kernel k(x,y)=exp(-||x-y||^2/epsilon), it introduces the density-normalization parameter alpha: k_alpha(x,y)=k(x,y)/(q(x)^alpha q(y)^alpha) with q(x)=sum_y k(x,y), then row-normalizes to a random-walk transition matrix P(x,y)=k_alpha(x,y)/d_alpha(x). alpha=1 yields the Laplace-Beltrami operator (geometry decoupled from sampling density), alpha=1/2 the Fokker-Planck operator, alpha=0 the normalized graph Laplacian. The diffusion distance D_t^2(x,y)=sum_{i>=1} lambda_i^{2t}(psi_i(x)-psi_i(y))^2 is realized exactly by the map Psi_t(x)=(lambda_1^t psi_1(x), lambda_2^t psi_2(x), ...), where (lambda_i, psi_i) are eigenpairs of P and t is the number of random-walk steps (diffusion time).
papers.relevance: This is the mathematical bedrock of essentially every method in our paper. Our information-geometric metric will be built on the powered diffusion operator P^t; the alpha=1 normalization is exactly what we need to prevent islet cell-type abundance imbalance (abundant beta/alpha cells vs. rare HNF1A-perturbed transitional states) from distorting geometry. The eigenvalue-weighted diffusion distance is the direct precursor to the multiscale and information-geometric distances we generalize.
papers.quantitative_findings: Published ACHA vol. 21, pp. 5-30. Among the most-cited manifold-learning papers; ~10,000+ Google Scholar citations. Companion PNAS papers by Coifman et al. (2005), 'Geometric diffusions... Diffusion maps' (PNAS 102(21):7426-7431) and '...Multiscale methods' (102(21):7432-7437), formalize the multiscale diffusion-semigroup viewpoint (~3,000+ citations combined).
papers.approx_impact: ~10,000+ Google Scholar citations (field-defining classic)

papers.citation: Nadler, B., Lafon, S., Coifman, R.R. & Kevrekidis, I.G. (2006). Diffusion maps, spectral clustering and reaction coordinates of dynamical systems. Applied and Computational Harmonic Analysis, 21(1), 113–127. (arXiv:math/0503445; companion NIPS 2005 'Diffusion maps, spectral clustering and eigenfunctions of Fokker-Planck operators', arXiv:math/0506090).
papers.key_contribution: Provides the rigorous theory of how random-walk timescales encode cluster/metastable structure — precisely the 'how random-walk timescales relate to cell-state structure' question. Shows that in the continuum limit the random-walk generator converges to a backward Fokker-Planck operator whose eigenfunctions are the slow reaction coordinates. Eigenvalues of P relate to relaxation timescales via lambda_i = exp(-t/tau_i), i.e. tau_i = -1/ln(lambda_i). A spectral gap after the k-th eigenvalue implies k metastable states (basins/clusters) separated by slow transitions; the leading nontrivial eigenvectors are the dynamically meaningful coordinates that survive as t grows. This turns 'choose diffusion time t' into a principled statement about which cell-state structure is resolved at which timescale.
papers.relevance: Directly justifies using spectral gaps / timescales tau_i to choose the number of clusters and to identify metastable cell states (e.g., distinguishing a genuine HNF1A intermediate/transitional beta-cell basin from noise). It is the theoretical hinge between our 'multiscale' random-walk timescales and topologically meaningful cell-state partitions, and grounds the eigenvalue-gap model-selection our conformal layer will calibrate.
papers.quantitative_findings: ACHA vol. 21, pp. 113-127. ~1,500–1,800 Google Scholar citations across the ACHA and companion NIPS versions. Establishes lambda_i = exp(-t/tau_i) timescale interpretation and metastability-from-spectral-gap result.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,500+ citations (core random-walk/spectral-clustering theory)

papers.citation: Haghverdi, L., Buettner, F. & Theis, F.J. (2015). Diffusion maps for high-dimensional single-cell analysis of differentiation data. Bioinformatics, 31(18), 2989–2998. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btv325.
papers.key_contribution: First adaptation of Coifman-Lafon diffusion maps to scRNA-seq. Introduces single-cell-specific kernel choices: a locally adaptive Gaussian kernel width (per-cell bandwidth sigma set from neighbor distances) to cope with strongly varying density along differentiation trajectories, and an explicit treatment of measurement uncertainty / missing (dropout) values in the affinity computation. Demonstrates that diffusion components, unlike PCA/t-SNE, preserve continuous branching lineage geometry and are robust to sampling density and noise. Implemented in the R package destiny.
papers.relevance: Establishes the recipe for building the affinity graph on noisy, dropout-heavy islet scRNA-seq: variable-bandwidth kernels and uncertainty-aware affinities are exactly the ingredients we need so that low-count HNF1A-perturbed cells are not artificially isolated. Sets the precedent that diffusion geometry (not Euclidean PCA) is the right substrate for continuum cell-state structure.
papers.quantitative_findings: Bioinformatics 31(18):2989-2998. ~1,100 Google Scholar citations (Semantic Scholar/scispace list ~690+). Foundational for the destiny package.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,100 citations (brought diffusion maps into single-cell)

papers.citation: Haghverdi, L., Büttner, M., Wolf, F.A., Buettner, F. & Theis, F.J. (2016). Diffusion pseudotime robustly reconstructs lineage branching. Nature Methods, 13(10), 845–848. DOI:10.1038/nmeth.3971.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces Diffusion Pseudotime (DPT), whose central object is a scale-integrated random-walk operator. Rather than fixing one diffusion time t, DPT sums the transition matrix over ALL timescales: using the accumulated transition matrix M = sum_{t=1}^infty (T^t - trivial component) = (I - T)^{-1} minus the stationary/rank-1 part, giving DPT^2(x,y) = sum_{i>=1} (lambda_i/(1-lambda_i))^2 (psi_i(x)-psi_i(y))^2. This is a scale-free (parameter-t-free) diffusion distance. DPT robustly orders cells, detects branch points via anti-correlated distance orderings from opposite trajectory tips, and identifies transient/metastable states and endpoints.
papers.relevance: DPT's move from a single diffusion time to an integrated-over-all-timescales operator is the conceptual seed of our 'multiscale' contribution — but DPT applies it only to 1-D pseudotime ordering, not to clustering with confidence. We can lift the (lambda_i/(1-lambda_i)) timescale reweighting into a clustering metric and attach conformal coverage to branch/endpoint assignments (critical for calling HNF1A-associated fate decisions).
papers.quantitative_findings: Nature Methods 13(10):845-848. ~1,900 Google Scholar citations (Semantic Scholar 1,069: 173 methods-, 301 results-citations). Key equation: accumulated-transition-matrix distance with weights lambda_i/(1-lambda_i).
papers.approx_impact: ~1,900 citations (standard pseudotime method; scale-free diffusion distance)

papers.citation: Wolf, F.A., Hamey, F.K., Plass, M., Solana, J., Dahlin, J.S., Göttgens, B. & Theis, F.J. (2019). PAGA: graph abstraction reconciles clustering with trajectory inference through a topology preserving map of single cells. Genome Biology, 20, 59. DOI:10.1186/s13059-019-1663-x.
papers.key_contribution: Partition-based Graph Abstraction. After partitioning the kNN graph (Louvain/Leiden), PAGA builds a coarse-grained graph whose nodes are partitions and whose edge weights are a statistical connectivity measure: the ratio of the number of observed inter-partition edges to the number expected under a random null model with the same degree sequence. Thresholding this connectivity yields a topology-preserving abstraction that simultaneously encodes disconnected clusters AND continuous trajectories (reconciling clustering and trajectory inference). Provides a single, resolution-tunable map of global data topology and is far cheaper than per-cell trajectory methods.
papers.relevance: This is the closest existing method to 'topologically-aware' clustering, and its partition-connectivity null-model ratio is a natural place to inject calibrated confidence: our conformal layer can turn PAGA's heuristic connectivity threshold into a coverage-controlled test of whether two putative islet cell states are genuinely connected on the manifold. PAGA is also the default topology backbone in Scanpy, so it defines the interface our method must beat/extend.
papers.quantitative_findings: Genome Biology 20:59. ~1,475+ citations (scispace) and growing (1,400+ across sources). Connectivity = observed/expected inter-cluster edge ratio under a degree-preserving random null.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,500 citations (standard topology-preserving abstraction; core of Scanpy)

papers.citation: McInnes, L., Healy, J. & Melville, J. (2018). UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction. arXiv:1802.03426. (Applied to single cells by Becht, E. et al. (2019), Dimensionality reduction for visualizing single-cell data using UMAP, Nature Biotechnology, 37, 38–47, DOI:10.1038/nbt.4314.)
papers.key_contribution: Defines the graph-construction procedure that now dominates single-cell pipelines. For each point i, UMAP finds k nearest neighbors, sets rho_i = distance to the nearest neighbor (local-connectivity constraint), and calibrates a per-point bandwidth sigma_i so that sum_j exp(-(d(x_i,x_j)-rho_i)/sigma_i) = log2(k). This yields directed fuzzy membership weights w_{i->j}=exp(-(d(x_i,x_j)-rho_i)/sigma_i), symmetrized by a fuzzy-set union / probabilistic t-conorm: w_{ij}=w_{i->j}+w_{j->i}-w_{i->j}w_{j->i}. The layout minimizes the cross-entropy between this fuzzy simplicial-set graph and a low-dim graph. Becht et al. benchmark it on scRNA-seq/CyTOF, showing faster, more reproducible, better-organized embeddings than t-SNE.
papers.relevance: The adaptive per-point bandwidth (rho_i, sigma_i) and the probabilistic t-conorm symmetrization are the exact graph-construction primitives we should adopt/robustify for the islet kNN graph, and they are what Scanpy's neighbors graph uses by default (feeding Leiden, PAGA, and DPT). Understanding UMAP-graph as a fuzzy simplicial complex is also the bridge to the algebraic-topology/persistent-homology component of our 'topologically-aware' design.
papers.quantitative_findings: UMAP arXiv preprint + JOSS (McInnes et al. 2018, JOSS 3(29):861): tens of thousands of citations combined (~15,000+). Becht et al. Nat Biotech 37:38-47: ~4,800 citations. Bandwidth rule: sum of memberships = log2(k); symmetrization by t-conorm.
papers.approx_impact: UMAP ~15,000+; Becht single-cell UMAP ~4,800 (dominant graph/embedding standard)

papers.citation: Moon, K.R., van Dijk, D., Wang, Z., Gigante, S., Burkhardt, D.B., Chen, W.S., ... Krishnaswamy, S. (2019). Visualizing structure and transitions in high-dimensional biological data (PHATE). Nature Biotechnology, 37, 1482–1492. DOI:10.1038/s41587-019-0336-3.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces an explicitly information-geometric distance for single-cell embedding. PHATE builds an alpha-decay adaptive-bandwidth kNN diffusion operator P, powers it to an optimal time t chosen by the knee of the von Neumann entropy of P^t, then defines the 'potential distance' by log-transforming the diffusion probabilities: U_i = -log(P^t row i) and V_t(i,j) = ||U_i - U_j||_2, an informational/log-potential distance (related to Hellinger / KL geometry on the probability simplex) that, unlike raw diffusion distance, is not dominated by local neighbors and preserves both local and global/branch structure. Embeds V_t via metric MDS. Introduces DEMaP (denoised embedding manifold preservation) to quantify how faithfully an embedding preserves geodesic structure.
papers.relevance: This is the single most on-point precedent for the 'information-geometric' term in our title: PHATE already treats rows of P^t as probability distributions and uses a log/informational distance between them. We generalize this from visualization to a statistically calibrated, conformal clustering objective (e.g., a Fisher-Rao / Hellinger metric on the diffusion-probability simplex across scales). DEMaP is a ready-made quantitative yardstick for our method's structure preservation.
papers.quantitative_findings: Nature Biotechnology 37:1482-1492. ~1,500 Google Scholar citations (Semantic Scholar 688). Potential/informational distance V_t=||-log P^t_i + log P^t_j||; optimal t via von Neumann entropy knee; DEMaP metric introduced.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,500 citations (information-geometric embedding standard)

papers.citation: Kuchroo, M., Huang, J., Wong, P., Grenier, J.-C., Shung, D., Tong, A., ... Krishnaswamy, S. (2022). Multiscale PHATE identifies multimodal signatures of COVID-19. Nature Biotechnology, 40(5), 681–691. DOI:10.1038/s41587-021-01186-x.
papers.key_contribution: Delivers a genuinely multiscale, topology-building diffusion method via diffusion condensation: iteratively (1) compute the diffusion operator P at bandwidth epsilon, (2) move each point toward its diffusion-weighted neighborhood (x <- P x), (3) merge points that collapse together, (4) grow the bandwidth. This produces a continuous hierarchy (tree) of resolutions from single cells up to coarse populations, where each condensation iteration corresponds to an increasing diffusion timescale. Multiscale PHATE learns and visualizes this whole granularity spectrum, and salient/stable scales are selected from the 'gradient' (rate of merging) of the condensation process. Applied to 54M cells from 168 COVID-19 patients to extract multi-resolution features predictive of mortality.
papers.relevance: This is the state of the art for the 'multiscale' axis of our paper and the most direct competitor: it already couples random-walk timescales to a hierarchy of cell-state resolutions. Its critical gap — which we fill — is the absence of any calibrated statistical confidence on which scales/clusters are real. We can build our conformal-confidence and topological (persistence-style) machinery directly on top of a condensation hierarchy to flag low-confidence rare states such as HNF1A-driven transitional beta-cells.
papers.quantitative_findings: Nature Biotechnology 40(5):681-691. ~75+ citations by early reporting and climbing (Altmetric 457; 34,000+ accesses). Built on Brugnone et al. 'Coarse Graining of Data via Inhomogeneous Diffusion Condensation' (IEEE BigData 2019).
papers.approx_impact: ~75–250 citations (fast-rising; leading multiscale diffusion method)

result.synthesis:
The subfield rests on a single unifying object: a random walk (Markov transition matrix P) built on an affinity graph over cells, whose powers P^t implement a multiscale probing of manifold structure. Coifman and Lafon (2006), with the Coifman et al. (2005) PNAS companions, established the core machinery — the alpha-family of density-normalized diffusion operators, the eigen-decomposition P psi_i = lambda_i psi_i, and the diffusion distance D_t^2 = sum_i lambda_i^{2t}(psi_i(x)-psi_i(y))^2 realized by the map lambda_i^t psi_i(x). Nadler, Lafon, Coifman and Kevrekidis (2006) supplied the dynamical interpretation that makes 'random-walk timescale' a meaningful phrase for cell biology: eigenvalues correspond to relaxation timescales tau_i = -1/ln(lambda_i), spectral gaps signal metastable states/basins, and the slow eigenfunctions are reaction coordinates (in the continuum, eigenfunctions of a backward Fokker-Planck operator). Cell states are thus the metastable basins of a diffusion process, and the number of resolvable states is a function of how long the walk runs.

Single-cell genomics operationalized this in a rapid sequence. Haghverdi et al. (2015) ported diffusion maps to scRNA-seq with adaptive kernels and dropout/uncertainty handling; Haghverdi et al. (2016, DPT) replaced the arbitrary choice of a single diffusion time with a scale-integrated operator M = sum_t T^t = (I-T)^{-1} (trivial part removed), giving a parameter-free distance weighted by lambda_i/(1-lambda_i) — an early 'sum over all timescales' idea. On the graph-construction side, UMAP (McInnes et al. 2018; Becht et al. 2019 for single cells) defined the now-dominant recipe: per-point local connectivity rho_i, entropy-calibrated bandwidth sigma_i (memberships summing to log2 k), and fuzzy-set (t-conorm) symmetrization — the graph that feeds Leiden clustering, PAGA and DPT in Scanpy. PAGA (Wolf et al. 2019) added a topology-preserving coarse graph whose partition-connectivity is an observed-vs-expected edge ratio against a degree-preserving null, reconciling discrete clusters with continuous trajectories. PHATE (Moon et al. 2019) reframed the distance information-geometrically, treating rows of P^t as probability distributions and using a log-potential (informational) distance; Multiscale PHATE (Kuchroo et al. 2022) turned diffusion timescale into an explicit hierarchy via diffusion condensation.

The trajectory of the field is therefore convergent with our paper's three adjectives — information-geometric (PHATE), multiscale (DPT's scale integration, Multiscale PHATE's condensation hierarchy), and topologically-aware (PAGA, and downstream RNA-velocity/Markov methods like CellRank/G-PCCA that coarse-grain the walk into metastable macrostates via a real Schur decomposition). What no existing method offers is statistical calibration: every one of these tools chooses bandwidths, diffusion times, cluster counts, and connectivity thresholds by heuristics (von Neumann entropy knees, log2 k rules, eigenvalue-gap eyeballing, null-model ratios) with no finite-sample guarantee. That is the open lane our conformal-confidence layer occupies.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) No calibrated uncertainty on cluster membership or cluster count. Diffusion maps, DPT, PAGA, PHATE, and Multiscale PHATE all pick the number of states via unguaranteed heuristics (spectral gaps, entropy knees, connectivity thresholds). A conformal layer that produces finite-sample coverage guarantees on (a) cell-to-cluster assignment, (b) the existence/connectivity of a putative state, and (c) the correct resolution/scale is genuinely novel and directly submittable. 2) Multiscale structure exists but is not statistically reconciled across scales. DPT integrates timescales only for 1-D pseudotime; Multiscale PHATE builds a hierarchy but offers no per-scale confidence. Opportunity: a conformal test for which scales/tau_i host stable, reproducible partitions. 3) Information geometry is used for visualization, not inference. PHATE's log-potential distance is never turned into a clustering objective or a probabilistic model. Opportunity: a Fisher-Rao/Hellinger metric on the diffusion-probability simplex as the clustering distance, with conformal calibration. 4) Topology is coarse. PAGA connectivity is a single null-model ratio; there is no persistent-homology-grade tracking of which topological features (components, loops, branches) persist across diffusion timescales. Opportunity: couple random-walk timescales to persistence, then attach coverage guarantees. 5) Graph-construction fragility. Results depend sharply on k, epsilon, and alpha with no principled, data-driven, guarantee-backed selection. 6) Rare / transitional states are systematically under-resolved — precisely the failure mode that matters for HNF1A-MODY, where the biologically important signal is a small, possibly transitional beta-cell subpopulation; single-scale graphs either merge it away or isolate it as noise, and no current method flags it as low-confidence rather than silently dropping it.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt/adapt concretely: (1) Coifman-Lafon alpha=1 (Laplace-Beltrami) density normalization k_alpha(x,y)=k(x,y)/(q(x) q(y)) so uneven islet cell-type abundances do not bias geometry; expose alpha as an ablation. (2) Variable/adaptive-bandwidth kNN kernel (Haghverdi 2015; PHATE alpha-decay kernel) with per-cell epsilon_i from neighbor distances for dropout-heavy data. (3) UMAP graph primitives for the base kNN graph: local-connectivity offset rho_i, entropy-calibrated sigma_i (sum_j exp(-(d-rho_i)/sigma_i)=log2 k), and probabilistic-t-conorm symmetrization w_ij = w_{i->j}+w_{j->i}-w_{i->j}w_{j->i}. (4) Random-walk timescale model selection (Nadler 2006): compute tau_i = -1/ln(lambda_i) and use spectral gaps as the candidate cluster-count signal that the conformal layer then validates. (5) DPT's scale-integrated operator M=(I-T)^{-1} with weights lambda_i/(1-lambda_i) as a scale-free affinity, but re-weight timescales rather than summing uniformly to build our multiscale clustering distance. (6) PHATE's information-geometric potential distance V_t = ||-log P^t_i + log P^t_j|| (equivalently a Hellinger/Fisher-Rao distance on the P^t simplex) as the metric on which we run conformal clustering — this operationalizes 'information-geometric.' Choose t via the von Neumann-entropy knee of P^t as a starting heuristic to calibrate against. (7) Diffusion condensation (Multiscale PHATE / Brugnone 2019) x <- P x with merging to generate the multiscale hierarchy/tree, giving per-scale candidate partitions for conformal scoring; use the condensation-gradient for salient-scale proposals. (8) PAGA partition-connectivity (observed/expected inter-cluster edges under a degree-preserving null) as the topology-aware coarse-graph statistic, upgraded from a heuristic threshold to a coverage-controlled conformal test. (9) Optionally G-PCCA / real-Schur coarse-graining (CellRank, Lange et al. 2022, Nat Methods) to define metastable macrostates as topology-aware cluster centers. Report DEMaP (Moon 2019) as a quantitative structure-preservation benchmark against PHATE/UMAP/diffusion maps.

result.subtopic: Markov Stability and multiscale community detection (dynamics-based graph clustering, VI-robust plateau selection) — as the multiscale clustering backbone for information-geometric, topologically-aware scRNA-seq clustering
result.synthesis:
Markov Stability (MS) reframes community detection as a dynamical, not combinatorial, problem: instead of counting intra-cluster edges, it asks how well a partition contains the flow of a random walker over a controllable time horizon. The lineage begins with Lambiotte, Delvenne & Barahona's "Laplacian Dynamics" preprint (arXiv:0812.1770, 2008; expanded as the 2014 IEEE TNSE paper "Random Walks, Markov Processes and the Multiscale Modular Organization of Complex Networks", ~599 cites) and the companion PNAS paper Delvenne, Yaliraki & Barahona (2010, ~501 cites). The central object is the clustered autocovariance of a Markov process on the graph: for the continuous-time random walk with Laplacian L_rw = I − D^-1 A, transition operator P(t) = exp(−t L_rw), stationary Π = diag(π), π_i = d_i/2m, the stability of a partition (indicator matrix H) is r(t,H) = trace[H^T (Π P(t) − π π^T) H] — the excess probability that a walker sits in the same community at times 0 and t. Markov time t is an intrinsic resolution knob: small t resolves many fine communities, large t coarsens to few. The load-bearing theoretical bridge for our paper is that the linearization of MS recovers the classical objectives — at t=1 the linearized stability equals Newman–Girvan modularity, and for general t it equals the single-parameter Reichardt–Bornholdt Potts modularity with resolution γ = 1/t (Reichardt & Bornholdt 2006, ~2000 cites). MS therefore *contains* modularity/spectral clustering as limiting cases while curing their pathologies: the modularity resolution limit that dissolves small-but-real clusters (Fortunato & Barthélemy 2007, ~3000 cites) becomes a scanned, reportable range rather than a fixed blind spot.

Two refinements matter directly for single-cell data, which is dominated by continuous transcriptional trajectories rather than clique-like modules. Schaub, Delvenne, Yaliraki & Barahona (2012, PLoS ONE, ~156 cites) formalize MS as a "zooming lens" and expose the field-of-view limit: a one-step (linearized) walk cannot see beyond one hop, so short-t or linearized MS fails to detect non-clique, elongated communities (rings, paths, lineage-like structures) — exactly the geometry of islet β-cell differentiation. Using the full exponential P(t) = exp(−tL) lets longer walks integrate elongated flow and recover trajectory-shaped clusters. Second, the pipeline for turning vector data (e.g., cells × PCA coordinates) into a geometric/kNN graph and then applying MS is worked out by Liu & Barahona (2020, Applied Network Science, ~58 cites), who show multiscale MS estimates the number of clusters and is robust to graph-construction choices — a direct template for scRNA-seq.

The practical selection question — which of the continuum of t-partitions to trust — is answered with information theory, not eigengaps. Delmotte, Tate, Yaliraki & Barahona (2011, Physical Biology) introduced the robustness workflow now standard in the field: at each t, run the Louvain/Leiden optimizer from many random seeds and measure the ensemble's average Variation of Information VI(t) (Meilă 2007, ~1729 cites), a true metric on the lattice of partitions with VI(C,C') = H(C|C') + H(C'|C). Low VI(t) means the landscape has one dominant optimum (robust to optimizer randomness); an extended low-VI block in the cross-time matrix VI(t,t') means one partition persists across a *range* of scales — a plateau. Robust scales are the coincidence of VI(t) dips, VI(t,t') blocks, and plateaus in the number of communities. This entire machinery — generalized MS Q_gen(t,H) = trace[H^T (F(t) − Σ_k v_{2k-1} v_{2k}^T) H] for undirected/directed/signed graphs, Leiden optimization, and automated VI plateau detection — is packaged in PyGenStability (Arnaudon, Schindler, Peach, Gosztolai, Hodges, Schaub & Barahona, ACM TOMS Algorithm 1044, 2024), the reference implementation we should build on.

papers.citation: Delvenne, J.-C., Yaliraki, S. N., & Barahona, M. (2010). Stability of graph communities across time scales. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS), 107(29), 12755–12760. (arXiv:0812.1811)
papers.key_contribution: Defines the stability of a partition as the clustered autocovariance of a Markov process on the graph, r(t,H) = trace[H^T (Π P(t) − π π^T) H], with Markov time t as an intrinsic resolution parameter that induces a hierarchy of increasingly coarse partitions; shows partitions can be ranked at each t and their optimal time-spans identified.
papers.relevance: This is the canonical statement of Markov Stability and the dynamical objective our multiscale scRNA-seq method optimizes. The autocovariance formulation is precisely the object we intend to replace with an information-geometric (Fisher–Rao) analogue on the probability simplex, so it is the base equation of our contribution.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~501 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Establishes that modularity and normalized Fiedler/spectral partitioning arise as t→specific limits of stability; validates on hierarchical benchmark graphs where a single quality function fails.
papers.approx_impact: ~500 citations; foundational PNAS paper of the subfield

papers.citation: Lambiotte, R., Delvenne, J.-C., & Barahona, M. (2014). Random Walks, Markov Processes and the Multiscale Modular Organization of Complex Networks. IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering, 1(2), 76–90. (First circulated 2008 as 'Laplacian Dynamics and Multiscale Modular Structure in Networks', arXiv:0812.1770)
papers.key_contribution: Systematic dynamical framework deriving quality functions from Markov processes; proves the linearized stability at t=1 equals Newman–Girvan modularity, and for general t equals single-parameter (Reichardt–Bornholdt) modularity with resolution γ = 1/t. Unifies continuous-time (combinatorial Laplacian L=D−A) and discrete/normalized random-walk (L_rw=I−D^-1 A) formulations.
papers.relevance: Supplies the exact 'linearised stability = modularity at time 1' bridge the paper leans on, letting us present our multiscale objective as a principled generalization of modularity rather than a new heuristic, and giving a fast linearized approximation (γ=1/t) for large islet datasets.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~599 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Derives the resolution-parameter correspondence t↔1/γ analytically and demonstrates multiscale recovery on hierarchical/benchmark networks where fixed-resolution modularity misses scales.
papers.approx_impact: ~600 citations; the most-cited technical MS reference

papers.citation: Schaub, M. T., Delvenne, J.-C., Yaliraki, S. N., & Barahona, M. (2012). Markov Dynamics as a Zooming Lens for Multiscale Community Detection: Non Clique-Like Communities and the Field-of-View Limit. PLoS ONE, 7(2), e32210.
papers.key_contribution: Shows Markov time acts as a zooming lens and identifies the 'field-of-view limit': short-time / linearized (one-hop) dynamics cannot resolve non-clique communities (rings, chains, elongated structures); the full exponential P(t)=exp(−tL) with longer t is required to detect them.
papers.relevance: Directly governs whether continuous developmental/differentiation trajectories in islet scRNA-seq (β-cell state continua under HNF1A loss) can be captured as coherent communities. Motivates using full MS, not linearized modularity, and informs the minimum Markov time needed to see trajectory-shaped clusters.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~156 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Demonstrates on ring-of-cliques and non-clique benchmarks that modularity-type (t→0) objectives systematically fail where flow-based MS at appropriate t succeeds.
papers.approx_impact: ~150 citations; the key 'why full dynamics matter' paper

papers.citation: Arnaudon, A., Schindler, D. J., Peach, R. L., Gosztolai, A., Hodges, M., Schaub, M. T., & Barahona, M. (2024). Algorithm 1044: PyGenStability, a Multiscale Community Detection Framework with Generalized Markov Stability. ACM Transactions on Mathematical Software, 50(2). (arXiv:2303.05385, 2023)
papers.key_contribution: Reference software and generalization: optimizes generalized Markov Stability Q_gen(t,H) = trace[H^T (F(t) − Σ_{k=1}^m v_{2k-1} v_{2k}^T) H] over undirected/directed/signed graphs and user-defined quality functions, via Louvain or Leiden; automates robust-scale detection using normalized VI — average NVI(t) across optimizer runs plus persistently low NVI(t,t') blocks — and selects basins around local minima of Block NVI(t).
papers.relevance: The concrete, extensible implementation to build on. Its generalized F(t)/null-model interface is exactly where we can plug an information-geometric similarity and a conformal calibration layer; its directed-graph support handles RNA-velocity graphs, and its automated VI plateau selection is the baseline our conformal method must beat/calibrate.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~10 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026; recent). Provides Leiden-based optimization, constructors for continuous/combinatorial/directed (PageRank teleportation) and signed MS, and automated plateau selection driven by Block NVI(t) minima.
papers.approx_impact: ~10 citations (new); the standard modern MS toolkit

papers.citation: Meilă, M. (2007). Comparing clusterings—an information based distance. Journal of Multivariate Analysis, 98(5), 873–895.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces Variation of Information VI(C,C') = H(C|C') + H(C'|C) = 2H(C,C') − H(C) − H(C'), a true metric on the space of partitions (nonnegative, symmetric, triangle inequality), bounded by log n; the normalized form NVI = VI/H(C,C') ∈ [0,1].
papers.relevance: The metric that operationalizes 'robust/plateau' partition selection across Markov time in every MS workflow (VI(t) over optimizer ensembles; VI(t,t') for cross-scale persistence). We adopt VI both as the plateau detector and as an information-theoretic distance consistent with our information-geometric framing.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~1729 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Establishes metric axioms and bounds enabling principled comparison of partitions of differing cardinality — essential when the number of communities changes across Markov time.
papers.approx_impact: ~1700 citations; the standard clustering-comparison metric

papers.citation: Liu, Z., & Barahona, M. (2020). Graph-based data clustering via multiscale community detection. Applied Network Science, 5, Article 3. (arXiv:1909.04491)
papers.key_contribution: End-to-end pipeline turning vector data into a geometric/kNN graph (comparing construction methods) and applying multiscale Markov Stability to cluster it; shows MS estimates the number of clusters intrinsically and is robust to graph-construction hyperparameters, outperforming methods that require k to be fixed a priori.
papers.relevance: The direct methodological template for scRNA-seq: cells → kNN graph in PCA/latent space → multiscale MS → VI-robust scales. Demonstrates that MS removes the arbitrary 'choose k / choose Leiden resolution' step that plagues standard Seurat/Scanpy pipelines — a central selling point of our method on islet data.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~58 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). On synthetic and benchmark datasets, multiscale graph-based clustering matches or beats k-means/spectral/DBSCAN without externally setting the cluster count; robustness quantified via stability of partitions across graph parameters.
papers.approx_impact: ~60 citations; the canonical 'MS for data clustering' reference

papers.citation: Fortunato, S., & Barthélemy, M. (2007). Resolution limit in community detection. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS), 104(1), 36–41.
papers.key_contribution: Proves modularity maximization has an intrinsic resolution limit: modules smaller than a scale set by the total network size (~sqrt(2m)) are merged even when they are unambiguous clusters, so a single modularity optimum can miss real small communities.
papers.relevance: The core motivation for going multiscale, and a concrete risk in our stress test: a rare HNF1A-mutant β-cell subpopulation could be dissolved by any single-resolution method. MS reframes the limit as a scanned range, and our conformal-confidence layer is what lets us declare such a small cluster statistically real.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~3026 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Analytic scaling argument (merging of cliques in ring-of-cliques networks) quantifying when modules below ~sqrt(L) edges are undetectable at the modularity optimum.
papers.approx_impact: ~3000 citations; the definitive resolution-limit result

papers.citation: Reichardt, J., & Bornholdt, S. (2006). Statistical mechanics of community detection. Physical Review E, 74(1), 016110.
papers.key_contribution: Casts community detection as a Potts spin model with tunable resolution γ, Q_RB(γ) = (1/2m) Σ_ij (A_ij − γ d_i d_j/2m) δ(c_i,c_j); γ=1 recovers Newman–Girvan modularity, and varying γ sweeps community scale.
papers.relevance: Provides the resolution-parameter family that linearized Markov Stability equals with γ = 1/t, tying our Markov-time scan to a well-understood single-parameter modularity and enabling a fast approximate MS mode on large datasets.
papers.quantitative_findings: ~2000 citations (Semantic Scholar, 2026). Introduces the general null-model/resolution formulation underlying most tunable-resolution community-detection methods, including the linearized MS objective.
papers.approx_impact: ~2000 citations; origin of the tunable resolution parameter

result.gaps_opportunities:
GAP 1 — MS is Euclidean/second-order, not information-geometric. Stability r(t,H) = trace[H^T(ΠP(t) − ππ^T)H] uses a flat inner-product (autocovariance) on the random-walk distributions. Opportunity (our core novelty): replace the Euclidean autocovariance with an information-geometric divergence between each node's walker distribution P(t)_{i·} and the stationary π on the probability simplex — e.g. Fisher–Rao distance or an α-divergence — yielding an 'information-geometric Markov stability' whose plateaus respect the statistical manifold of transcriptional states rather than raw Euclidean flow. VI (Meilă) already lives in this information-theoretic world, giving internal consistency.

GAP 2 — No calibrated uncertainty. VI(t) robustness and VI(t,t') plateaus are ensemble heuristics: they tell you a partition is reproducible under optimizer randomness, not that a given cell's assignment has finite-sample coverage. No paper in this lineage attaches per-cell confidence. Opportunity: wrap conformal prediction around the VI-selected plateau partition to emit per-cell cluster-membership sets with distribution-free coverage guarantees — upgrading 'robust' to 'calibrated'. This is the paper's 'conformal confidence' contribution and is genuinely absent from the MS literature.

GAP 3 — Topology beyond the graph is unused. MS uses only the weighted graph; it ignores higher-order/persistent-homology structure. The only single-cell-adjacent multiscale-topology work (Hoekzema et al., Entropy 2022, eigenscores/multiscale Laplacian score/persistent Rayleigh quotient) does spectral/topological *signal selection*, not MS clustering. Opportunity: make plateau detection topologically aware — e.g. treat the VI(t,t') block structure or the flow graph across t as a filtration and use persistence of low-VI blocks as a topological plateau statistic, marrying MS with TDA.

GAP 4 — Field-of-view + resolution limits vs. rare/continuum states. Short-t/linearized MS cannot see non-clique, trajectory-shaped communities (Schaub 2012), and single-resolution modularity dissolves small clusters (Fortunato–Barthélemy 2007). For HNF1A MODY this is exactly the failure mode: β-cell dedifferentiation forms a continuum, and mutant subpopulations may be small. Opportunity: full-exponential MS at longer t to capture the continuum, plus the conformal layer to certify small/rare clusters as real rather than merged noise.

GAP 5 — Directed/velocity graphs under-explored for scRNA-seq. MS supports directed graphs via PageRank teleportation for the stationary distribution (PyGenStability), a natural fit for RNA-velocity kNN graphs, but this is essentially untried in single-cell practice — an easy, novel win.
result.methods_we_should_use:
1) OBJECTIVE — Full continuous-time Markov Stability on the cell graph: build a kNN/geometric graph in latent (PCA/scVI) space (Liu–Barahona pipeline), then maximize r(t,H) = trace[H^T (Π e^{−t L_rw} − π π^T) H] with random-walk Laplacian L_rw = I − D^-1 A, Π = diag(π), π_i = d_i/2m. Use the FULL matrix exponential (not the t→0 linearization) so trajectory-like, non-clique β-cell state communities survive the field-of-view limit (Schaub 2012). Sweep t on a log grid.

2) FAST/SANITY MODE — Linearized MS ≡ Reichardt–Bornholdt modularity with γ = 1/t: Q_RB(γ) = (1/2m) Σ_ij (A_ij − γ d_i d_j/2m) δ(c_i,c_j). Use for large islet atlases and to report the exact modularity-at-t=1 correspondence.

3) OPTIMIZER — Leiden (guaranteed connected, well-separated communities) maximizing generalized MS Q_gen(t,H) = trace[H^T (F(t) − Σ_k v_{2k-1} v_{2k}^T) H] via PyGenStability (Algorithm 1044). For RNA-velocity graphs use the directed constructor (PageRank teleportation for π); for signed correlation graphs use the signed generalized F(t).

4) SCALE SELECTION / PLATEAUS — Meilă VI machinery (Delmotte robustness workflow): at each t run the optimizer from many seeds; compute average normalized NVI(t) = VI over the ensemble (low ⇒ robust to optimizer noise), and the cross-scale matrix NVI(t,t') (extended low-NVI blocks ⇒ scale plateaus). Select robust scales at the coincidence of Block-NVI(t) local minima, NVI(t,t') blocks, and plateaus in the community count. VI(C,C') = H(C|C') + H(C'|C); NVI ∈ [0,1].

5) NOVEL LAYER (our contribution) — (a) Information-geometric MS: replace the Euclidean autocovariance with a Fisher–Rao / α-divergence between P(t)_{i·} and π on the simplex, giving an information-geometric stability whose plateaus we detect with the same VI apparatus. (b) Conformal confidence: calibrate conformal prediction on the VI-selected plateau partition to output per-cell cluster-membership sets with finite-sample coverage; report coverage on held-out islet cells. (c) Topological plateau detector: build a filtration from NVI(t,t') and use persistence of low-VI blocks (persistent homology) as a topologically-aware robustness statistic, connecting MS plateaus to TDA. Stress-test all three on HNF1A MODY islet scRNA-seq, checking that dedifferentiated/rare β-cell states appear as their own conformally-certified plateau clusters rather than being merged (resolution limit) or shattered (field-of-view limit).

result.subtopic: Persistent homology and topological data analysis foundations: filtrations (Vietoris-Rips vs alpha vs Cech vs DTM), stability theorems, and stable vectorizations (diagrams, landscapes, images), with emphasis on which constructions are robust to density variation and outliers

papers.citation: Zomorodian, A., & Carlsson, G. (2005). Computing persistent homology. Discrete & Computational Geometry, 33(2), 249-274.
