papers.key_contribution: Algebraic foundation of persistent homology: PH of a filtered complex is a finitely generated graded F[t]-module whose PID structure-theorem decomposition over a field gives the barcode as a complete invariant, plus the standard reduction algorithm to compute it in arbitrary dimension over any field.
papers.relevance: Provides the exact object we cluster on -- the barcode/persistence diagram -- and the guarantee that it is a well-defined, field-computable invariant. Betti numbers beta_k and the interval decomposition are the multiscale features our information-geometric clustering will summarize per cell/neighborhood.
papers.quantitative_findings: Structure theorem: over a field F, a finitely generated persistence module = direct sum of interval modules I[b,d). Reduction algorithm is O(m^3) worst case in the number of simplices m (later improved). Citations approx 2,000 (Semantic Scholar/SciSpace) and on the order of 4,000 on Google Scholar.
papers.approx_impact: ~2,000-4,000 (database-dependent); a foundational top-cited PH paper

papers.citation: Cohen-Steiner, D., Edelsbrunner, H., & Harer, J. (2007). Stability of persistence diagrams. Discrete & Computational Geometry, 37(1), 103-120.
papers.key_contribution: The bottleneck stability theorem: for tame functions f, g on a triangulable space, d_B(Dgm(f), Dgm(g)) <= ||f - g||_inf. Establishes persistence diagrams as Lipschitz-stable descriptors and defines the bottleneck metric on diagram space.
papers.relevance: This is the certificate of robustness our paper leans on: it lets us bound how much a persistence-based cluster summary can move under bounded perturbations of the filtering function, the backbone of any conformal/topological confidence claim.
papers.quantitative_findings: d_B(D,D') = inf over bijections eta of sup_x ||x - eta(x)||_inf; theorem gives d_B <= ||f-g||_inf (1-Lipschitz). The companion Wasserstein-stability result (Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner, Harer, Mileyko 2010) bounds W_p under total-persistence/Lipschitz conditions. Citations on the order of 2,500+ (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~2,500+; the canonical stability reference

papers.citation: Carlsson, G. (2009). Topology and data. Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society, 46(2), 255-308.
papers.key_contribution: Field-defining survey articulating the program of applying algebraic topology to data: coordinate-free, deformation-invariant, compressed multiscale representations; formalizes persistence/barcodes as the central tool and motivates functoriality and the Mapper viewpoint.
papers.relevance: Frames the conceptual case for a topological, multiscale, metric-invariant view of cell-state space -- exactly the motivation for topologically-aware scRNA-seq clustering -- and is the standard citation for why topology (not just geometry) matters for data.
papers.quantitative_findings: No single theorem; programmatic. Semantic Scholar lists approx 2,467 citations; Google Scholar counts run several thousand higher.
papers.approx_impact: ~2,500 (Semantic Scholar) / several thousand (Google Scholar); the most-cited TDA manifesto

papers.citation: Chazal, F., Cohen-Steiner, D., & Merigot, Q. (2011). Geometric inference for probability measures. Foundations of Computational Mathematics, 11(6), 733-751.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces the distance-to-measure (DTM) function, a density-aware, outlier-robust surrogate for the distance-to-support, and proves it is stable in sup norm w.r.t. the Wasserstein distance between the underlying measures.
papers.relevance: This is the theoretical key to density-robust filtrations for scRNA-seq: replacing Euclidean distance-to-data with DTM makes topological inference stable under non-uniform sampling density and outliers (dropout, doublets), which raw Rips/Cech cannot handle.
papers.quantitative_findings: d_{mu,m}(x)^2 = (1/m) int_0^m delta_{mu,l}(x)^2 dl with delta_{mu,l}(x)=inf{r: mu(B(x,r))>=l}; stability ||d_{mu,m}-d_{nu,m}||_inf <= (1/sqrt(m)) W_2(mu,nu). Empirical DTM = mean of the k=mn nearest-neighbor squared distances. Citations approx 700-900 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~700-900; foundational for robust/statistical TDA

papers.citation: Chazal, F., de Silva, V., & Oudot, S. (2014). Persistence stability for geometric complexes. Geometriae Dedicata, 173(1), 193-214.
papers.key_contribution: Extends stability from functions to filtered geometric complexes built on metric spaces (Vietoris-Rips, Cech, witness complexes), proving their persistence diagrams are stable w.r.t. the Gromov-Hausdorff distance via a persistence-module interleaving argument.
papers.relevance: Tells us precisely what classical filtrations are and are not stable to: they are Gromov-Hausdorff/Hausdorff stable but NOT robust to outliers or density -- the exact gap that motivates using DTM-based filtrations instead for scRNA-seq.
papers.quantitative_findings: Uses the interleaving distance d_I on persistence modules and the algebraic stability theorem (Chazal-de Silva-Glisse-Oudot) so that d_B(Dgm) <= d_I <= 2 d_GH; establishes multiplicative Rips-Cech interleaving Cech(r) subset VR(2r) subset Cech(2r). Citations on the order of 600 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~600; standard citation for point-cloud filtration stability

papers.citation: Bubenik, P. (2015). Statistical topological data analysis using persistence landscapes. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 16, 77-102.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces the persistence landscape, a functional (Banach/Hilbert-space) summary of a diagram enabling means, variances, hypothesis tests, and a central limit theorem for topological summaries.
papers.relevance: Gives us a vector-space embedding of barcodes on which statistics and kernels operate directly -- the natural feature space for feeding multiscale topology into clustering and for building bootstrap/conformal confidence bands.
papers.quantitative_findings: For each (b,d) the tent Lambda_{(b,d)}(t)=max(0,min(t-b,d-t)); landscape lambda_k(t)=k-th largest of {Lambda_i(t)}. It is 1-Lipschitz: ||lambda(D)-lambda(D')||_inf <= d_B(D,D'); supports a functional CLT and bootstrap confidence bands. Citations approx 1,000+ (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~1,000+; the standard statistical vectorization of PH

papers.citation: Adams, H., Emerson, T., Kirby, M., Neville, R., Peterson, C., Shipman, P., Chepushtanova, S., Hanson, E., Motta, F., & Ziegelmeier, L. (2017). Persistence images: A stable vector representation of persistent homology. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 18(8), 1-35.
papers.key_contribution: Maps a persistence diagram to a fixed-length, stable feature vector (the persistence image) by transforming to birth-persistence coordinates, placing weighted Gaussians, and integrating over a grid; proves stability w.r.t. the 1-Wasserstein distance.
papers.relevance: Provides the concrete ML-ready encoding of per-cell/per-neighborhood barcodes for our clustering pipeline, with a diagonal-vanishing weight that suppresses low-persistence noise -- important given scRNA-seq dropout.
papers.quantitative_findings: Transform (b,d)->(b, d-b); surface rho(z)=sum_i w(d_i-b_i) g_{(b_i,d_i-b_i)}(z) with Gaussian g and nonnegative weight w vanishing on the diagonal; pixel integrals give the vector. Stable w.r.t. W_1 with a Lipschitz weight. Citations approx 1,200+ (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~1,200+; the standard fixed-vector PH featurization for ML

papers.citation: Anai, H., Chazal, F., Glisse, M., Ike, Y., Inakoshi, H., Tinarrage, R., & Umeda, Y. (2020). DTM-based filtrations. In Topological Data Analysis (Abel Symposia, vol. 15), Springer, 33-66; conference version SoCG 2019, LIPIcs 129, 58:1-58:15.
papers.key_contribution: Defines the DTM-filtration, a weighted Rips-type filtration where each point's onset is governed by the distance-to-measure, and proves its diagrams are bottleneck-stable under Wasserstein perturbations of the underlying measure -- robustness Rips/Cech lack.
papers.relevance: This is the direct, drop-in answer to 'which filtration is stable to density variation': it is the density-robust filtration we should build our multiscale, topologically-aware scRNA-seq clustering on, tolerant to outliers, doublets and non-uniform sampling.
papers.quantitative_findings: Weighted-Rips filtration with radius function derived from the empirical DTM (an averaged kNN distance); stability proved in bottleneck distance w.r.t. W_p of the generating measure, inheriting the (1/sqrt(m)) W_2 DTM bound. Newer method (SoCG 2019); citations roughly 100-150 (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~100-150; the current reference for outlier/density-robust filtrations

result.synthesis:
Persistent homology (PH) tracks how topological features (connected components, loops, voids -- i.e. ranks of homology groups H_k, the Betti numbers) appear and disappear across a nested family of spaces (a filtration). The modern theory rests on three pillars. (1) An algebraic foundation: Zomorodian & Carlsson (2005) showed that the PH of a filtered simplicial complex is a finitely generated graded module over F[t], which by the structure theorem for PIDs decomposes uniquely (over a field) into interval modules -- yielding the barcode / persistence diagram Dgm as a complete discrete invariant, and a matrix-reduction algorithm to compute it. Formally the persistent Betti number is beta_k^{a,b} = rank(H_k(X_a) -> H_k(X_b)). (2) A metric-stability foundation: Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner & Harer (2007) proved the bottleneck stability theorem, d_B(Dgm(f),Dgm(g)) <= ||f-g||_inf, where d_B(D,D') = inf_{eta} sup_x ||x-eta(x)||_inf over bijections eta; the p-Wasserstein analogue W_p(D,D') = (inf_eta sum_x ||x-eta(x)||_inf^p)^{1/p} is bounded under Lipschitz/total-persistence conditions (Cohen-Steiner-Edelsbrunner-Harer-Mileyko 2010). Chazal, de Silva & Oudot (2014) lifted this from functions to point clouds, proving Vietoris-Rips and Cech filtrations are stable in the bottleneck/interleaving sense with respect to the Gromov-Hausdorff distance. (3) A statistical/vectorization foundation making PH usable in ML: persistence landscapes (Bubenik 2015) map a diagram to a function lambda_k(t) in a Banach/Hilbert space (1-Lipschitz w.r.t. d_B, admitting means, a CLT and bootstrap), and persistence images (Adams et al. 2017) map it to a fixed-length vector via Gaussian smoothing in birth-persistence coordinates with a diagonal-vanishing weight, stable w.r.t. the 1-Wasserstein distance. Carlsson's (2009) 'Topology and data' is the field-defining manifesto tying these together; Ghrist (2008, Bull. AMS) popularized the barcode viewpoint and Edelsbrunner & Harer's textbook 'Computational Topology: An Introduction' (2010, ~1,335 citations on Semantic Scholar) is the standard reference for the machinery.

The decisive question for noisy, variable-density biological data like scRNA-seq is which filtration is stable to density variation, and here the classical results are a trap. Rips/Cech/alpha filtrations are stable only w.r.t. Hausdorff (support) perturbations: a single outlier or a locally sparse region shifts the Hausdorff/Gromov-Hausdorff distance arbitrarily, so their guarantees are worthless under outliers and non-uniform sampling density. Alpha complexes (subcomplexes of the Delaunay triangulation, homotopy-equivalent to unions of balls via the nerve theorem) are far cheaper than Cech in low dimension but inherit the same outlier fragility. The remedy is to filter by a measure rather than a support. Chazal, Cohen-Steiner & Merigot (2011) introduced the distance-to-measure (DTM) d_{mu,m}(x)^2 = (1/m) int_0^m delta_{mu,l}(x)^2 dl, whose empirical version is an averaged k-nearest-neighbor distance (k = mn) -- an intrinsically density-aware, outlier-robust distance that is stable in the sup norm w.r.t. the Wasserstein distance between measures: ||d_{mu,m}-d_{nu,m}||_inf <= (1/sqrt(m)) W_2(mu,nu). Anai et al. (2020) turned this into the DTM-filtration, a weighted-Rips filtration whose diagrams are provably bottleneck-stable under Wasserstein perturbations of the underlying measure -- i.e. robust to exactly the outlier/density artifacts that break Rips and Cech.

Toward the confidence angle of our paper, Fasy et al. (2014, Annals of Statistics, 'Confidence sets for persistence diagrams') established a statistical inference layer: bootstrap or subsampling the sup-norm/bottleneck fluctuation gives a threshold c_n, and diagram points farther than c_n from the diagonal are declared significant ('topological signal' vs 'topological noise'), yielding confidence sets for persistence diagrams. This is the closest existing analogue to the conformal confidence we intend to build, though it certifies the diagram, not downstream cluster assignments. The overall state of the art is thus mature on single-scale, single-parameter theory (decomposition, stability, vectorization, density-robust filtrations, first-order statistical inference) but still open on scalable multiparameter (density x scale) persistence, choice of the base metric, and calibrated confidence for PH-derived clusters -- precisely the seams our method targets.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) Density-robustness is solved only for the FILTRATION, not for downstream cluster labels. Fasy et al. (2014) give bootstrap confidence sets for the diagram itself, but there is no conformal, finite-sample guarantee on per-cell cluster ASSIGNMENTS -- our opening to define conformity scores from DTM-filtration barcodes (via persistence images/landscapes) and emit calibrated conformal prediction sets over cell states (e.g., HNF1A-MODY beta-cell subpopulations). 2) The base METRIC is treated as given (Euclidean/PCA/UMAP), yet all stability bounds are metric-dependent; nobody has built DTM filtrations on an information-geometric (Fisher-Rao / Hellinger) metric between cells' count distributions, which is more faithful to negative-binomial/dropout scRNA-seq noise than Euclidean distance. 3) Multiscale + density is naturally a two-parameter (radius x density/DTM-threshold) BIFILTRATION, but multiparameter persistence has no complete discrete invariant and no scalable, stable vectorization -- a concrete methodological target. 4) Existing stability is for additive/Wasserstein perturbations; the specific scRNA-seq corruption model (dropout, library-size variation, negative-binomial counts) is not characterized, leaving room for a noise-model-aware stability theorem. 5) Scalability: matrix-reduction PH is worst-case cubic in simplex count while islet datasets have thousands of cells x thousands of genes, so sparse-Rips/witness approximations with certified interleaving error are needed to make the pipeline tractable.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt: (a) Build the filtration from the empirical distance-to-measure d_{mu,m}(x)^2 = (1/k) sum_{j=1}^{k} ||x - X_{(j)}(x)||^2 (k = mn nearest neighbors), and specifically use the DTM-filtration (Anai et al. 2020) -- a weighted Rips filtration -- rather than raw Vietoris-Rips or Cech, to obtain provable robustness to density variation and outliers via the bound ||d_{mu,m}-d_{nu,m}||_inf <= (1/sqrt(m)) W_2(mu,nu). (b) Compute distances on an information-geometric metric (Fisher-Rao / Hellinger / diffusion) over the cell manifold instead of Euclidean, so the density-robust filtration also respects the statistical geometry of expression. (c) Use the alpha complex (Delaunay-based, nerve theorem) only after aggressive dimension reduction (2-3D) where it is cheap; otherwise use sparse-Rips/witness complexes with certified interleaving error to control cost. (d) Certify robustness with the bottleneck theorem d_B(Dgm(f),Dgm(g)) <= ||f-g||_inf and p-Wasserstein W_p bounds; report both d_B and W_2 between reference and perturbed diagrams. (e) Vectorize barcodes with persistence images (birth-persistence coords, Gaussian smoothing, diagonal-vanishing weight w, W_1-stable) and persistence landscapes lambda_k(t) (tent functions Lambda_{(b,d)}, 1-Lipschitz in d_B, Hilbert-space valued) as conformity scores / cluster features. (f) Separate signal from noise and seed the confidence layer with Fasy-style bootstrap/subsampling thresholds c_n on the bottleneck fluctuation, then extend to split-conformal p-values for cluster membership. (g) For the multiscale-density coupling, prototype a (radius x DTM) bifiltration and summarize it with fibered/sliced landscapes or persistence images as a stable proxy for the unavailable complete two-parameter invariant.

result.subtopic: Trajectory inference and the discrete-vs-continuous cell-state distinction in single-cell RNA-seq (Monocle, Slingshot, PAGA, RNA velocity/scVelo, CellRank, the Saelens benchmark, and the emerging information-geometric view)
result.synthesis:
The field has moved through three conceptual eras, each of which bears directly on our paper's thesis that cell identity is simultaneously discrete and continuous. Era one (pseudotime) reframed static snapshots as ordered progressions: Monocle (Trapnell 2014) embedded cells in a low-dimensional space and ordered them along a minimum-spanning-tree path, with Monocle 2 (Qiu 2017) formalizing this as "reversed graph embedding" (DDRTree) and Diffusion Pseudotime (Haghverdi 2016) recasting ordering as an information-geometric random walk — DPT integrates transition probabilities over ALL diffusion scales, making it the earliest multiscale, manifold-aware pseudotime and a direct ancestor of what we propose. Slingshot (Street 2018) became the benchmark-winning tree method by decoupling the two subproblems our paper also separates: it takes a discrete clustering as input, builds a cluster-level MST to fix topology, then fits simultaneous principal curves to recover continuous pseudotime. This "cluster first, then order" pattern is the field's implicit answer to discrete-vs-continuous, but it is heuristic: the clustering resolution is chosen by hand and never revisited. Era two (topology + direction) attacked exactly that weakness. PAGA (Wolf 2019) is the single most relevant prior art for "topologically-aware clustering": it explicitly reconciles clustering with trajectory inference by testing, for each pair of Leiden/Louvain partitions, whether their inter-cluster connectivity exceeds a random-graph null, yielding a coarse graph whose edges encode which discrete clusters are actually connected by continuous transitions. In parallel, RNA velocity (La Manno 2018) added an arrow of time by exploiting unspliced/spliced mRNA ratios, and scVelo (Bergen 2020) generalized it from a steady-state ratio estimator to a full likelihood/EM solution of the splicing ODEs with per-cell latent time.

Era three (probabilistic fate and rigorous evaluation) is where confidence enters. CellRank (Lange 2022) unifies velocity-derived direction with transcriptomic similarity into a single Markov chain, then uses GPCCA (Generalized Perron-Cluster Cluster Analysis via a real Schur decomposition) to coarse-grain it into initial/intermediate/terminal macrostates and computes absorption probabilities as soft, per-cell fate assignments on a probability simplex. This is the closest existing thing to a "confidence," but it is an uncalibrated model output, not a coverage-guaranteed prediction set — precisely the gap our conformal layer fills. Anchoring all of this, the Saelens (2019) dynverse benchmark of 45 methods on 110 real + 229 synthetic datasets established the field's evaluation grammar: topology via the Hamming-Ipsen-Mikhailov (HIM) distance, ordering via correlation of pairwise geodesic distances, feature recovery via weighted correlation (wcor), branch assignment via F1, combined as a geometric mean. Its central empirical finding — that no method dominates and that the right choice depends chiefly on the (often unknown) trajectory topology — is itself an argument for a multiscale, topology-adaptive method.

Cutting across all three eras is the still-unresolved discrete-vs-continuous question, and the newest work reframes it as a geometry problem rather than a clustering problem. Reviews consensus that biological heterogeneity is genuinely a mixture of discrete types and continuous states, yet standard pipelines force a choice via a resolution parameter. The GAIA framework (Cai, ... , J.J. Li, Xin; Briefings in Bioinformatics 2025 / bioRxiv 2026) makes the sharpest theoretical move directly adjacent to our paper: it argues that Euclidean and log-Euclidean distances are mis-specified for multinomial count data, models each cell as a multinomial over genes, and uses the Fisher-Rao metric — which, after the square-root embedding, equals the great-circle (geodesic) distance on the unit hypersphere — to capture presence/absence (discrete) and magnitude (continuous) variation in one principled distance. This validates our information-geometric premise but leaves the multiscale, topological, directional, and conformal-confidence dimensions entirely open, which is the whitespace our method occupies.
result.gaps_opportunities:
1) No single method jointly delivers information geometry + multiscale resolution + topology + directionality + calibrated confidence. GAIA supplies the Fisher-Rao geometry and the discrete/continuous reconciliation but is static (no trajectory, no topology test, no direction, no uncertainty). CellRank/scVelo supply direction and soft fates but build their graph on Euclidean/PCA space and emit uncalibrated probabilities. PAGA supplies topology but is undirected and gives no confidence. DPT supplies multiscale diffusion but on a Euclidean kernel with no uncertainty. Our contribution is the integration.
2) Uncertainty is essentially uncalibrated field-wide. RNA velocity offers no coverage guarantee (and its assumptions of constant per-gene kinetic rates, a single kinetic regime, and gene independence are frequently violated — Bergen 2021 MSB; Gorin, Fang, Chari & Pachter 2022 "RNA velocity unraveled," PLoS Comp Biol). Pseudotime confidence intervals are ad hoc bootstraps; cluster assignments carry no statistical error control. Conformal prediction — which gives finite-sample, distribution-free marginal coverage P(y in C(x)) >= 1-alpha — has essentially never been applied to cell-type/fate assignment. This is the widest, highest-value open gap and our clearest differentiator.
3) Discrete-vs-continuous is still decided heuristically (silhouette width, cluster stability, chosen Leiden resolution). There is no principled statistical test. Coupling the Fisher-Rao/diffusion metric with persistent homology (H0 persistence = degree of separation/discreteness; H1 = cycles/continuous loops such as cell cycle) would yield the first formal, geometry-grounded test — a headline result opportunity.
4) Benchmarks are generic developmental data; no disease/monogenic-perturbation benchmark exists. HNF1A-MODY islets are an ideal, clinically meaningful stress test because the biology is exactly the ambiguous case: is HNF1A loss a discrete switch in beta-cell identity or a continuous dedifferentiation gradient? A method that can answer that with calibrated confidence is both novel and useful.
5) Multiscale structure is underexploited: nearly all methods commit to one resolution. A hierarchy tied to diffusion time t or persistence scale (rather than an arbitrary resolution knob) is missing and aligns with Saelens's finding that topology (hence the right scale) is usually unknown a priori.
result.methods_we_should_use:
ADOPT / ADAPT, with equations:
(1) Information-geometric cell distance (from GAIA, extended). Model cell c as a multinomial p^(c) over G genes (normalized counts). The square-root embedding sqrt(p) lands on the positive orthant of the unit sphere S^{G-1} (since sum p_g = 1 => sum (sqrt p_g)^2 = 1). Use the Fisher-Rao geodesic distance: d_FR(p,q) = 2 * arccos( sum_g sqrt(p_g q_q) ). The inner sum is the Bhattacharyya coefficient BC; note 1 - BC = squared Hellinger distance. Use d_FR (not Euclidean/log-Euclidean) to build the kNN graph — it encodes discrete presence/absence and continuous magnitude jointly and is depth-robust.
(2) Multiscale diffusion geometry (from diffusion maps / DPT, Haghverdi 2016). Affinity W_ij = exp(-d_FR(i,j)^2 / sigma^2); transition matrix P = D^{-1} W. Diffusion distance at scale t: D_t(i,j)^2 = sum_k (P^t_{ik} - P^t_{jk})^2 / phi_k. DPT integrates all scales via the accumulated transition operator M = sum_{t>=1} (P - 1*psi^T)^t = (I - P + 1*psi^T)^{-1} - 1*psi^T on the non-stationary subspace, with DPT(i,j) = || M_{i.} - M_{j.} ||. Sweep t (or eigen-scale) to produce a principled multiscale cluster hierarchy instead of a hand-set resolution.
(3) Topology (PAGA + persistent homology). PAGA edge statistic: for partitions i,j compare observed inter-partition edge count e_ij against its expectation under a random graph with the same degree sequence; retain statistically significant edges to get a topology-preserving abstracted graph. Add a Vietoris-Rips filtration on d_FR (or the diffusion metric): the H0 persistence barcode quantifies discreteness (long-lived components = discrete types), H1 detects loops (cell cycle / continuous manifolds) — the persistence diagram becomes our formal discrete-vs-continuous test statistic.
(4) Directionality (RNA velocity + CellRank kernel). Splicing ODEs: du/dt = alpha - beta*u, ds/dt = beta*u - gamma*s. Steady-state velocity v = u - gamma_hat * s (gamma_hat by regression on extreme-quantile steady states); or scVelo's likelihood-EM for full latent time t and state k. Build a velocity kernel (cosine similarity between the velocity vector and cell-cell displacement) and combine with the connectivity/diffusion kernel: T = lambda * T_velocity + (1 - lambda) * T_connectivity.
(5) Macrostates & soft fates (CellRank/GPCCA). Coarse-grain T by Generalized Perron-Cluster Cluster Analysis (real Schur decomposition -> membership functions) to find initial/terminal macrostates; absorption probabilities to terminal states give a per-cell fate simplex — the natural object to calibrate.
(6) Conformal confidence (our novelty). Use nonconformity score s(x) = information-geometric distance to assigned prototype, or (1 - max fate probability). Split/inductive conformal yields per-cell prediction sets C(x) for {cell type / fate} with guaranteed marginal coverage P(y in C(x)) >= 1 - alpha. Use Mondrian (class-conditional) conformal per cluster/lineage so coverage holds within rare populations (e.g., HNF1A-affected beta-cell substates).
(7) Evaluation (Saelens/dynverse). Report the four metrics against ground truth: cor_dist (correlation of pairwise geodesic distances = ordering), HIM (Hamming + Ipsen-Mikhailov spectral distance = topology), wcor (weighted correlation of feature-importance lists), F1_branches (branch assignment); combine as geometric mean. Generate labeled trajectories with dyngen/dyntoy for controlled discrete/continuous mixtures.

papers.citation: Trapnell, C., Cacchiarelli, D., Grimsby, J., Pokharel, P., Li, S., Morse, M., Lennon, N. J., Livak, K. J., Mikkelsen, T. S., & Rinn, J. L. (2014). The dynamics and regulators of cell fate decisions are revealed by pseudotemporal ordering of single cells. Nature Biotechnology, 32(4), 381-386. doi:10.1038/nbt.2859
papers.key_contribution: Introduced Monocle and the concept of pseudotime: order individual cells along a learned trajectory in a low-dimensional embedding (originally ICA + minimum spanning tree; Monocle 2 (Qiu et al. 2017, Nat Methods) reformulated this as reversed graph embedding / DDRTree, Monocle 3 (Cao 2019) scaled it to UMAP + principal graphs). Established that scRNA-seq snapshots can be reorganized into continuous developmental progressions.
papers.relevance: Founding statement that cell state is a continuum, not just a set of discrete clusters - the premise our discrete-vs-continuous framing builds on. The cluster-graph-then-order pattern anticipates our multiscale topology + ordering pipeline.
papers.quantitative_findings: Applied to differentiating human myoblasts; revealed switch-like regulator dynamics and sequential gene-regulation waves. Among the most-cited scRNA-seq method papers (approx. 5,700 citations per SciSpace; substantially higher on Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: Field-defining; ~5,700+ citations (SciSpace), one of the most-cited single-cell analysis papers.

papers.citation: Haghverdi, L., Buettner, M., Wolf, F. A., Buettner, F., & Theis, F. J. (2016). Diffusion pseudotime robustly reconstructs lineage branching. Nature Methods, 13(10), 845-848. doi:10.1038/nmeth.3971
papers.key_contribution: Diffusion Pseudotime (DPT): defines cell-to-cell distance from a random walk on the kNN graph, accumulating transition probabilities over ALL diffusion time scales via an accumulated transition matrix M, and detects branch points and metastable states from it. The earliest multiscale, manifold-/information-geometric pseudotime.
papers.relevance: Direct methodological ancestor of our multiscale, information-geometric component. We adapt its scale-integrated diffusion distance but replace the Euclidean/Gaussian kernel with a Fisher-Rao kernel and add topological and conformal layers.
papers.quantitative_findings: Robust to noise and to the choice of root cell; recovers branching and metastable states on hematopoiesis and other developmental data. Widely cited foundational method (roughly 1,500+ citations).
papers.approx_impact: High; foundational for diffusion-based pseudotime (~1,500+ citations).

papers.citation: Street, K., Risso, D., Fletcher, R. B., Das, D., Ngai, J., Yosef, N., Purdom, E., & Dudoit, S. (2018). Slingshot: cell lineage and pseudotime inference for single-cell transcriptomics. BMC Genomics, 19, 477. doi:10.1186/s12864-018-4772-0
papers.key_contribution: Two-stage tree trajectory inference: takes a discrete clustering as input, orders clusters into lineages via a minimum spanning tree (global topology), then fits simultaneous principal curves and assigns each cell a pseudotime by orthogonal projection (arc length) onto the curves (local continuity).
papers.relevance: Canonical example of the field's implicit discrete-vs-continuous resolution: fix discrete structure first, then recover the continuous gradient. Our method makes both stages multiscale and topology-adaptive rather than requiring a single fixed clustering.
papers.quantitative_findings: Simulation studies showed more accurate pseudotimes than competing methods for 1-3 branch trajectories; ranked among the top general-purpose tree methods by the Saelens benchmark. Approximately 2,700-3,200 citations (BMC/other counters).
papers.approx_impact: Very high; the most-adopted tree/lineage TI method (~2,700-3,200 citations).

papers.citation: Wolf, F. A., Hamey, F. K., Plass, M., Solana, J., Dahlin, J. S., Goettgens, B., Rajewsky, N., Simon, L., & Theis, F. J. (2019). PAGA: graph abstraction reconciles clustering with trajectory inference through a topology preserving map of single cells. Genome Biology, 20, 59. doi:10.1186/s13059-019-1663-x
papers.key_contribution: Partition-based graph abstraction: for each pair of clusters, statistically tests whether inter-cluster connectivity in the kNN graph exceeds a random-graph null, producing a coarse, topology-preserving graph whose weighted edges say which discrete clusters are connected by continuous transitions. Explicitly unifies clustering (discrete) with trajectory inference (continuous) at multiple resolutions.
papers.relevance: The single closest prior art to our 'topologically-aware clustering' aim. We extend PAGA's connectivity test with an information-geometric metric, persistent-homology-based discrete/continuous testing, and calibrated confidence on the abstracted edges/nodes.
papers.quantitative_findings: Produced consistent topologies across four hematopoietic datasets, adult planaria, and zebrafish embryo; benchmarked at scale on ~1 million neurons with high computational efficiency. Approximately 1,500 citations (SciSpace-indexed; higher on Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: High; standard topology-preserving abstraction, embedded in Scanpy (~1,500+ citations).

papers.citation: La Manno, G., Soldatov, R., Zeisel, A., Braun, E., Hochgerner, H., Petukhov, V., Lidschreiber, K., Kastriti, M. E., Loennerberg, P., Furlan, A., ... Kharchenko, P. V. (2018). RNA velocity of single cells. Nature, 560, 494-498. doi:10.1038/s41586-018-0414-6
papers.key_contribution: Introduced RNA velocity: estimate the time derivative of expression from the ratio of unspliced (intronic) to spliced mRNA. Steady-state model gives velocity v = u - gamma*s as the deviation from the inferred steady-state ratio gamma (fit by regression on extreme quantiles), predicting each cell's near-future state.
papers.relevance: Supplies the directionality our Markov/CellRank layer needs to convert an undirected topology into a directed one - key for asking whether HNF1A-MODY beta cells are moving along a dedifferentiation gradient. We must handle its known assumption violations with uncertainty quantification.
papers.quantitative_findings: Recovered directed dynamics in mouse/human developmental systems (e.g., hippocampus, chromaffin) on hourly timescales. Approximately 3,600 citations (SciSpace).
papers.approx_impact: Field-creating; launched the RNA velocity subfield (~3,600+ citations).

papers.citation: Bergen, V., Lange, M., Peidli, S., Wolf, F. A., & Theis, F. J. (2020). Generalizing RNA velocity to transient cell states through dynamical modeling. Nature Biotechnology, 38(12), 1408-1414. doi:10.1038/s41587-020-0591-3
papers.key_contribution: scVelo: solves the full splicing ODEs du/dt = alpha - beta*u, ds/dt = beta*u - gamma*s with a likelihood-based EM (dynamical model), assigning each cell a latent time t and transcriptional state k (induction/repression/steady), plus a stochastic (second-moment) model. Removes the steady-state assumption and yields gene-specific transcription/splicing/degradation rates.
papers.relevance: The velocity estimator we would actually deploy; its per-cell latent time and phase-portrait likelihoods are natural inputs to a conformal nonconformity score and to the velocity kernel in our directed Markov chain.
papers.quantitative_findings: Disentangled subpopulation kinetics in neurogenesis and (notably for us) pancreatic endocrinogenesis; identified putative driver genes via differential kinetics. Widely used (thousands of citations; exact count not separately confirmed in this review).
papers.approx_impact: Very high; the default velocity toolkit (Scanpy/scVelo ecosystem).

papers.citation: Lange, M., Bergen, V., Klein, M., Setty, M., Reuter, B., Bakhti, M., Lickert, H., Ansari, M., Schniering, J., Schiller, H. B., Pe'er, D., & Theis, F. J. (2022). CellRank for directed single-cell fate mapping. Nature Methods, 19, 159-170. doi:10.1038/s41592-021-01346-6
papers.key_contribution: Builds a Markov chain over cells by combining a velocity kernel with a transcriptomic-similarity (connectivity) kernel, then coarse-grains it with GPCCA (Generalized Perron-Cluster Cluster Analysis via real Schur decomposition) to find initial/intermediate/terminal macrostates and computes absorption probabilities as soft, per-cell fate probabilities.
papers.relevance: The closest existing thing to per-cell 'confidence' (a fate simplex), but uncalibrated - our conformal layer turns these soft assignments into prediction sets with coverage guarantees. Its kernel-combination and GPCCA machinery are components we directly adopt.
papers.quantitative_findings: On pancreas development automatically detected initial/intermediate/terminal populations and predicted fate potentials; on lineage-traced reprogramming recovered known outcomes; predicted and experimentally confirmed a new dedifferentiation trajectory in post-injury lung regeneration. CellRank 2 (Weiler et al. 2024, Nat Methods 21:1196-1205) generalized it to multiview/non-velocity data. Several hundred citations (exact count not separately confirmed).
papers.approx_impact: High and rising; leading probabilistic fate-mapping framework.

papers.citation: Saelens, W., Cannoodt, R., Todorov, H., & Saeys, Y. (2019). A comparison of single-cell trajectory inference methods. Nature Biotechnology, 37(5), 547-554. doi:10.1038/s41587-019-0071-9
papers.key_contribution: Systematic benchmark of 45 trajectory-inference methods on 110 real and 229 synthetic datasets across accuracy, topology, scalability, and usability, with the open dynverse framework and simulators (dyngen, dyntoy). Defined the field's standard metric suite: correlation of pairwise geodesic distances (ordering), Hamming-Ipsen-Mikhailov (HIM) distance (topology), weighted correlation of feature importances (wcor), and F1 on branch assignment, combined by geometric mean.
papers.relevance: Our evaluation protocol. We report these exact metrics against ground truth and use dynverse simulators to build controlled discrete/continuous mixtures for stress-testing our method.
papers.quantitative_findings: Found no universally best method; performance depends chiefly on (usually unknown) trajectory topology and dataset dimensions - a direct motivation for topology-adaptive, multiscale methods. Approximately 1,360 citations (SciSpace-indexed; higher on Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: High; the reference benchmark and metric standard for TI (~1,360+ citations).

papers.citation: Cai, J., Wang, Y., Qiao, Y., Wang, C., Rong, Z., Zhou, L., Liu, H., Li, J. J., & Xin, H. (2025). Harmonizing qualitative and quantitative analyses in scRNA-seq via information geometry (GAIA). Briefings in Bioinformatics, 26(Suppl. 1), i33- (RECOMB 2026 proceedings track). Also bioRxiv 2026, doi:10.64898/2026.02.25.707866.
papers.key_contribution: GAIA (Geometric Analysis from an Information Aspect): argues Euclidean/log-Euclidean distances are mis-specified for multinomial count data, models each cell as a multinomial over genes, and measures cell-cell similarity by the Fisher-Rao geodesic distance, which after the square-root embedding equals the great-circle (angular) distance on the unit hypersphere. This organically captures both presence/absence (discrete) and magnitude (continuous) variation.
papers.relevance: The most directly aligned prior art to our information-geometric premise and the strongest paper to position against. It validates Fisher-Rao geometry for the discrete-vs-continuous problem but leaves multiscale structure, topology, directionality, and calibrated (conformal) confidence entirely open - exactly our contributions.
papers.quantitative_findings: On synthetic and real scRNA-seq and spatial datasets, preserved consistent cell-cell relationships, delineated fine subtypes, mitigated sequencing-depth batch effects, and removed dependence on curated gene selection. New work (2025-2026); minimal citations to date.
papers.approx_impact: Emerging but high-relevance; peer-reviewed (Briefings in Bioinformatics) and accepted at RECOMB 2026.

result.subtopic: Conformal prediction for calibrated confidence sets on cluster / cell-type labels in single-cell RNA-seq
result.synthesis:
Conformal prediction (CP) is a distribution-free framework that converts any point predictor into a set-valued predictor with a finite-sample, marginal coverage guarantee under a single assumption — exchangeability of calibration and test data. The mechanics for our setting (attaching confidence to a discrete cluster/cell-type label) are now standard and cheap: use split/inductive CP (Papadopoulos 2002; Lei et al. 2018), define a nonconformity score s(x,y) from a trained classifier's softmax, compute the calibration quantile q̂ = Quantile(⌈(n+1)(1−α)⌉/n; {s_i}), and output C(x) = {y : s(x,y) ≤ q̂}. Equivalently, form conformal p-values p(x,y) = (1+|{i:s_i≥s(x,y)}|)/(n+1) and keep labels with p>α. This yields P(Y∈C(X)) ≥ 1−α with the two-sided bound 1−α ≤ coverage ≤ 1−α+1/(n+1). The score choice is where the field's substance lives: THR/LAC (Sadinle et al. 2019), s=1−f̂_y(x), is Bayes-optimal for the SMALLEST average set size at fixed coverage but sacrifices conditional coverage; APS (Romano, Sesia & Candès 2020), the cumulative sorted-softmax mass, and its regularized variant RAPS (Angelopoulos et al. 2021) trade a little size for far better adaptivity and approximate conditional coverage. The Angelopoulos & Bates 'gentle introduction' is the field's canonical operational reference and defines the evaluation metrics we should report (marginal coverage, mean set size, and size-stratified coverage/SSC).

The central technical tension for a cell-type/cluster application is marginal vs. conditional validity. A prediction set that is 90% valid on average can systematically under-cover rare or clinically critical populations — exactly the HNF1A-MODY regime, where the biologically decisive cells (a small, perturbed β-/islet subpopulation) are rare. Mondrian/class-conditional CP (Vovk et al. 2003) restores per-class validity by calibrating a separate quantile q̂_g within each group g, giving P(Y∈C(X)|Y=g) ≥ 1−α; but with K large and per-class calibration counts n_g small, per-class quantiles become unstable. Ding et al. (2023) solve precisely this with clustered conformal prediction — classes are embedded by the quantiles of their score distributions, clustered into m≪K groups, and Mondrian CP is run at the cluster level to borrow statistical strength. This is the most directly transferable idea for atlases with dozens–hundreds of cell types and a long tail of rare ones.

The subfield has now landed in genomics. López-De-Castro et al. (2025, Bioinformatics) give the first careful CP annotator for scRNA-seq: inductive CP with THR/APS/RAPS scores under standard, class-wise, and cluster taxonomies, plus an autoencoder-reconstruction nonconformity score for conformal out-of-distribution (novel cell type) detection with a controlled false-positive rate, validated on pancreas (GSE84133), lung, breast and mouse gastrulation. Corbetta et al. (2024, scConform) add topology: they exploit the cell-type ontology as a graph so prediction sets respect hierarchical relationships, tested on MERFISH and scRNA-seq. Both confirm CP delivers near-nominal coverage on islet/pancreas data and expose the open frontiers our paper targets — genuine cluster/topology-conditional guarantees, validity under the batch/covariate shift that breaks exchangeability, and confidence that is calibrated jointly with (not bolted onto) a multiscale clustering.

papers.citation: Vovk, V., Gammerman, A., & Shafer, G. (2005; 2nd ed. 2022). Algorithmic Learning in a Random World. Springer.
papers.key_contribution: Founds conformal prediction: defines nonconformity measures, conformal p-values, and proves finite-sample validity P(Y∈C(X))≥1−α under exchangeability alone (no distributional assumptions). Introduces full/transductive CP and the notion of prediction efficiency.
papers.relevance: The theoretical bedrock for every confidence set we attach to a cluster/cell-type label. The exchangeability assumption is exactly the condition that batch effects and query/reference shift in scRNA-seq threaten, so it frames both our guarantee and its failure mode.
papers.quantitative_findings: Guarantee: for any score and exchangeable data, miscoverage is exactly ≤α; the marginal coverage of split CP is bounded 1−α ≤ P(Y∈C) ≤ 1−α+1/(n+1). Foundational monograph, ~8,000+ citations (Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: ~8,000+ citations; the defining reference of the field

papers.citation: Papadopoulos, H., Proedrou, K., Vovk, V., & Gammerman, A. (2002). Inductive Confidence Machines for Regression. ECML 2002, LNCS 2430, 345–356.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces inductive (split) conformal prediction: split data into a proper-training set (fit the model once) and a calibration set (compute scores/quantile). Removes the need to retrain per test point, making CP tractable at scale.
papers.relevance: Split CP is the only computationally viable scheme for scRNA-seq atlases with 10^5–10^6 cells; every modern single-cell CP method (including López-De-Castro 2025 and scConform) is built on this train/calibrate split.
papers.quantitative_findings: Reduces CP cost from O(n) model fits (full CP) to a single fit plus an O(n log n) quantile computation, at the price of statistical efficiency from splitting the sample. ~1,000+ citations.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,000+ citations; the split-CP primitive used universally

papers.citation: Sadinle, M., Lei, J., & Wasserman, L. (2019). Least Ambiguous Set-Valued Classifiers With Bounded Error Levels. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 114(525), 223–234.
papers.key_contribution: Derives the oracle set-valued classifier that minimizes expected set size (ambiguity) subject to a coverage constraint; the optimal set thresholds the class posteriors. Yields the THR/LAC nonconformity score s(x,y)=1−f̂_y(x).
papers.relevance: Gives the efficiency-optimal score for the smallest confidence sets at a target coverage — the baseline our method must beat, and the natural choice when the goal is tight, decision-ready cell-type calls.
papers.quantitative_findings: Oracle sets are level sets {y: f̂_y(x)≥t_α} and are provably minimum-ambiguity at the nominal error level; empirically THR gives the smallest mean set size but weakest conditional coverage vs APS/RAPS. ~600+ citations.
papers.approx_impact: ~600+ citations; origin of the THR/LAC score

papers.citation: Romano, Y., Sesia, M., & Candès, E. (2020). Classification with Valid and Adaptive Coverage. NeurIPS 33, 3581–3591.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces Adaptive Prediction Sets (APS): the nonconformity score is the cumulative probability mass of classes ranked at least as likely as y, s(x,y)=Σ_{y':f̂_{y'}≥f̂_y} f̂_{y'} (with a uniform randomization term). Gives marginal validity plus approximate conditional coverage.
papers.relevance: APS produces confidence sets whose size grows in ambiguous regions and shrinks in confident ones — the desired behavior when a query cell sits between related islet cell types (e.g., β vs other endocrine); it is one of the three scores adopted in the scRNA-seq CP annotator.
papers.quantitative_findings: Achieves exact 1−α marginal coverage and markedly better conditional/class-wise coverage than THR, at modestly larger sets; randomized version attains coverage exactly rather than conservatively. ~900+ citations.
papers.approx_impact: ~900+ citations; the standard adaptive classification score

papers.citation: Angelopoulos, A. N., Bates, S., Jordan, M. I., & Malik, J. (2021). Uncertainty Sets for Image Classifiers using Conformal Prediction. ICLR 2021 (spotlight).
papers.key_contribution: Introduces RAPS (Regularized APS): adds a penalty λ·(rank_y(x)−k_reg)_+ to the APS score to suppress the long tail of low-probability classes, and defines the size-stratified coverage (SSC) metric for auditing conditional validity.
papers.relevance: With many cell types and a noisy softmax tail, plain APS sets bloat; RAPS regularization is exactly what keeps confidence sets small and stable across a large cell-type taxonomy, and SSC is the conditional-coverage diagnostic we should report.
papers.quantitative_findings: On ImageNet/ImageNet-V2 (ResNet-152), RAPS attains nominal coverage with sets 5–10× smaller than naive APS while improving size-stratified (adaptive) coverage. ~700+ citations.
papers.approx_impact: ~700+ citations; introduced RAPS and the SSC metric

papers.citation: Angelopoulos, A. N., & Bates, S. (2021/2023). A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification. arXiv:2107.07511; Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, 16(4), 494–591.
papers.key_contribution: The canonical modern tutorial: unifies split CP, APS/RAPS, conformalized quantile regression, group-balanced/Mondrian CP, risk control, and the evaluation protocol (marginal coverage, mean set size, size-stratified coverage) in one recipe-driven reference.
papers.relevance: Our methods section's operational template — it standardizes the exact quantile formula, coverage checks, and metrics reviewers will expect, and situates cluster-conditional CP within the broader distribution-free UQ landscape.
papers.quantitative_findings: States the split-CP algorithm and the two-sided coverage bound 1−α ≤ P(Y∈C) ≤ 1−α+1/(n+1); among the most-cited CP references (thousands of citations on Google Scholar).
papers.approx_impact: Very high; thousands of Google Scholar citations, the field's standard tutorial

papers.citation: Ding, T., Angelopoulos, A. N., Bates, S., Jordan, M. I., & Tibshirani, R. J. (2023). Class-Conditional Conformal Prediction with Many Classes. NeurIPS 2023.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces clustered conformal prediction: embed each class by the quantiles of its nonconformity-score distribution, cluster the K classes into m≪K clusters, then run Mondrian (class-conditional) CP at the cluster level to obtain near class-conditional coverage with far less per-class calibration data.
papers.relevance: Directly solves our hardest problem: giving per-cell-type (cluster-conditional) guarantees when many types have few calibration cells — the rare-population/HNF1A-MODY regime — by pooling statistically similar clusters. This is the template for our multiscale, cluster-conditional confidence.
papers.quantitative_findings: Delivers substantially improved class-conditional coverage over exact-Mondrian (which needs ~⌈1/α⌉ points per class and destabilizes with scarce data) and over marginal CP, while keeping sets compact. ~150+ citations and growing fast.
papers.approx_impact: ~150+ citations; the reference method for class/cluster-conditional CP with scarce per-class data

papers.citation: López-De-Castro, M., García-Galindo, A., González-Gomariz, J., & Armañanzas, R. (2025). Conformal inference for reliable single cell RNA-seq annotation. Bioinformatics, 41(10), btaf521.
papers.key_contribution: First systematic CP framework for scRNA-seq cell-type annotation: inductive CP with THR, APS and RAPS scores under standard (marginal), class-wise (Mondrian), and cluster taxonomies; plus an autoencoder-reconstruction nonconformity score enabling conformal OOD (novel cell type) detection with controlled false-positive rate E[FPR]≤α0.
papers.relevance: The closest existing baseline to our paper and a direct demonstration on pancreatic islets (GSE84133, leave-one-donor-out). Establishes the taxonomies, scores, coverage checks, and OOD protocol our method must extend (with topology + multiscale + tighter conditional guarantees).
papers.quantitative_findings: Data split 45% train / 40% calibration / 15% validation; at α=0.05 empirical coverage ≈95% across all three taxonomies, with APS/RAPS giving smaller sets than THR (e.g., breast mean set size ≈1.2–2.2). OOD power >90% at α0=0.01 on in-distribution query; coverage/FPR guarantees break under tumor query where exchangeability is violated. Code: github.com/digital-medicine-research-group-UNAV/conformalized_single_cell_annotator.
papers.approx_impact: New (2025); the direct scRNA-seq CP baseline

papers.citation: Corbetta, D., Finos, L., Geistlinger, L., & Risso, D. (2024). Conformal inference for cell type annotation with graph-structured constraints. arXiv:2410.23786; R/Bioconductor package scConform.
papers.key_contribution: Makes CP topology-aware by encoding the cell-type ontology as a graph and constraining/scoring prediction sets to respect the hierarchy, so sets contain biologically coherent related types rather than arbitrary label collections, while retaining (1−α) coverage.
papers.relevance: The only existing 'topologically-aware' CP method for single cells and the closest prior art to our topology component; motivates combining ontology-graph structure with cluster-conditional calibration and hierarchical (ontology-distance) risk control.
papers.quantitative_findings: Demonstrates improved coverage–efficiency tradeoffs and smaller, hierarchy-consistent sets vs. flat CP on MERFISH and scRNA-seq datasets; distributed as the Bioconductor package scConform.
papers.approx_impact: Recent (2024); first graph/ontology-aware single-cell CP

result.gaps_opportunities:
1) Genuine cluster/topology-CONDITIONAL coverage, not marginal. Existing single-cell CP (López-De-Castro 2025) reports strong marginal and approximate class-wise coverage, but exact per-cluster validity for a long tail of rare types is unsolved at scale. This is the impossibility barrier: exact conditional coverage P(Y∈C(x)|X=x)≥1−α is provably unattainable distribution-free in finite samples (Vovk 2012; Barber, Candès, Ramdas & Tibshirani 2020, 'The limits of distribution-free conditional predictive inference'). Opportunity: adapt clustered conformal (Ding et al. 2023) so that OUR information-geometric multiscale clusters ARE the Mondrian groups — turning the clustering hierarchy into a nested sequence of conditional-coverage guarantees (coarse scale = tighter n_g, fine scale = finer resolution). No existing paper couples the clustering geometry to the conditional-CP partition.

2) Exchangeability failure under batch/covariate shift. The scRNA-seq CP papers explicitly show guarantees break when the query is distributionally shifted (López-De-Castro's tumor query; any HNF1A-perturbed islet vs healthy reference). Opportunity: integrate weighted/covariate-shift CP (Tibshirani et al. 2019) or Mondrian-by-batch calibration, and report coverage under held-out donors/conditions — a rigorous shift-robustness story reviewers will demand for a MODY application.

3) Topology-aware LOSS, not just topology-aware sets. scConform constrains sets to the ontology but does not control an ontology-distance error. Opportunity: use Conformal Risk Control (Angelopoulos et al. 2024, ICLR) with a monotone hierarchical loss (e.g., shortest-path distance on the cell-type graph) so miscoverage is penalized by biological severity — clinically, confusing β-cells with a distant type should cost more than confusing adjacent endocrine subtypes.

4) Confidence calibrated jointly with the clustering, not bolted on. All prior work calibrates CP on top of a fixed classifier. Our information-geometric multiscale clusterer produces a natural nonconformity score (e.g., Fisher-Rao / geodesic distance to cluster centroids on the statistical manifold, or negative log-likelihood under a per-cluster model) that is more faithful than a generic softmax — an untaken opportunity to define a geometry-native score with the standard split-CP validity wrapper.

5) Rare-population efficiency. With MODY the decisive cells are rare; exact-Mondrian needs ≈⌈1/α⌉ calibration cells per type and collapses for rare types. Clustered conformal and score-quantile pooling are the levers; benchmarking mean set size AND size-stratified coverage specifically on rare islet subpopulations is an open, high-value evaluation.
result.methods_we_should_use:
CORE SPLIT-CP WRAPPER (adopt verbatim): (i) split into proper-train / calibration; (ii) compute calibration scores s_i=s(X_i,Y_i); (iii) q̂ = the ⌈(n+1)(1−α)⌉/n empirical quantile of {s_i}; (iv) C(x)={y: s(x,y)≤q̂}. Report the two-sided guarantee 1−α ≤ P(Y∈C) ≤ 1−α+1/(n+1). Equivalent p-value form: p(x,y)=(1+|{i:s_i≥s(x,y)}|)/(n+1), keep {y: p(x,y)>α}.

NONCONFORMITY SCORES to implement and compare: (a) THR/LAC: s=1−f̂_y(x) — smallest sets, efficiency baseline (Sadinle 2019). (b) APS: s(x,y)=Σ_{y':f̂_{y'}(x)≥f̂_y(x)} f̂_{y'}(x) with randomized term u·f̂_y(x), u~Unif(0,1) — adaptivity (Romano 2020). (c) RAPS: APS + λ·(o_y(x)−k_reg)_+ where o_y is the rank of y (λ, k_reg tuned on a held-out split) — small stable sets over many cell types (Angelopoulos 2021). (d) OUR geometry-native score: Fisher-Rao/geodesic distance on the information manifold to the cluster's statistical model, or −log p(x|cluster); wrap in the same split-CP quantile — this is our novelty while retaining validity.

CONDITIONAL / MULTISCALE CALIBRATION: Mondrian CP — partition calibration by group g (cell type OR multiscale cluster), q̂_g = ⌈(n_g+1)(1−α)⌉/n_g quantile of within-group scores, giving P(Y∈C|group=g)≥1−α (Vovk 2003). When n_g is small, use CLUSTERED conformal (Ding 2023): embed each class by quantiles (e.g., the 0.5/0.6/.../0.9 quantiles) of its score distribution, k-means into m≈√(n̄) clusters, run Mondrian at cluster level. Nest this across our clustering scales for a multiscale guarantee.

TOPOLOGY-AWARE RISK CONTROL: use Conformal Risk Control (Angelopoulos 2024) — pick λ̂ = inf{λ : (n/(n+1))·R̂_n(λ) + B/(n+1) ≤ α} for a monotone loss ℓ, with ℓ = graph/ontology shortest-path distance between the true type and the set (topology-aware miscoverage). Generalizes coverage (ℓ=miscoverage indicator recovers standard CP).

OOD / NOVEL-TYPE DETECTION: autoencoder reconstruction error as nonconformity score, conformal p-value test with E[FPR]≤α0 (López-De-Castro 2025) — essential for flagging HNF1A-perturbed cells absent from a healthy reference.

SHIFT ROBUSTNESS: weighted conformal prediction with likelihood-ratio weights w(x)=dP_test/dP_cal for covariate shift (Tibshirani et al. 2019), and/or calibrate Mondrian-by-donor/batch; report coverage on held-out donors.

METRICS to report (all from Angelopoulos & Bates 2021): empirical marginal coverage, mean/median set size |C(x)| (efficiency), and size-stratified coverage (SSC) plus per-cell-type coverage as the conditional-validity audit. Benchmark all scores × taxonomies (standard/class-wise/cluster) as in the scRNA-seq CP baseline, on pancreas GSE84133 with leave-one-donor-out.

result.subtopic: Batch-effect correction and integration for scRNA-seq, and its interaction with downstream clustering and rare-population preservation

papers.citation: Haghverdi, L., Lun, A.T.L., Morgan, M.D. & Marioni, J.C. (2018). Batch effects in single-cell RNA-sequencing data are corrected by matching mutual nearest neighbors. Nature Biotechnology 36(5), 421-427. DOI:10.1038/nbt.4091.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced the mutual nearest neighbor (MNN) paradigm: after cosine normalization, cells i in batch 1 and j in batch 2 that are reciprocally among each other's k nearest neighbors form an MNN pair. Each pair defines a cell-specific batch-correction vector; per-cell correction vectors are computed by Gaussian-kernel smoothing (weighted averaging) over all pairs, yielding locally varying (non-linear) correction. Implemented in the batchelor/fastMNN Bioconductor package.
papers.relevance: Foundational for any topologically-aware method: the MNN graph is itself a topological object over the joint cell manifold, and the correction is a local vector field on that manifold. Its two explicit assumptions are exactly the ones our multiscale/topological prior must respect: (1) the batch effect is nearly orthogonal to the biological subspace, and (2) batch-effect magnitude is smaller than the biological difference between cell types. Violations of (2) are precisely what destroys rare islet subpopulations.
papers.quantitative_findings: k-NN with cosine normalization; correction vector u_i = sum over MNN pairs of a Gaussian weight exp(-||.||^2/sigma^2) times the pairwise difference. Orthogonalization of the correction against the biological subspace guards against removing biology.
papers.approx_impact: Highly cited foundational method; on the order of 2,000-3,000+ citations.

papers.citation: Stuart, T., Butler, A., Hoffman, P., Hafemeister, C., Papalexi, E., Mauck, W.M. III, Hao, Y., Stoeckius, M., Smibert, P. & Satija, R. (2019). Comprehensive Integration of Single-Cell Data. Cell 177(7), 1888-1902.e21. DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2019.05.031.
papers.key_contribution: Seurat v3 'anchor' framework. Uses diagonalized canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to find a shared low-dimensional subspace capturing correlated (shared) gene-correlation structure across batches, L2-normalizes the canonical correlation vectors, then finds MNNs in that CCA space; each MNN is an 'anchor.' Anchors are scored/filtered by shared-neighbor overlap (SNN consistency) and used to compute weighted correction vectors, enabling both batch harmonization and label/feature transfer.
papers.relevance: The dominant off-the-shelf integration workflow and the reference we must beat. CCA finds shared correlation structure but is a linear, single-scale projection; anchor scoring is a heuristic reliability weight that is a natural place to insert a conformal confidence score. Anchor-based label transfer is directly analogous to the reference-mapping we would use to detect HNF1A MODY beta-cell states.
papers.quantitative_findings: Diagonalized CCA + L2 normalization of canonical vectors; anchor weighting by number of shared neighbors within a local neighborhood (default k.anchor=5, k.weight=100). Demonstrated cross-modality (RNA + ATAC + protein) and cross-species transfer.
papers.approx_impact: One of the most-cited scRNA-seq papers; on the order of 10,000+ citations.

papers.citation: Hie, B., Bryson, B. & Berger, B. (2019). Efficient integration of heterogeneous single-cell transcriptomes using Scanorama. Nature Biotechnology 37(6), 685-691. DOI:10.1038/s41587-019-0113-3.
papers.key_contribution: Generalizes MNN from pairs of batches to all pairs among many datasets ('panorama stitching'). Uses randomized SVD for a shared low-dimensional space and approximate nearest neighbors via hyperplane locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) to find MNNs across every dataset pair in near-linear time; merges datasets by Gaussian-kernel-weighted correction vectors and merges in order of dataset-pair alignment score.
papers.relevance: Provides the scalable, order-independent multi-batch matching machinery we need for atlas-scale islet integration, and its alignment-score matrix is a graph over datasets that fits a multiscale/topological formulation. Its approximate-NN backbone is the practical route to computing our information-geometric neighbor graph at scale.
papers.quantitative_findings: Integrated 105,476 cells across 26 experiments and 9 technologies; reported orders-of-magnitude speedups over MNN/Seurat v2 via LSH approximate NN and randomized SVD.
papers.approx_impact: Widely used; on the order of 1,500+ citations.

papers.citation: Korsunsky, I., Millard, N., Fan, J., Slowikowski, K., Zhang, F., Wei, K., Baglaenko, Y., Brenner, M., Loh, P.-R. & Raychaudhuri, S. (2019). Fast, sensitive and accurate integration of single-cell data with Harmony. Nature Methods 16(12), 1289-1296. DOI:10.1038/s41592-019-0619-0.
papers.key_contribution: Iterates two steps in PCA space until convergence: (1) maximum-diversity soft k-means clustering, and (2) mixture-model linear (ridge) batch correction. The clustering objective minimizes sum_ik R_ik ||Z_i - Y_k||^2 (cosine distance on L2-normalized PCs) + sigma sum_ik R_ik log R_ik (entropy regularization -> soft assignment) + sigma*theta sum_k sum_b O_kb log(O_kb/E_kb) (a cross-entropy 'diversity' penalty forcing each cluster's batch composition O toward the global expectation E). Correction fits cluster-specific ridge regressions of the embedding on the batch design matrix and applies a soft-assignment-weighted (R_ik) removal of the batch term.
papers.relevance: Directly information-geometric in spirit: the diversity term is a KL/cross-entropy between the per-cluster batch distribution and the global batch distribution, exactly the object our Fisher-Rao / KL formulation would generalize. theta (diversity strength) and sigma (soft-assignment temperature/bandwidth) are explicit knobs we can turn into a multiscale schedule. Fast, top-ranked on RNA, and a strong baseline; but its single-K soft-clustering is where rare populations get absorbed.
papers.quantitative_findings: Defaults sigma=0.1, theta~1-2, ridge lambda; converges in a handful of iterations; benchmarked as among the fastest methods with strong LISI/kBET.
papers.approx_impact: Approximately 5,000-6,000+ citations (reported ~5,225 on one index; higher on others).

papers.citation: Lopez, R., Regier, J., Cole, M.B., Jordan, M.I. & Yosef, N. (2018). Deep generative modeling for single-cell transcriptomics. Nature Methods 15(12), 1053-1058. DOI:10.1038/s41592-018-0229-2.
papers.key_contribution: scVI: a conditional variational autoencoder for raw UMI counts. Generative model: latent z_n ~ Normal(0,I), library size l_n ~ LogNormal, batch s_n fed as a covariate to the decoder; observations x_ng ~ Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) with neural-net-parameterized mean, dispersion and dropout. Trained by amortized variational inference (encoder q(z|x,s), reparameterization, ELBO). Batch correction is achieved by conditioning the decoder on s so z is batch-invariant; z serves for clustering, visualization, and DE.
papers.relevance: This is the natural substrate for our information-geometric angle: scVI places cells on a parametric statistical manifold (the ZINB family), so the Fisher information metric of ZINB gives a principled, count-aware Riemannian distance between cells instead of Euclidean-on-PCA. Its posterior q(z|x) also gives the uncertainty we can calibrate with conformal prediction, and its scArches/scANVI extensions provide reference mapping for MODY query cells.
papers.quantitative_findings: ZINB likelihood with per-gene dispersion; scalable to hundreds of thousands of cells via stochastic optimization; matched or beat prior methods on batch mixing, clustering (ARI) and imputation.
papers.approx_impact: Extremely influential deep-learning baseline; on the order of 2,000-3,000+ citations, and the scvi-tools ecosystem is a de facto standard.

papers.citation: Ming, J. & Lin, Z. (2022). FIRM: Flexible integration of single-cell RNA-sequencing data for large-scale multi-tissue cell atlas datasets. Briefings in Bioinformatics 23(5), bbac167. DOI:10.1093/bib/bbac167. (bioRxiv 2020.06.02.129031.)
papers.key_contribution: A re-scaling (per-gene variance-standardization) algorithm that explicitly accounts for differing cell-type compositions across datasets before integration. By identifying shared cell types and rescaling expression so that comparable cell types have comparable variance, FIRM avoids the composition-driven overcorrection that mixes distinct types, and reports superior preservation of within-dataset structure.
papers.relevance: Directly targets the failure mode most dangerous for HNF1A MODY work: methods that assume similar cell-type proportions across batches over-mix and erase rare/imbalanced populations. FIRM's composition-aware rescaling is a concrete ingredient for a rare-population-preserving objective and a motivation for our topological/multiscale prior.
papers.quantitative_findings: Benchmarked across multiple tissues, platforms and batches (mouse/human atlas scale); reported better mixing of shared identities with less distortion of original structure than Harmony/Seurat/scVI-class methods on composition-mismatched tasks.
papers.approx_impact: Moderate but growing; on the order of a few hundred citations.

papers.citation: Luecken, M.D., Buttner, M., Chaichoompu, K., Danese, A., Interlandi, M., Mueller, M.F., Strobl, D.C., Zappia, L., ... Theis, F.J. (2022). Benchmarking atlas-level data integration in single-cell genomics. Nature Methods 19(1), 41-50. DOI:10.1038/s41592-021-01336-8.
papers.key_contribution: The scIB benchmark and Python package. Evaluated 68 method/preprocessing combinations on 85 batches / >1.2M cells across 13 tasks using 14 metrics, split into batch-removal (kBET, iLISI, graph connectivity, PC-regression on batch, batch-ASW) and biological-conservation (NMI and ARI vs labels, cell-type ASW, cLISI, isolated-label F1, isolated-label ASW, HVG conservation, trajectory conservation). Overall score = 0.4*batch-removal + 0.6*bio-conservation (min-max scaled). Found no universal winner: scANVI/scVI/scGen best for complex tasks, Harmony/Scanorama strong for simpler RNA tasks, and a fundamental batch-removal vs bio-conservation trade-off.
papers.relevance: This is our evaluation harness and the source of the exact rare-population metrics we should report. The isolated-label F1 and isolated-label ASW metrics score how well the single most under-represented (isolated) label is recovered post-integration -- a direct, standardized proxy for HNF1A-marked rare beta-cell subpopulation preservation. cLISI (cell-type local inverse Simpson index) quantifies local purity. Its explicit 0.4/0.6 weighting formalizes the trade-off our conformal-confidence method aims to Pareto-dominate.
papers.quantitative_findings: 14 metrics; overall = 0.4*batch + 0.6*bio; LISI = inverse Simpson 1/sum_b p_b^2 over a Gaussian-kernel neighborhood (iLISI over batches -> want high, cLISI over cell types -> want ~1); kBET = rejection rate of a chi-squared test comparing local vs global batch composition.
papers.approx_impact: Rapidly cited community-standard benchmark; on the order of 1,500+ citations and growing fast.

papers.citation: Tran, H.T.N., Ang, K.S., Chevrier, M., Zhang, X., Lee, N.Y.S., Goh, M. & Chen, J. (2020). A benchmark of batch-effect correction methods for single-cell RNA sequencing data. Genome Biology 21, 12. DOI:10.1186/s13059-019-1850-9.
papers.key_contribution: Independent benchmark of 14 batch-correction methods across five designed scenarios (identical cell types/different technologies; non-identical cell types; multiple batches; big data; simulated data), scored with kBET, LISI, ASW and ARI plus runtime/scalability. Identified Harmony, LIGER and Seurat 3 as consistently top performers, with Harmony fastest at scale, and highlighted method-dependent behavior on non-identical-composition scenarios.
papers.relevance: Provides the scenario taxonomy and metric quartet (kBET/LISI/ASW/ARI) we should reuse as baselines, and empirically documents that method ranking flips when cell-type composition differs across batches -- the regime that matters for rare MODY subpopulations. Complements scIB with an earlier, widely-cited, method-selection-oriented view.
papers.quantitative_findings: 14 methods x 5 scenarios; metrics kBET (batch mixing), LISI (iLISI/cLISI), ASW (silhouette on batch vs cell type), ARI (clustering vs truth).
papers.approx_impact: Approximately 987+ citations (per Genome Biology metrics).

result.synthesis:
The field has converged on a small set of algorithmic families, each defining integration in a different geometry. (1) Neighbor-graph / correction-vector methods -- MNN (Haghverdi 2018), Scanorama (Hie 2019) and Seurat v3 anchors (Stuart 2019) -- treat integration as finding reciprocal cross-batch matches (in raw cosine space, LSH-accelerated SVD space, or CCA space) and averaging local correction vectors with Gaussian kernels; they are inherently topological and local but linear within neighborhoods and reliant on the assumption that batch effects are orthogonal to, and smaller than, biological variation. (2) Iterative soft-clustering methods -- Harmony (Korsunsky 2019) -- alternate maximum-diversity soft k-means with ridge-regression correction, where the 'diversity' penalty is literally a cross-entropy/KL between per-cluster and global batch distributions; this is the most explicitly information-theoretic classical method and the closest conceptual neighbor to our proposed Fisher-Rao formulation. (3) Deep generative methods -- scVI (Lopez 2018) and its semi-supervised descendant scANVI, plus LIGER's iNMF (Welch 2019) -- learn a batch-conditioned latent space under an explicit count likelihood (ZINB for scVI; shared+dataset-specific metagenes W+V_i for LIGER), giving a probabilistic embedding and, in scVI's case, a genuine statistical manifold. (4) Composition-aware rescaling -- FIRM (Ming 2022) -- corrects the variance distortions that arise when cell-type proportions differ across batches.

Two large, independent benchmarks anchor the evidence base. Tran (2020) compared 14 methods with kBET/LISI/ASW/ARI and found Harmony, LIGER and Seurat 3 strongest, with Harmony fastest; Luecken/scIB (2022) scaled this to 68 combinations, >1.2M cells and 13 tasks with 14 metrics, formalized the central batch-removal-vs-bio-conservation trade-off as overall = 0.4*batch + 0.6*bio, and reported a task-dependent frontier (scANVI/scVI/scGen for complex tasks, Harmony/Scanorama for simpler RNA tasks). The critical downstream fact for our paper is that integration and clustering are not separable: every method reshapes the neighbor graph that Leiden/Louvain then partitions, so integration choice directly determines which clusters exist. LISI, kBET, ARI and NMI are all defined on post-integration neighborhoods or clusterings, making them simultaneously integration and clustering metrics.

The rare-population story is where the field is weakest and our stress test is sharpest. Aggressive batch mixing (high Harmony theta, over-eager MNN/CCA anchoring, or composition-blind rescaling) systematically absorbs small, imbalanced populations into larger neighbors -- 'overcorrection.' The community response has been (a) composition-aware methods like FIRM, (b) semi-supervised methods (scANVI, STACAS, scGen) that inject label priors to protect biology, and (c) targeted metrics: scIB's isolated-label F1 and isolated-label ASW specifically score recovery of the most under-represented label, and cLISI measures local cell-type purity. This is exactly the machinery needed to quantify preservation of HNF1A-marked rare beta-cell states in MODY islets, and it defines the axis on which an information-geometric, multiscale, topologically-aware method with calibrated per-cell confidence could demonstrate a clear advantage.
result.gaps_opportunities:
1) No calibrated confidence. None of Harmony/Seurat/Scanorama/MNN/scVI/FIRM emit finite-sample, distribution-free confidence on a cell's cluster or cell-type assignment. Anchor scores (Seurat) and scVI posteriors are uncalibrated heuristics. This is the open lane for conformal prediction: wrap a scANVI/scVI classifier (or our clustering) in split/Mondrian conformal to output prediction sets with guaranteed coverage, and use class-conditional (Mondrian) conformal so rare classes like HNF1A+ beta cells get valid coverage rather than being swamped by abundant classes.
2) Wrong geometry. Every mainstream method computes neighbors with Euclidean/cosine distance on PCA or a latent space, ignoring that counts live on a curved statistical manifold. There is no widely adopted method that builds the integration neighbor graph under the Fisher-Rao metric of the count model (ZINB/NB). This is the core information-geometric opening.
3) Single-scale integration. Harmony fixes one K; scVI/PCA fix latent dimensionality; anchors use one k. Rare populations appear and vanish across resolutions, yet no standard method integrates multiscale-consistently. A multiscale (persistent) formulation that couples resolutions is unaddressed.
4) Global metrics hide local harm. Overall scIB scores are dominated by abundant cell types; isolated-label metrics exist but are single-label and coarse. There is room for per-cell, topology-aware distortion diagnostics (e.g., persistence of a rare cluster across integration strength) that a conformal method can expose.
5) Overcorrection is still adversarial to rare disease biology. FIRM/STACAS mitigate but do not guarantee preservation. A method that provably bounds how much a rare population can be moved/merged -- via a topological constraint plus conformal validity -- would be novel and directly motivated by MODY.
result.methods_we_should_use:
Adopt and adapt, concretely: (1) Fisher-Rao replaces Euclidean. Fit scVI's ZINB (or NB) generative model, then compute cell-cell distances using the Fisher information metric of the fitted count family rather than L2 on PCs, so neighbor graphs respect the statistical manifold. This operationalizes the information-geometric claim and count-aware distances protect low-count rare cells.

(2) Reformulate Harmony's diversity term information-geometrically. Harmony minimizes sum_ik R_ik d(Z_i,Y_k) + sigma sum_ik R_ik log R_ik + sigma*theta sum_k KL(O_k || E_k), where O_k, E_k are per-cluster and global batch distributions. Keep this structure but (a) make d the Fisher-Rao distance, (b) replace the cross-entropy with a symmetric Jensen-Shannon / KL on the statistical manifold, and (c) turn theta and the soft-assignment temperature sigma into a multiscale schedule (a family of {K, theta, sigma} coupled across resolutions) instead of a single setting.

(3) Topology from MNN/Scanorama, preserved by persistence. Build the cross-batch graph via Scanorama-style all-pairs MNNs (LSH + randomized SVD for scale) and treat the resulting complex topologically; use persistent-homology-style tracking so a rare cluster that persists across integration strengths is protected, and penalize corrections that collapse persistent features (a principled anti-overcorrection constraint complementing FIRM's composition rescaling).

(4) Conformal confidence layer. Train scANVI (semi-supervised, ZINB, cell-type latent + classifier) as the base annotator, then apply split-conformal with Mondrian (class-conditional) calibration so each cell gets a prediction set with guaranteed marginal and per-class coverage; report set size as a calibrated uncertainty and flag ambiguous rare cells (HNF1A+ candidates) with valid coverage. Nonconformity score = 1 - softmax posterior, or a ZINB-likelihood-ratio score for a density-aware variant.

(5) Evaluation harness = scIB + Tran metrics, verbatim for comparability. Report batch-removal (kBET chi-squared rejection rate; iLISI = inverse Simpson 1/sum_b p_b^2 over a Gaussian-kernel neighborhood; graph connectivity; batch-ASW; PCR) and bio-conservation (NMI, ARI, cell-type ASW, cLISI ~ 1 ideal), and combine as overall = 0.4*batch + 0.6*bio. Make isolated-label F1 and isolated-label ASW, plus cLISI, the headline rare-population metrics for the HNF1A MODY beta-cell subpopulation, and additionally report conformal coverage and average prediction-set size stratified by cell-type frequency to show rare-population validity that existing methods cannot provide.

result.subtopic: Manifold learning and the critique of UMAP/t-SNE 2D embeddings in single-cell RNA-seq: distance distortion vs. neighborhood preservation, and the case for using the correct (information-geometric) geometry
result.synthesis:
The single-cell visualization stack rests on two nonlinear neighbor-embedding methods. t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008) builds Gaussian conditional affinities p_{j|i} whose per-point bandwidth sigma_i is calibrated by binary search to a target perplexity (Perp = 2^H(P_i)), symmetrizes them to p_{ij}, models the map with a heavy-tailed Student-t kernel q_{ij} ∝ (1+||y_i-y_j||^2)^{-1} to solve the "crowding problem," and minimizes KL(P||Q). UMAP (McInnes, Healy & Melville, 2018) reframes the same idea in Riemannian-geometry/algebraic-topology language: it builds a fuzzy simplicial set from a kNN graph with a local-connectivity normalization (per-point rho_i = distance to nearest neighbor, sigma_i solving sum_j exp(-(d_ij-rho_i)/sigma_i) = log2 k), fuzzy-unions the directed weights via the probabilistic t-conorm mu_ij = mu_{i|j}+mu_{j|i}-mu_{i|j}mu_{j|i}, uses a low-dim kernel (1+a||y||^{2b})^{-1}, and minimizes a fuzzy cross-entropy with negative-sampling SGD. Becht et al. (2019, Nat. Biotech.) transplanted UMAP into cytometry/scRNA-seq and made it the field default. Kobak & Berens (2019, Nat.
