Commun.) then established the practical protocol that makes these embeddings trustworthy for transcriptomics — PCA initialization (for global-structure reproducibility), learning rate eta = n/12, early exaggeration, and multi-scale kernels combining perplexities (e.g., 30 and n/100) — implicitly conceding that a single scale is inadequate.

A theoretical and critical literature then interrogated what these maps actually optimize and preserve. Damrich & Hamprecht (2021, NeurIPS) derived UMAP's *effective* loss in closed form and showed the negative-sampling implementation does NOT minimize the stated cross-entropy nor reproduce its own high-dimensional similarities; the realized attraction/repulsion balance is dataset-size dependent, which reframes UMAP as an (implicitly contrastive) method whose output geometry is partly an optimization artifact. Wang, Huang, Rudin & Shaposhnik (2021, JMLR) decomposed t-SNE/UMAP/TriMap into attractive vs. repulsive force terms, proved the local-vs-global structure preservation is governed by that balance, and introduced PaCMAP with three edge classes (neighbor, mid-near, further pairs) on a dynamic schedule to hold global structure first and refine local structure later. This "you cannot get both scales from one objective" result is precisely the multiscale gap our method targets. The critique peaked with Chari & Pachter (2023, PLoS Comput. Biol.): using kNN Jaccard dissimilarity, cell-type rank correlation, and the max/min pairwise-distance ratio D/d, they showed 2D embeddings distort distances 4-to-200-fold, drop neighbor rank correlation to <=0.4, and — via a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style argument — that preserving pairwise distances to 20% error for 10,000 cells needs >=1,842 dimensions; they dramatized this by fitting scRNA-seq into an "elephant" drawing with comparable scores and argued 2D maps are a "specious art."

Lause, Berens & Kobak (2024, PLoS Comput. Biol.) rebutted this directly: the critique's metrics (global pairwise-distance/Spearman correlation) are ones *every* 2D method fails by construction, so they cannot discriminate a good embedding from an elephant. Using neighborhood-preservation and class-preservation metrics (kNN recall, kNN classification accuracy) they showed t-SNE/UMAP recover local and mesoscale structure far above chance and are biologically informative. The unresolved core of the debate — distance preservation is impossible in 2D, but neighborhood/topology preservation is achievable and is what matters — is exactly the opening for a method that (a) works in the correct information geometry rather than a generic Euclidean embedding, and (b) reports calibrated confidence instead of an uncertified picture. PHATE (Moon et al., 2019, Nat. Biotech.) is the strongest existing move in direction (a): it builds an alpha-decay adaptive-bandwidth diffusion operator P = D^{-1}K, diffuses to a time t chosen by a von Neumann–entropy knee, and defines an *information-geometric* "potential distance" IP_t(i,j) = ||-log P^t_i + log P^t_j||_2 (Euclidean distance between log-transformed diffused distributions), embedding those via metric MDS. The −log transform turns diffusion probabilities into an informational (divergence-like) distance sensitive to low-probability, long-range transitions — encoding manifold geometry rather than raw coordinates, and beating t-SNE/UMAP/diffusion maps on denoised manifold preservation (DEMaP).

papers.citation: van der Maaten, L. & Hinton, G. (2008). Visualizing Data using t-SNE. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 9, 2579-2605.
papers.key_contribution: Introduced t-SNE: Gaussian conditional affinities p_{j|i} with per-point bandwidth calibrated to a target perplexity (Perp = 2^H(P_i)), a heavy-tailed Student-t low-dimensional kernel q_ij ∝ (1+||y_i-y_j||^2)^{-1} to fix the 'crowding problem,' and a KL(P||Q) objective with gradient dC/dy_i = 4 sum_j (p_ij - q_ij)(y_i-y_j)(1+||y_i-y_j||^2)^{-1}.
papers.relevance: The base neighbor-embedding formalism our method must beat and reference; its perplexity/entropy calibration is a direct antecedent of an information-theoretic, multiscale affinity design, and its KL/crowding analysis motivates why raw 2D distances are not metric.
papers.quantitative_findings: Student-t map resolves crowding; demonstrated superior cluster separation vs. Sammon mapping, Isomap, and LLE on MNIST/Olivetti/COIL-20. Perplexity typically 5-50.
papers.approx_impact: ~45,000+ citations (Google Scholar); among the most-cited machine-learning papers of all time.

papers.citation: McInnes, L., Healy, J. & Melville, J. (2018). UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction. arXiv:1802.03426 (with JOSS 3(29):861).
papers.key_contribution: Recast neighbor embedding in Riemannian-geometry/algebraic-topology terms: fuzzy simplicial set from a kNN graph with local-connectivity normalization (rho_i = nearest-neighbor distance; sigma_i solving sum_j exp(-(d_ij-rho_i)/sigma_i)=log2 k), fuzzy-union symmetrization via the probabilistic t-conorm, low-dim kernel (1+a||y||^{2b})^{-1}, and fuzzy cross-entropy minimized by negative-sampling SGD.
papers.relevance: Explicitly invokes 'the correct manifold geometry' (uniform density on a locally-connected Riemannian manifold) and topology (simplicial sets) — the same conceptual pillars as our information-geometric, topologically-aware method, but it discards higher homology and never certifies its topological claims, which is the gap we exploit.
papers.quantitative_findings: Competitive with t-SNE on visualization quality, better global-structure retention, and markedly faster runtime with no embedding-dimension cap; scales to millions of points.
papers.approx_impact: ~11,000 citations (Semantic Scholar); tens of thousands on Google Scholar; the de-facto default in single-cell pipelines (Scanpy/Seurat).

papers.citation: Kobak, D. & Berens, P. (2019). The art of using t-SNE for single-cell transcriptomics. Nature Communications, 10, 5416.
papers.key_contribution: Established the reproducible-embedding protocol for scRNA-seq: PCA initialization (for global structure and determinism), learning rate eta = n/12, early exaggeration, multi-scale similarity kernels combining perplexities (e.g., 30 and n/100), and downsampling/landmark initialization for very large datasets (via FIt-SNE).
papers.relevance: Its multi-scale kernel and PCA-init recipe is the strongest existing acknowledgment that a single perplexity/scale is inadequate — direct motivation for our principled multiscale (diffusion-time filtration) design, and the correct baseline protocol to benchmark against.
papers.quantitative_findings: Multi-scale kernels improve both local and global preservation on Tasic 2018 (~24k neurons) and 10x (~1.3M cells); eta=n/12 and PCA init reduce fragmentation and non-reproducibility.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,000+ citations (approximate); the standard best-practices reference for t-SNE in transcriptomics.

papers.citation: Wang, Y., Huang, H., Rudin, C. & Shaposhnik, Y. (2021). Understanding How Dimension Reduction Tools Work: An Empirical Approach to Deciphering t-SNE, UMAP, TriMap, and PaCMAP for Data Visualization. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 22(201), 1-73.
papers.key_contribution: Decomposed neighbor-embedding methods into explicit attractive and repulsive force terms, showed local-vs-global structure preservation is a direct function of that attraction/repulsion balance, and introduced PaCMAP with three edge classes (neighbor, mid-near, further pairs) on a dynamic weighting schedule that preserves global structure first, then local.
papers.relevance: Provides the theoretical basis for our multiscale thesis: no single objective preserves all scales, so a scheduled/filtered multi-scale construction (mid-near analog of intermediate diffusion times) is required — and gives the force-decomposition vocabulary to design and analyze our optimizer.
papers.quantitative_findings: PaCMAP matches or beats t-SNE/UMAP/TriMap on joint local (kNN accuracy) and global (triplet/centroid) metrics with robust hyperparameters and faster runtime; 'mid-near' pairs shown to be the mechanism for global-structure recovery.
papers.approx_impact: Several hundred citations (approximate); influential DR-analysis paper and the PaCMAP reference.

papers.citation: Damrich, S. & Hamprecht, F. A. (2021). On UMAP's True Loss Function. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (NeurIPS 2021). arXiv:2103.14608.
papers.key_contribution: Derived UMAP's effective loss in closed form and proved the negative-sampling implementation optimizes a DIFFERENT objective than the published fuzzy cross-entropy, in a dataset-size-dependent way; UMAP therefore does not reproduce its own theoretically motivated high-dimensional similarities, and the realized attraction/repulsion balance (hence the embedding geometry) is partly an artifact of the sampling scheme.
papers.relevance: Shows that the 'geometry' UMAP displays is not the geometry it claims — a foundational warning that a generic embedding's shape is optimizer-dependent and unfalsifiable. Motivates our commitment to an explicit, analyzable objective and to n-invariant, calibrated confidence rather than trusting the raw layout.
papers.quantitative_findings: Effective repulsion is attenuated by a factor tied to the negative-sampling rate and n; corrected/parametric UMAP recovers the intended similarities and connects UMAP to contrastive (Barlow-twins-like) learning.
papers.approx_impact: ~100+ citations (approximate); a key theory paper cited across the DR/contrastive-learning literature.

papers.citation: Chari, T. & Pachter, L. (2023). The specious art of single-cell genomics. PLOS Computational Biology, 19(8), e1011288. (bioRxiv 2021).
papers.key_contribution: Quantified 2D-embedding distortion with kNN Jaccard dissimilarity, cell-type neighbor rank correlation, and the max/min pairwise-distance ratio D/d, and argued via a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style bound that faithful distance preservation is impossible in 2D; dramatized by fitting scRNA-seq into an 'elephant' drawing with scores comparable to t-SNE/UMAP. Recommends task-specific reductions, ambient/high-D metric computation, hierarchical/graph views, and metric-preserving methods (e.g., MCML).
papers.relevance: The central critique our paper answers: it establishes that generic 2D embeddings distort the real geometry, motivating our 'use the correct geometry' stance. Its distortion metrics (D/d ratio, Jaccard, rank correlation) are exactly the distortion audits we should report to demonstrate improvement.
papers.quantitative_findings: kNN Jaccard dissimilarity vs. ambient space >0.7; cell-type rank correlation <=0.4 in PCA-preprocessed 2D embeddings; distance-ratio distortion 4-to-200-fold; ~1,842 dimensions needed to preserve pairwise distances within 20% error for 10,000 cells.
papers.approx_impact: ~300+ citations (approximate, preprint+journal); a high-visibility, controversial paper that reframed the field's use of embeddings.

papers.citation: Lause, J., Berens, P. & Kobak, D. (2024). The art of seeing the elephant in the room: 2D embeddings of single-cell data do make sense. PLOS Computational Biology, 20(9), e1012403. (bioRxiv 2024.03.26.586728).
papers.key_contribution: Rebutted Chari & Pachter by showing their global pairwise-distance/Spearman metrics are ones any 2D method fails by construction (and thus cannot distinguish a good embedding from the elephant); introduced neighborhood-preservation and class-preservation metrics (kNN recall, kNN classification accuracy) under which t-SNE/UMAP recover local and mesoscale structure far above chance and remain biologically meaningful.
papers.relevance: Crystallizes the field's live open question — distance preservation is impossible but neighborhood/topology preservation is achievable and is what matters — which is precisely the axis (neighborhood + topology + conformal confidence) on which our method should be evaluated and should win.
papers.quantitative_findings: t-SNE/UMAP achieve high kNN recall and class-preservation on standard scRNA-seq datasets, vastly exceeding the elephant construction and random baselines; distance-correlation metrics shown to be uninformative for ranking 2D methods.
papers.approx_impact: Growing citations (approximate; 2024); the definitive published counterpoint to the 'specious art' critique.

papers.citation: Moon, K. R., van Dijk, D., Wang, Z., Gigante, S., Burkhardt, D. B., Chen, W. S., ... Krishnaswamy, S. (2019). Visualizing structure and transitions in high-dimensional biological data. Nature Biotechnology, 37, 1482-1492.
papers.key_contribution: PHATE: builds an alpha-decay adaptive-bandwidth kernel K_ij = exp(-(d_ij/eps_i)^alpha), row-normalizes to a Markov diffusion operator P = D^{-1}K, diffuses to a time t chosen by a von Neumann–entropy knee, and defines an information-geometric 'potential (informational) distance' IP_t(i,j) = ||-log P^t_i + log P^t_j||_2, embedded by metric MDS. The -log transform converts diffusion probabilities into a divergence-like distance sensitive to low-probability long-range transitions.
papers.relevance: The closest existing embodiment of our thesis: it embeds the *information geometry* of the data manifold (a divergence between diffused distributions) rather than a generic Euclidean layout, and uses diffusion time as a scale parameter. Directly seeds our information-geometric distance and multiscale (diffusion-time) design; we extend it with topological certification and conformal confidence, and stress-test on subtle HNF1A MODY signal.
papers.quantitative_findings: Outperforms t-SNE, UMAP, diffusion maps, Isomap, PCA on DEMaP (denoised manifold preservation of geodesic distances) across simulated and real datasets; resolves continuous progressions, branches, and clusters simultaneously.
papers.approx_impact: ~1,500+ citations (approximate, Google Scholar); the reference information-geometric/diffusion embedding for single-cell trajectories.

result.gaps_opportunities: 1) The distance-vs-neighborhood debate is unresolved because it lacks calibrated uncertainty. Chari & Pachter say 2D distorts; Lause et al. say neighborhoods survive — but neither attaches finite-sample coverage guarantees to a cluster assignment or a neighbor set. No published scRNA-seq embedding/clustering method reports distribution-free confidence on 'is this cell really in this cluster / is this neighbor real,' which is exactly what conformal prediction provides and what a clinical MODY subtyping decision needs. 2) Every method here is effectively single-scale: t-SNE fixes one perplexity, UMAP one k, PHATE one diffusion time t. Multiscale is currently ad hoc (Kobak & Berens hand-combine two perplexities; Wang/Rudin use mid-near pairs on a schedule). A principled scale-space / diffusion-time filtration with a persistence-across-scales criterion is missing. 3) Topology is invoked (UMAP's fuzzy simplicial sets) but never certified: no method reports the persistent homology of the embedding versus the ambient manifold, so topological faithfulness is asserted, not measured. Topologically-aware clustering that reports bottleneck/Wasserstein distance between ambient and embedded persistence diagrams is an open niche. 4) Information geometry is used descriptively (PHATE's -log P^t) but not grounded in the statistical manifold of the count noise: scRNA-seq is Poisson/negative-binomial count data, yet distances are computed in log-normalized Euclidean space, which is statistically incorrect. A proper Fisher-Rao/Hellinger treatment of the count likelihood is unexploited. 5) Evaluation metrics (DEMaP, kNN recall, Jaccard, D/d ratio) exist but are never combined into a single uncertainty-calibrated benchmark, and none has been stress-tested on a subtle monogenic phenotype like HNF1A MODY, where the effect size is small, cluster boundaries are clinically load-bearing, and false confidence is costly — an ideal, unclaimed proving ground for a confidence-aware method.
result.methods_we_should_use: Adopt as the information-geometric backbone: PHATE's construction — alpha-decay adaptive kernel K_ij = exp(-(d_ij/eps_i)^alpha), Markov normalization P = D^{-1}K, diffusion to power t, and the potential/informational distance IP_t = ||-log P^t_i + log P^t_j||_2 — but replace the single entropy-selected t with a diffusion-time scale-space {t_1<...<t_m} to build the multiscale layer, and select scales by von Neumann entropy of P^t (spectral-entropy knee) generalized to a persistence-of-structure-across-t criterion. Ground the base distance d_ij in the correct statistical manifold: use Fisher-Rao or Hellinger/Bhattacharyya distance under a Poisson/negative-binomial count model (or an Aitchison/CLR compositional metric), so pairwise dissimilarities are divergences on a statistical manifold rather than Euclidean distances on log-counts. Borrow t-SNE's entropy/perplexity calibration for per-point bandwidths and UMAP's local-connectivity normalization (rho_i, sigma_i with sum_j exp(-(d_ij-rho_i)/sigma_i)=log2 k) and fuzzy t-conorm symmetrization to make the multiscale graph well-conditioned; use Kobak & Berens defaults (PCA init, eta=n/12, early exaggeration) as baseline/warm-start. Add topological certification: compute Vietoris-Rips (or alpha) persistent homology H0/H1 on both the ambient information-geometric distance matrix and the embedding, and report bottleneck / p-Wasserstein distance between persistence diagrams as the topological-fidelity metric — and use persistence across the diffusion-time filtration to define topologically stable clusters (features that persist across scales). Layer conformal confidence on top: use split/inductive conformal prediction with a cluster-assignment nonconformity score (e.g., 1 - soft cluster-membership probability, or a diffusion-distance-to-centroid score) to emit prediction sets with distribution-free finite-sample coverage 1-alpha for each cell's cluster label; and use a Mondrian/class-conditional conformal variant so coverage holds per cell type (critical for the rare HNF1A MODY population). Analyze the optimizer with Damrich & Hamprecht's effective-loss method and Wang/Rudin's attraction-repulsion decomposition to keep our objective explicit and n-invariant. Report the full evaluation battery: PHATE's DEMaP (geodesic preservation), Lause et al.'s kNN recall and class-preservation as the primary axis, and Chari & Pachter's D/d distortion ratio + kNN Jaccard as distortion audits — plus conformal empirical coverage/efficiency curves, on the HNF1A MODY islet stress test.

result.subtopic: Topological data analysis (TDA) applied to single-cell and gene-expression data: Mapper, persistent homology, and the discrete-vs-continuous (cell type vs trajectory) problem, 2007-2026

papers.citation: Singh, G., Memoli, F., & Carlsson, G. (2007). Topological Methods for the Analysis of High Dimensional Data Sets and 3D Object Recognition. Eurographics Symposium on Point-Based Graphics (PBG 2007), pp. 91-100.
papers.key_contribution: Introduces the Mapper algorithm: choose a filter/lens function f: X -> R^d, cover the image with overlapping intervals, pull back and cluster within each preimage (partial clustering), and build the nerve of the resulting cover as a simplicial complex/graph summarizing the shape of the data.
papers.relevance: Direct ancestor of scTDA and of any 'topologically-aware clustering.' Our multiscale interpretable graph is a Mapper variant; the paper defines the knobs we re-engineer (resolution, gain). Its parameter-instability is the gap our conformal-confidence layer addresses.
papers.quantitative_findings: Two free parameters (resolution = #bins, gain = overlap %); nerve-complex output with no statistical stability guarantees; later shown to admit arbitrary topological changes under parameter perturbation.
papers.approx_impact: ~690 citations (Semantic Scholar); >1,200 on Google Scholar. Foundational.

papers.citation: Carlsson, G. (2009). Topology and Data. Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society, 46(2), 255-308.
papers.key_contribution: Founding manifesto of applied persistent homology: filtrations of Vietoris-Rips/Cech complexes, homology H_k and Betti numbers (beta_0 components, beta_1 loops, beta_2 voids), functoriality, and persistence (features persisting across scale are signal).
papers.relevance: Theoretical foundation for our persistent-homology component: discrete cell type = persistent beta_0 (separated components); continuous trajectory = single connected component possibly carrying persistent beta_1 loops/branches.
papers.quantitative_findings: Establishes persistence, stability and functoriality; codifies Betti numbers and the persistence diagram as target summaries.
papers.approx_impact: 2,643 citations (Semantic Scholar). Among the most-cited TDA references.

papers.citation: Nicolau, M., Levine, A. J., & Carlsson, G. (2011). Topology based data analysis identifies a subgroup of breast cancers with a unique mutational profile and excellent survival. PNAS, 108(17), 7265-7270.
papers.key_contribution: Progression Analysis of Disease (PAD): combines Disease-Specific Genomic Analysis (DSGA, a healthy-state residual transform) with Mapper to reveal shape invisible to clustering, discovering a c-MYB-high, ER-positive breast-cancer subgroup with excellent survival.
papers.relevance: Canonical proof that topology isolates rare, clinically meaningful subpopulations flat clustering erases -- the template for our HNF1A-MODY stress test. DSGA (contrast against a healthy reference manifold) is directly reusable for MODY-vs-normal islets.
papers.quantitative_findings: Subgroup with markedly superior survival; DSGA decomposes expression into normal + disease residual components fed to Mapper.
papers.approx_impact: 599 citations (Semantic Scholar). Landmark disease-subtyping application of TDA.

papers.citation: Rizvi, A. H., Camara, P. G., Kandror, E. K., Roberts, T. J., Schieren, I., Maniatis, T., & Rabadan, R. (2017). Single-cell topological RNA-seq analysis reveals insights into cellular differentiation and development. Nature Biotechnology, 35(6), 551-560.
papers.key_contribution: scTDA -- first Mapper-based, model-independent, unsupervised framework for scRNA-seq time courses. Resolves four transient states (pluripotent, precursor, progenitor, differentiated), orders transcriptional programs in pseudotime, and supplies a topological gene-significance statistic.
papers.relevance: THE direct predecessor; we generalize on three axes: (1) replace 1-Pearson with an information-geometric metric, (2) replace single-resolution Mapper with multiscale persistent homology, (3) add calibrated conformal confidence. Its gene score (Moran's-I-style assortativity on the Mapper adjacency) is worth re-implementing.
papers.quantitative_findings: Metric = 1 - Pearson; filter = 2D MDS; cover = 26x26 (and 62x62) rectangular patches at 66% overlap; top ~5,000/4,600 high-variance genes. Gene score s_i = (N/(N-1)) * [sum_ab e_{i,a} A_{ab} e_{i,b}] / (sum_g e_{i,g})^2 on adjacency A, significance by 5,000-permutation null and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.
papers.approx_impact: 270 citations (Semantic Scholar). Anchor paper of the subfield.

papers.citation: Wolf, F. A., Hamey, F. K., Plass, M., Solana, J., Dahlin, J. S., Gottgens, B., Rajewsky, N., Simon, L., & Theis, F. J. (2019). PAGA: graph abstraction reconciles clustering with trajectory inference through a topology preserving map of single cells. Genome Biology, 20(1), 59.
papers.key_contribution: Partition-based graph abstraction: partitions the kNN graph (Louvain/Leiden), then scores partition connectivity (observed vs expected inter-partition edges under a random-edge null) to yield a coarse-grained, topology-preserving graph unifying discrete clustering with continuous trajectory inference at multiple resolutions.
papers.relevance: The most widely used topology-aware single-cell tool and our primary comparator -- it explicitly targets the discrete-vs-continuous reconciliation we address, but with a heuristic connectivity threshold, no persistent-homology backbone, and no per-cell confidence.
papers.quantitative_findings: Connectivity from a hypergeometric-style expected-edge model with a significance threshold; ships in Scanpy; scales to millions of cells; demonstrated on hematopoiesis and whole-organism atlases.
papers.approx_impact: 1,497 citations (Semantic Scholar). Very high impact; de facto standard.

papers.citation: Govek, K. W., Yamajala, V. S., & Camara, P. G. (2019). Clustering-independent analysis of genomic data using spectral simplicial theory. PLOS Computational Biology, 15(11), e1007509. (R package: RayleighSelection.)
papers.key_contribution: Generalizes the Laplacian score for feature selection from graphs to simplicial complexes via the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian L_k = D_k^T D_k + D_{k+1} D_{k+1}^T. Ranks genes by a generalized Rayleigh quotient measuring consistency with data topology, without clustering, with permutation p-values.
papers.relevance: From the Camara lab (scTDA lineage) and the most information-geometry-adjacent method here: Hodge/spectral theory is the discrete Laplace-Beltrami operator on the data manifold. Selects HNF1A-associated genes directly on the Mapper/simplicial complex without predefining clusters.
papers.quantitative_findings: Score R(f) = f^T L f / f^T f; combinatorial Laplacian on 0-,1-,2-simplices; significance by label permutation; recovers topologically localized genes missed by variance-based selection.
papers.approx_impact: 37 citations (Semantic Scholar). Niche but technically pivotal for feature selection on complexes.

papers.citation: Benjamin, K., Bhandari, A., Kepple, J. D., et al., Byrne, H. M., & Harrington, H. A. (2024). Multiscale topology classifies cells in subcellular spatial transcriptomics. Nature, 630, 943-949. (Introduces TopACT; uses multiparameter persistent homology landscapes, Vipond et al. 2021, PNAS.)
papers.key_contribution: TopACT classifies cells at subcellular resolution without predefined boundaries, then pairs local classification with multiparameter persistent homology (MPH) landscapes to detect multiscale spatial organization; predicts and validates (immunofluorescence) a peripheral immune-cell ring in kidney glomeruli in a lupus-nephritis model.
papers.relevance: Current flagship showing multiscale + multiparameter topology recovers biologically real, validatable structure in transcriptomics -- validating our thesis that multiscale topology beats single-scale clustering. We adapt MPH landscapes as vectorized multiscale features and TopACT's local-then-global design.
papers.quantitative_findings: MPH landscapes (Vipond et al. 2021, PNAS, ~95 citations) over a bifiltration (spatial scale x density/abundance); outperforms one-parameter persistence and classical spatial statistics on agent-based-model benchmarks; ring validated by immunofluorescence.
papers.approx_impact: 76 citations (Semantic Scholar) in ~2 years; Nature 2024. Fast-rising, high-impact.

papers.citation: Lim, H. S., & Qiu, P. (2024). Quantifying the clusterness and trajectoriness of single-cell RNA-seq data. PLOS Computational Biology, 20(3), e1011866.
papers.key_contribution: Defines five geometric/topological scores for whether data is discrete clusters or a continuous trajectory: (1) entropy of the 0-dim persistent-homology merge-threshold distribution, (2) entropy of the pairwise diffusion-pseudotime distance histogram, (3) sequential vector magnitude across cluster centers, (4) AUC of a high-dimensional Ripley's K contrast vs uniform points, (5) degrees-of-connectivity vs neighborhood size. Projects 12,000 simulated datasets to a 2D UMAP landscape onto which real data are mapped.
papers.relevance: Most on-point paper for our central discrete-vs-continuous question, and it uses 0-dim persistent homology (birth/death of components) to score it -- directly informing the MODY question of whether the HNF1A subpopulation is a discrete type or a continuum. Its limit is our opportunity: it scores globally, does not cluster, and gives no per-cell confidence.
papers.quantitative_findings: Five scores; 0-dim PH merge-threshold entropy is low for clustered / high for trajectory data; 12,000 simulated datasets projected to a 2D landscape; validated on multiple real scRNA-seq sets.
papers.approx_impact: 7 citations (Semantic Scholar); recent (2024) but the most direct precedent for a discrete/continuous decision.

result.synthesis:
The field splits into three waves. The foundational wave (2007-2011) established the two engines every downstream method reuses: Mapper (Singh, Memoli & Carlsson 2007), which turns a lens function plus an overlapping cover into an interpretable simplicial/graph summary, and persistent homology (codified in Carlsson 2009), which tracks Betti numbers beta_0 (components), beta_1 (loops) and beta_2 (voids) across a filtration to separate persistent signal from noise. Nicolau, Levine & Carlsson (2011) proved on breast cancer that this shape-first view isolates rare, clinically meaningful subgroups that flat clustering erases -- the conceptual license for topology-based disease subtyping. The single-cell wave (2017-2019) ported these to transcriptomics: Rizvi et al. (2017, scTDA) built the canonical Mapper pipeline for scRNA-seq (1-Pearson metric, 2D MDS lens, overlapping rectangular cover, an assortativity-style topological gene score with permutation/BH FDR); Wolf et al. (2019, PAGA) delivered the field's workhorse for reconciling discrete clusters with continuous trajectories via partition connectivity; and Govek, Yamajala & Camara (2019) generalized spectral feature selection to simplicial complexes via the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian, enabling clustering-independent gene ranking. The current wave (2021-2026) is defined by multiscale and multiparameter methods: multiparameter persistent homology landscapes (Vipond et al. 2021) and their flagship application TopACT (Benjamin et al. 2024, Nature) show that bifiltrations over scale and density recover spatially organized, experimentally validatable structure; scGeom (Huynh & Cang 2024, Briefings in Bioinformatics) fuses Ollivier-Ricci graph curvature with local/relative persistent homology and persistence entropy to flag transition cells; and Lim & Qiu (2024) use 0-dimensional persistence explicitly to score the discrete-vs-continuous character of a dataset.

Read together, the field moves from single-scale, single-parameter, heuristic summaries toward multiscale, multiparameter, statistically-grounded ones -- but the statistical grounding is still shallow. scTDA and RayleighSelection give permutation p-values for gene significance, and PAGA gives a connectivity heuristic, yet none produces a calibrated confidence statement about whether a given cell belongs to a given cluster or type. Mapper's instability to lens, resolution and gain (recently shown to induce arbitrary topological changes under mild perturbation) remains unmitigated in single cell, and the metric on cell space is almost always Euclidean-on-PCA, correlation, or diffusion distance -- none of which respects the count/compositional, heteroscedastic nature of scRNA-seq.

For an information-geometric, multiscale, topologically-aware clustering method with conformal confidence, the state of the art therefore leaves three clean openings that no existing paper occupies simultaneously: (i) an information-geometric metric (Fisher-Rao/Hellinger) as the input geometry to both the filtration and the Mapper lens; (ii) a unified multiscale treatment that reads discreteness off persistent beta_0 and continuity/branchiness off beta_1 instead of fixing one resolution; and (iii) distribution-free, finite-sample confidence via conformal prediction on the topological features. The HNF1A-MODY islet stress test is well chosen: like the Nicolau breast-cancer study it is a rare-subpopulation detection problem, and we found no prior TDA study of MODY or HNF1A perturbation in pancreatic islets.
result.gaps_opportunities:
1. No information-geometric metric. Every single-cell TDA method (scTDA's 1-Pearson, PAGA's Euclidean-on-PCA kNN, scGeom's Euclidean PCA distance, Lim & Qiu's diffusion distance) uses a metric that ignores that scRNA-seq is count/compositional data with mean-variance coupling. Opportunity: use the Fisher-Rao / Hellinger geodesic as the base metric feeding both the Vietoris-Rips filtration and the Mapper lens -- literally the information-geometric novelty, done by no prior TDA-scRNA paper.

2. No calibrated uncertainty. scTDA and RayleighSelection give permutation p-values for GENE significance; PAGA gives a connectivity heuristic; TopACT gives a classification -- but nothing gives a per-cell, finite-sample, distribution-free confidence set for the cluster/type assignment itself. Conformal prediction is absent from the entire subfield. This is the paper's clearest white space.

3. Multiscale is fragmented. Persistent homology is scale-free but usually collapsed to scalar summaries; Mapper fixes one resolution/gain; multiparameter PH (Vipond, Benjamin) handles two parameters but is computationally heavy and applied only to SPATIAL transcriptomics, not full-transcriptome dissociated scRNA-seq. Opportunity: a genuinely multiscale clustering that keeps the persistence structure (Betti curves / landscapes across scale) instead of collapsing it, on expression manifolds.

4. Discrete-vs-continuous is scored but not acted on. Lim & Qiu (2024) quantify clusterness/trajectoriness globally but do not cluster; PAGA reconciles them heuristically. Opportunity: a method that decides per-region whether structure is discrete (persistent beta_0-dominated) or continuous (single component with persistent beta_1/branch loops) and adapts the clustering accordingly -- directly the MODY question (discrete HNF1A+ subtype vs a continuum of islet states).

5. Mapper instability is unaddressed in single cell. Known arbitrary topological changes under lens/cover perturbation. Opportunity: distribution-guided / statistically-selected covers plus a stability certificate (bottleneck-distance bounds), which pairs naturally with the conformal layer.

6. Rare monogenic perturbations are unexplored by TDA. PAD (Nicolau) and TopACT show topology finds rare biologically real subgroups, yet there is no TDA study of MODY/HNF1A islets. This makes HNF1A-MODY a genuinely novel, well-motivated stress test rather than a re-run of an existing benchmark.
result.methods_we_should_use:
Adopt and cite these concrete constructs (equations named):

- Information-geometric base metric (headline). Square-root/Hellinger embedding of normalized expression p, q onto the positive orthant of the sphere: Fisher-Rao geodesic d_FR(p,q) = 2*arccos(sum_i sqrt(p_i*q_i)) (equivalently Hellinger H(p,q) = sqrt(1 - sum_i sqrt(p_i*q_i))). Use as distance in (a) the Vietoris-Rips filtration and (b) the Mapper lens, replacing scTDA's 1-Pearson. Optionally precede with a variance-stabilizing / Fisher-information-preserving transform (Anscombe/sqrt) to respect count noise.

- Persistent homology pipeline. Build VR_epsilon over d_FR; compute persistence diagrams Dgm_k = {(b_i,d_i)} for k=0,1; read discreteness from persistent beta_0 and continuity/branch loops from persistent beta_1. Vectorize with Bubenik persistence landscapes lambda_k(t) and/or persistence images for downstream ML. Quantify robustness with bottleneck distance d_B(X,Y)=inf_gamma sup_x ||x-gamma(x)||_inf and p-Wasserstein W_p, using the stability theorem to bound sensitivity. Summarize discreteness with persistence entropy E = -sum_i p_i*log p_i, p_i=(d_i-b_i)/L, L=sum_j(d_j-b_j) (as in scGeom / Lim & Qiu 0-dim entropy).

- Mapper with an information-geometric lens + distribution-guided cover. Lens = leading Fisher-Rao MDS or diffusion coordinates (or a codensity); overlapping cover with adaptively chosen resolution/gain (distribution-guided Mapper) to combat instability; output the nerve complex as the interpretable multiscale graph.

- Multiparameter persistence landscapes (Vipond et al. 2021; Benjamin et al. 2024). Bifiltration over (Fisher-Rao scale epsilon, local density/codensity) so the summary is robust to sampling density and captures multiscale organization; convert to MPH-landscape feature vectors for clustering/classification.

- Combinatorial Hodge Laplacian feature selection (Govek et al. 2019). On the Mapper/simplicial complex, score each gene f by the generalized Rayleigh quotient R(f)=f^T L_k f / f^T f with L_k = D_k^T D_k + D_{k+1} D_{k+1}^T; permutation null for p-values. Use to pull out HNF1A-associated genes without predefining clusters.

- Ollivier-Ricci curvature for transition detection (Huynh & Cang 2024). On the kNN graph, kappa(x,y) = 1 - W_1(m_x,m_y)/d(x,y) with m_x a lazy-random-walk measure; negative curvature marks bridge/transition cells, positive marks within-community cores -- a complementary signal for the discrete/continuous decision.

- scTDA topological gene score (Rizvi et al. 2017). Reuse s_i = (N/(N-1)) * [sum_{a,b} e_{i,a} A_{ab} e_{i,b}] / (sum_g e_{i,g})^2 on the Mapper adjacency A (Moran's-I-type assortativity), with 5,000-permutation null and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR, to map the HNF1A gene network onto the topology.

- Conformal confidence layer (our novelty; absent from this literature). Wrap the clustering in split/inductive conformal prediction, preferably Mondrian (class-conditional) conformal so coverage holds per cell type. Nonconformity score from the topological representation (Fisher-Rao distance to the assigned Mapper node centroid, or a persistence-landscape kNN score); calibrate on a held-out fold to emit per-cell prediction SETS with guaranteed marginal (and, under Mondrian, class-conditional) coverage 1-alpha. This yields calibrated 'this cell is HNF1A-MODY-like with confidence >= 1-alpha' statements and honest abstention on ambiguous transitional cells -- the capability the subfield currently lacks.

result.subtopic: Pancreatic islet single-cell biology and monogenic diabetes (HNF1A/MODY): cell-type atlases, ground-truth markers, beta-cell heterogeneity/dedifferentiation, and transcription-factor-driven aberrant states — establishing the biological benchmark for information-geometric, multiscale, topologically-aware clustering with conformal confidence.

papers.citation: Baron, M., Veres, A., Wolock, S.L., Faust, A.L., Gaujoux, R., Vetere, A., Ryu, J.H., Wagner, B.K., Shen-Orr, S.S., Klein, A.M., Melton, D.A., Yanai, I. (2016). A Single-Cell Transcriptomic Map of the Human and Mouse Pancreas Reveals Inter- and Intra-cell Population Structure. Cell Systems 3(4), 346-360.e4.
papers.key_contribution: First large droplet-based (inDrop) scRNA-seq atlas of pancreas; profiled >12,000 cells from 4 human donors and 2 mouse strains, resolving 15 clusters spanning all endocrine (alpha/beta/delta/gamma/epsilon), exocrine (acinar/ductal), vascular, Schwann, quiescent/activated stellate, and four immune cell types. Established the canonical ground-truth cell-type set for the field.
papers.relevance: Defines the reference cell-type partition and marker structure our clustering must recover, including rare epsilon cells and intra-beta heterogeneity (functional-maturation and ER-stress gradients) — precisely the graded structure our multiscale/topological method targets. Its raw count matrices are a standard benchmark.
papers.quantitative_findings: 12,000+ cells; 15 clusters; detected rare epsilon cells; identified beta-cell subpopulations differing in ER-stress (HSPA5, DDIT3) and maturation genes; validated ductal subtypes by IHC.
papers.approx_impact: Field-defining atlas, ~1,500-2,000 citations (approximate, Google Scholar order-of-magnitude); one of the most-cited pancreas scRNA-seq papers.

papers.citation: Muraro, M.J., Dharmadhikari, G., Grün, D., Groen, N., Dielen, T., Jansen, E., van Gurp, L., Engelse, M.A., Carlotti, F., de Koning, E.J.P., van Oudenaarden, A. (2016). A Single-Cell Transcriptome Atlas of the Human Pancreas. Cell Systems 3(4), 385-394.e3.
papers.key_contribution: CEL-Seq2 + FACS/robotics atlas of human pancreas; introduced/applied StemID, which builds a cell-cell distance from 1 - Pearson correlation, clusters, and infers lineage trees plus a StemID score combining number of links with transcriptome entropy to nominate multipotent/progenitor states.
papers.relevance: StemID's distance metric and entropy-based stemness score are a direct methodological precursor to our information-geometric distance and topological lineage inference. Its explicit graph-of-clusters (a Mapper-like construction) is the biological motivation for our topologically-aware branch/intermediate-state detection.
papers.quantitative_findings: Thousands of cells across donors; in-silico purified all main pancreatic cell types; StemID recovered known endocrine/exocrine types unbiasedly and ranked stemness via link count x entropy.
papers.approx_impact: Highly cited companion atlas, ~1,000+ citations (approximate); StemID/RaceID lineage remains a widely used framework.

papers.citation: Segerstölpe, Å., Palasantza, A., Eliasson, P., Andersson, E.-M., Andréasson, A.-C., Sun, X., Picelli, S., Sabirsh, A., Clausen, M., Bjursell, M.K., Smith, D.M., Kasper, M., Ämmälä, C., Sandberg, R. (2016). Single-Cell Transcriptome Profiling of Human Pancreatic Islets in Health and Type 2 Diabetes. Cell Metabolism 24(4), 593-607.
papers.key_contribution: Smart-seq2 full-length scRNA-seq of human islets in health vs T2D; defined cell-type-specific programs including rare delta/gamma/epsilon/stellate and alpha/beta/acinar subpopulations, and provided the canonical marker panel used across the field (data: ArrayExpress E-MTAB-5061).
papers.relevance: Supplies the explicit ground-truth marker genes we will use to score cluster purity and to define the beta-identity axis: alpha=GCG/LOXL4/DPP4/FAP, beta=INS/IAPP/ADCYAP1/PDX1/MAFA/NKX6-1, delta=SST/LEPR/GHSR, gamma=PPY, epsilon=GHRL, acinar=PRSS1, ductal=KRT19, endothelial=PECAM1/VWF/PLVAP, stellate=COL1A2, immune=CD74/HLA/tryptase. It is also a primary T2D case/control benchmark.
papers.quantitative_findings: 2,209 QC-passed cells from 10 donors (6 healthy, 4 T2D); t-SNE + clustering; identified delta-cell receptor repertoire (paracrine sensing) and cell-type-resolved T2D expression changes.
papers.approx_impact: Core reference atlas, ~1,500+ citations (approximate); the de facto marker/methods citation for human islet scRNA-seq.

papers.citation: Xin, Y., Kim, J., Okamoto, H., Ni, M., Wei, Y., Adler, C., Murphy, A.J., Yancopoulos, G.D., Lin, C., Gromada, J. (2016). RNA Sequencing of Single Human Islet Cells Reveals Type 2 Diabetes Genes. Cell Metabolism 24(4), 608-615.
papers.key_contribution: Fluidigm C1 scRNA-seq of purified human alpha/beta/delta/PP cells across non-diabetic and T2D donors; nominated 245 genes with altered expression in T2D, most previously unlinked to islet function.
papers.relevance: Completes the 2016 quartet of foundational islet atlases and provides an independent T2D differential-expression ground truth for cell-type-specific dysfunction, useful for cross-dataset validation and batch-integration stress tests.
papers.quantitative_findings: 1,492 endocrine cells; 245 T2D-dysregulated genes identified at cell-type resolution.
papers.approx_impact: Widely cited T2D islet atlas, ~500-800 citations (approximate).

papers.citation: Talchai, C., Xuan, S., Lin, H.V., Sussel, L., Accili, D. (2012). Pancreatic β Cell Dedifferentiation as a Mechanism of Diabetic β Cell Failure. Cell 150(6), 1223-1234.
papers.key_contribution: Landmark lineage-tracing study (beta-cell FoxO1 ablation) showing diabetic beta-cell loss is driven by dedifferentiation to progenitor-like states — not death — with re-expression of NEUROG3, OCT4, NANOG, L-MYC and partial conversion to alpha-cell fate (hyperglucagonemia).
papers.relevance: Establishes the biological reality of continuous, transcription-factor-controlled aberrant intermediate beta-cell states that hard single-resolution clustering cannot represent. This is the conceptual justification for our topologically-aware, multiscale, uncertainty-quantified approach and directly motivates the HNF1A/MODY stress test.
papers.quantitative_findings: FoxO1-null beta cells dedifferentiate under multiparity/aging stress; reversion to Ngn3+ progenitor-like and alpha-cell fates; introduced 'forbidden/disallowed' and progenitor markers (e.g., ALDH1A3 in later work) as dedifferentiation signatures.
papers.approx_impact: Landmark paper, ~1,800+ citations (approximate); founded the beta-cell dedifferentiation paradigm.

papers.citation: Weng, C., Yi, F., et al. (Gu/Li labs) (2023). Single cell multiomic analysis reveals diabetes-associated β-cell heterogeneity driven by HNF1A. Nature Communications 14, 5400.
papers.key_contribution: Integrated scRNA-seq + snATAC-seq + cell-type-specific 3D genome (Hi-C) of human islets; identified HNF1A as the principal driver of intra-donor beta-cell heterogeneity, with reduced HNF1A activity in T2D beta cells and a dedicated computational method to decompose intra- vs inter-donor heterogeneity.
papers.relevance: The single most directly relevant modern paper for our HNF1A/MODY stress test: it operationalizes HNF1A-driven beta-cell state as a continuous, multiomic axis and links HNF1A activity to a functional target (FXYD2 -> Na+ current). Its heterogeneity-decomposition and HNF1A target gene set (FXYD2, SLC2A2, PDX1) are ready-made features and validation targets for our method.
papers.quantitative_findings: HNF1A activity correlated with lower single-beta-cell Na+ currents; FXYD2 nominated as principal HNF1A-target mitigator; HNF1A expression reduced in T2D beta cells; intra-donor heterogeneity explicitly modeled.
papers.approx_impact: Rapidly cited multiomic study, ~50-150 citations (approximate, recent).

papers.citation: Fasolino, M., Schwartz, G.W., Golson, M.L., et al. (Vahedi/Kaestner, HPAP) (2022). Single-cell multi-omics analysis of human pancreatic islets reveals novel cellular states in type 1 diabetes. Nature Metabolism 4(2), 284-299.
papers.key_contribution: HPAP multimodal atlas (scRNA-seq, CyTOF, imaging mass cytometry) of ~80,000 cells / ~7M CyTOF / ~1M IMC cells across non-diabetic, autoantibody+, and T1D donors; discovered novel disease-associated states, e.g., ductal cells acquiring a tolerogenic-dendritic-cell-like signature.
papers.relevance: Demonstrates that disease creates non-canonical intermediate/aberrant cell states beyond the classic type set — the exact regime where fixed-resolution clustering fails and calibrated confidence is needed. HPAP/PANC-DB provides the large, harmonized multi-donor benchmark (with matched physiology) for our conformal-coverage evaluation.
papers.quantitative_findings: 24 donors; ~80,000 scRNA-seq cells; multimodal cross-validation of a tolerogenic ductal state in T1D; spatially resolved via IMC.
papers.approx_impact: Influential HPAP flagship, ~200+ citations (approximate).

papers.citation: Gaulton/Sander/Preissl-lab consortium (2025-2026). Single-cell decoding of human islet cell type-specific alterations in type 2 diabetes reveals converging genetic- and state-driven β-cell gene expression defects. bioRxiv 2025.01.17.633590; published in The EMBO Journal (2026), doi:10.1038/s44318-026-00744-w.
papers.key_contribution: Deep, large-cohort human islet scRNA-seq spanning non-diabetic, pre-diabetic, and T2D; resolves 14 cell types in every donor/glycemic state and shows genetic (eQTL/GWAS) and cell-state effects converge on a small beta-cell gene set, quantifying loss of 'high-function' and gain of 'senescent' beta-cell subpopulations.
papers.relevance: The current state-of-the-art large T2D atlas and the strongest modern benchmark for cell-type-resolved dysfunction with a continuum of beta-cell functional states (high-function <-> senescent). It defines the exact multiscale beta-cell substructure and the ~25-30% functional-mass-loss phenotype our method should recover and confidently label.
papers.quantitative_findings: 245,878 cells from 48 donors; 14 cell types; 92 beta-cell DEGs / 58 convergent candidate causal beta-cell genes; ~25-30% loss of functional beta-cell mass in T2D; reciprocal high-function-to-senescent shift.
papers.approx_impact: Newest major atlas (preprint -> EMBO J); few citations to date but high expected impact.

result.synthesis:
The single-cell picture of the human islet was established in 2016 by four near-simultaneous atlases: Baron et al. (droplet/inDrop, >12,000 cells), Muraro et al. (CEL-Seq2 + StemID lineage inference), Segerstölpe et al. (Smart-seq2, health vs T2D, the canonical marker source), and Xin et al. (Fluidigm C1, 245 T2D genes). Together they fixed the field's ground-truth taxonomy — endocrine alpha/beta/delta/gamma(PP)/epsilon plus acinar, ductal, endothelial, activated/quiescent stellate, and immune cells — and the marker panel that still serves as label supervision: INS/IAPP/ADCYAP1 (beta), GCG (alpha), SST (delta), PPY (gamma), GHRL (epsilon), PRSS1/CPA1/CELA3A (acinar), KRT19/CFTR (ductal), PECAM1/VWF/PLVAP (endothelial), COL1A2/PDGFRB/RGS5 (stellate), and PTPRC/CD68/tryptase (immune). Crucially, all four already reported intra-beta and intra-alpha heterogeneity (maturation and ER-stress gradients), signaling that the biology is not cleanly discrete — it is a set of manifolds with continuous internal structure and rare, hard-to-separate populations (epsilon, delta, stellate). This is the substrate our information-geometric, multiscale method is built for.

The second, disease-oriented layer reframes beta cells as a dynamic identity continuum rather than a fixed cluster. Talchai & Accili (2012) showed with lineage tracing that diabetic beta-cell 'loss' is largely dedifferentiation toward NEUROG3+/OCT4+/NANOG+ progenitor-like and even alpha-like fates, marked by de-repression of 'disallowed/forbidden' genes (e.g., ALDH1A3, LDHA, SLC16A1). Modern single-cell/multiomic work operationalizes this as transcription-factor-driven state axes: Weng et al. (2023, Nat Commun) pinpoint HNF1A — the MODY3 gene — as the principal driver of intra-donor beta-cell heterogeneity, reduced in T2D, acting through targets such as FXYD2 (Na+ handling), SLC2A2, and PDX1; the HPAP flagship (Fasolino et al. 2022, Nat Metab) uncovers entirely novel disease states (e.g., tolerogenic ductal cells in T1D); and the newest large atlas (Gaulton/Sander/Preissl consortium, 245,878 cells/48 donors, 2025-26) shows genetic and state-driven defects converge on ~58 beta-cell genes with a graded high-function-to-senescent shift and ~25-30% functional-mass loss in T2D. For HNF1A/MODY specifically, complementary functional-genomics studies in primary human islets/pseudoislets (Haliyur et al. 2019 JCI; González-Vélez/Kaestner-lab 2022-2024) show HNF1A loss dysregulates >100 targets (SLC2A2, PDX1, TMEM27, DPP4) and triggers ER stress (PERK/eIF2a/ATF4, CHOP/DDIT3, BiP/HSPA5).

The methodological throughline is that the biology is compositional count data living on curved manifolds with multiscale, branching, and aberrant-intermediate structure — yet the standard pipeline (log-normalize, PCA in Euclidean space, single-resolution Louvain/Leiden, hard labels) assumes none of that. The islet is arguably the ideal stress test precisely because it combines a well-agreed discrete backbone (the marker taxonomy, giving clean external validation via ARI/NMI) with continuous, TF-driven state axes (HNF1A/MODY, dedifferentiation, senescence) that hard clustering systematically misassigns. Existing resources (E-MTAB-5061, HPAP/PANC-DB, the 2025 deep atlas) provide the labeled, multi-donor, case/control data needed to benchmark both cluster recovery and calibrated confidence.
result.gaps_opportunities: 1) Geometry mismatch: every foundational pipeline imposes Euclidean distance (PCA on log-normalized counts) or ad hoc 1-Pearson (StemID) on data that are Poisson/negative-binomial counts on the probability simplex. No islet study uses the natural Fisher-Rao/information geometry of the count model — an open lane for our information-geometric distance. 2) Single-scale clustering: Leiden/Louvain at one resolution cannot simultaneously resolve the discrete cell-type backbone AND the within-beta continuum (maturation, ER-stress, HNF1A-activity, high-function-to-senescent). A principled multiscale operator (diffusion powers with an entropy-selected scale) is missing. 3) No topology-aware treatment of intermediate/aberrant states: dedifferentiation and alpha<->beta transitions are branch/loop structures that clustering flattens; persistent homology / Mapper are essentially unused as first-class clustering machinery in islet biology. 4) No calibrated uncertainty: assignments are hard labels with no coverage guarantee, which is dangerous exactly for rare types (epsilon, delta, stellate) and for ambiguous HNF1A-perturbed/dedifferentiating cells — conformal prediction sets are absent from this literature. 5) MODY3/HNF1A data scarcity: most HNF1A single-cell evidence is overexpression/knockdown in pseudoislets (Weng 2023, Kaestner-lab), not primary MODY3-donor islets; a method that flags aberrant intermediate states with confidence could extract maximal signal from these limited perturbation datasets and from in-vitro MODY models. 6) No benchmark that jointly scores recovery of (a) rare types, (b) continuous state axes, and (c) calibrated confidence — our HNF1A stress test can define one.
result.methods_we_should_use:
Information-geometric distance: model each cell as a point on the multinomial simplex and use the Fisher-Rao metric. Via the square-root embedding p -> sqrt(p), Fisher-Rao becomes the round metric on the sphere, giving closed-form geodesic distance d(p,q)=2*arccos(sum_i sqrt(p_i q_i)) (arccos of the Bhattacharyya/Hellinger affinity BC=sum sqrt(p_i q_i)). The sqrt transform also variance-stabilizes Poisson counts, so it is both statistically and geometrically motivated — adopt this in place of Euclidean-on-logCPM. Consider negative-binomial/Sinkhorn-regularized alternatives for overdispersion.

Multiscale diffusion: build a symmetric affinity from the above distance (adaptive Gaussian/alpha-decay kernel as in PHATE), row-normalize to a Markov operator P (Coifman-Lafon diffusion maps), and study the family {P^t}. Select scale(s) by the spectral gap / von Neumann entropy S(P^t) = -sum lambda_i(t) log lambda_i(t) of the eigenvalues, or diffusion-pseudotime distance (Haghverdi 2016). This yields the cell-type backbone at large t and within-beta state axes at small t.

Topological awareness: (a) Mapper (Singh-Memoli-Carlsson) using diffusion-distance lenses to expose branches (dedifferentiation, alpha<->beta) as graph topology; (b) persistent homology on a Vietoris-Rips/alpha filtration of the geodesic distance to get persistence diagrams; quantify/compare topology across conditions (WT vs HNF1A-perturbed) with bottleneck and Wasserstein distances between diagrams, and use persistence to pick stable cluster numbers.

Conformal confidence: wrap the clusterer/classifier in split or Mondrian (class-conditional) conformal prediction. With a nonconformity score s(x,y) (e.g., 1 - softmax prob, or negative geodesic-kNN margin), calibrate the quantile q_hat = ceil((n+1)(1-alpha))/n empirical quantile of calibration scores, and output prediction SETS {y : s(x,y) <= q_hat} guaranteeing marginal coverage >= 1-alpha. Class-conditional/Mondrian variants protect rare types (epsilon, delta); ambiguous HNF1A/dedifferentiating cells naturally get multi-label sets rather than forced assignments. Report empirical coverage and average set size.

Evaluation and ground truth: supervise/validate with the canonical marker panel above; score partitions against marker-defined labels using Adjusted Rand Index and Normalized Mutual Information, and rare-type recall. Anchor beta-cell state axes on curated gene programs — identity/maturation (PDX1, NKX6-1, MAFA, MAFB, UCN3, ERO1B, SLC2A2/GLUT), HNF1A-activity (FXYD2, SLC2A2, PDX1, TMEM27, DPP4 from Weng 2023), dedifferentiation/disallowed (ALDH1A3, LDHA, SLC16A1, NEUROG3), ER-stress (DDIT3/CHOP, HSPA5/BiP, ATF4), and senescence — using AUCell/module scores. Benchmark on E-MTAB-5061 (Segerstölpe), Baron/Muraro/Xin, HPAP/PANC-DB, and the 2025 deep 48-donor atlas, and stress-test on HNF1A-perturbation single-cell data (Weng 2023) as the MODY axis.

result.novelty_thesis: The negative-binomial VAE already carries a canonical statistical-manifold geometry — the Fisher-Rao pullback metric M(z)=J_f(z)^T I_NB(f(z)) J_f(z) that its own decoder Jacobian and count likelihood induce on the latent space — and our contribution is to make that single geometric object do all four inferential jobs at once: define the inter-cell distance, be scanned across scales by Markov stability, be probed for discrete-versus-continuous structure by persistent homology, and supply a geometry-native nonconformity score whose conformal prediction sets carry finite-sample, scale-nested coverage — converting scRNA-seq clustering from a stack of mutually inconsistent Euclidean heuristics into one coherent, uncertainty-calibrated inference on the manifold of the count model.
result.positioning: The delta is coherence-from-one-geometry plus two genuinely new algorithmic pieces, not any single component. vs scVI: scVI supplies the NB-VAE latent but then throws away the geometry its generative model induces, running kNN/Leiden under plain L2 on the posterior mean; we replace L2 with the Fisher-Rao pullback M(z)=J^T I_NB(f) J — the metric scVI implicitly defines but never uses. vs GAIA (Cai 2025, our most dangerous competitor): GAIA uses the closed-form multinomial Fisher-Rao (sqrt-embedding, d=2·arccos Σ√(p_i q_i)) directly in gene space, treating genes as independent, static, single-scale, no topology, no confidence; we use the *learned, correlation-aware, denoised* pullback on a generative latent and add multiscale + topology + conformal. We therefore cannot and must not claim to be first to bring information geometry to scRNA-seq — only first to (i) pull the Fisher-Rao metric back through an NB generative decoder at scale and (ii) unify it with multiscale community detection, a topological discrete/continuous test, and calibrated coverage. vs Arvanitidis 2022 (Pulling Back Information Geometry): the pullback M=J_f^T I(f) J_f framework already exists and explicitly names NB/Poisson/categorical decoders; our novelty is the first single-cell instantiation, the scalability machinery, and everything built on top — the metric itself is an instantiation, not a new framework, and reviewers will know this. vs single-resolution Leiden: we replace the hand-tuned resolution with Markov-stability scanning (full exp(-tL), resolution-limit-free CPM) and VI-plateau scale selection, reporting a hierarchy. vs standard Markov stability / PyGenStability: vanilla MS uses a Euclidean autocovariance on a Euclidean-built graph; we build the graph on the pullback metric and (stretch novelty) replace the autocovariance inner product with an information divergence between walker distributions and the stationary law on the simplex. vs scTDA / Rizvi 2017: scTDA uses 1-Pearson + Mapper with permutation p-values on *genes*; we use the pullback metric as the filtration base and turn persistent H0/H1 into a *per-region discreteness statistic that steers the clustering*, with conformal calibration on *cell labels*. vs López-De-Castro 2025 / scConform / Ding 2023: they wrap conformal around a fixed supervised softmax classifier; we define a geometry-native score (Fisher-Rao distance-to-centroid or NB neg-log-likelihood) and make our multiscale clusters themselves the Mondrian groups, giving nested per-scale conditional coverage plus a covariate-shift-weighted variant for case/control. vs PHATE/Multiscale PHATE/DPT: they use an information-geometric potential distance for *visualization* with heuristic scale selection and no calibrated confidence and no count-model Fisher metric.
precise_contributions: NB Fisher-Rao pullback metric for single cells, made computable at scale: instantiate M(z)=J_f(z)^T I_NB(f(z)) J_f(z) with the closed-form NB Fisher information (per-gene term 1/(mu+mu^2/theta)) and the scVI decoder Jacobian by reverse-mode autodiff; defeat the decoder non-identifiability of the induced geometry with a decoder-ensemble/Bayesian expected metric bar-M(z)=(1/K)Σ_k J_{g_k}^T I J_{g_k}; and make geodesic distances tractable for 10^5-10^6 cells via a kNN metric-graph with local-Riemannian edge lengths and landmark/Nystrom shortest-paths.

Information-geometric Markov stability: run multiscale community detection (full continuous-time stability r(t,H)=trace[H^T(Pi e^{-tL_rw}-pi pi^T)H], resolution-limit-free CPM optimizer, PyGenStability backbone) on the pullback-metric graph, and — as the load-bearing algorithmic novelty — replace the Euclidean autocovariance with a Fisher-Rao/alpha-divergence between each node's walker distribution P(t)_i and the stationary pi on the probability simplex, with scales selected by Variation-of-Information plateaus rather than one hand-set resolution.

A formal, calibrated discrete-versus-continuous test coupled to scale: build a DTM/Vietoris-Rips filtration on the pullback distance, read discreteness off persistent H0 and continuity/branch-loops off persistent H1, attach Fasy-style bootstrap significance to separate topological signal from noise, and use the resulting per-region statistic to decide locally whether to partition (discrete type) or preserve a continuum (e.g., the HNF1A dedifferentiation gradient) rather than forcing a global resolution.

Geometry-native, scale-nested conformal confidence: split/Mondrian conformal with a nonconformity score taken directly from the manifold (Fisher-Rao distance-to-cluster-centroid or negative NB log-likelihood under the cluster's fitted model) rather than a generic softmax; the multiscale clusters serve as the Mondrian partition, yielding a nested sequence of conditional-coverage guarantees across scales; plus a covariate-shift-weighted (Tibshirani) variant for healthy->MODY calibration and an autoencoder-reconstruction conformal OOD flag for HNF1A-perturbed cells absent from a healthy reference.

Internal-consistency / unification result: demonstrate that distance, the stability functional, the filtration, and the nonconformity score are all derived from the *same* Fisher-Rao metric, so the pipeline is self-consistent (VI selection is information-theoretic, PH stability is bounded in the same metric, conformal scores are geodesic) — the argument that this is one inference, not four bolted-together tools.

The HNF1A-MODY islet benchmark as a new joint evaluation task that simultaneously scores rare-cell-type recovery (epsilon/delta/stellate and HNF1A-perturbed beta substates), continuous-state-axis fidelity (maturation, ER-stress, HNF1A-activity via FXYD2/SLC2A2/PDX1/TMEM27), and calibrated per-cell coverage — a rare-subpopulation + confidence stress test no existing single-cell benchmark provides.
result.technical_risks: Ten risks, ranked by how likely they are to sink the paper. (1) Decoder non-identifiability of the pullback geometry (Hauberg 2018; Syrota 2024): distances computed with a proper Riemannian metric are coordinate-invariant, but the induced geometry still depends on which of many equally-likely decoders is learned — the metric is a point estimate of an arbitrary object off the data support. If not marginalized (decoder ensemble / Bayesian expected metric), a geometry-side reviewer rejects on sight. (2) Unsupervised-calibration circularity: conformal needs labels for calibration; if we calibrate against our own cluster assignments the coverage guarantee is tautological. The only honest resolutions are calibrating against marker-derived reference labels (making the conformal layer semi-supervised annotation) or stating the guarantee precisely as coverage vs held-out reference labels — this must be nailed or a stats reviewer voids the entire confidence claim. (3) Exchangeability breaks exactly where it matters: HNF1A/MODY cells are, by hypothesis, distributionally shifted from a healthy calibration set, so marginal conformal coverage does not transfer to the target population; needs weighted/covariate-shift conformal, and even then the guarantee is only approximate. (4) The geometry may not beat cheap baselines: Ahlmann-Eltze & Huber 2023 show shifted-log+PCA matches sophisticated methods and Pearson residuals can *harm* rare-population recovery; Lause 2024 show UMAP/t-SNE neighborhoods are fine. Real danger the elaborate pullback yields no measurable, causally-attributable gain over scVI+Leiden or log1p+PCA+Leiden. (5) Two distinct Fisher-Rao objects are conflated in the reviews and must not be conflated in the paper: the closed-form multinomial FR in gene space (GAIA) vs the learned NB-VAE pullback in latent space — the paper must argue why the latter's dependence on a possibly-misspecified VAE is worth its cost. (6) Scalability compounding: per-cell D×d Jacobians (D~2000 genes), O(N^2) geodesics, full matrix-exponential exp(-tL) over a t-grid, and worst-case cubic persistent-homology reduction — each is expensive and they stack; atlas-scale (245k cells) may be infeasible without aggressive approximation whose error must be certified. (7) Cencov-uniqueness overclaim: Cencov's theorem certifies the *output family's* Fisher metric as uniquely invariant, but the decoder pullback is not a sufficient-statistic morphism, so invoking Cencov to call the whole pipeline 'the unique correct geometry' is false and will be caught. (8) MODY3 data scarcity: Weng 2023 is HNF1A over/under-expression in pseudoislets, not primary MODY3-donor islets; the headline biological claim may rest on perturbation proxies, not real disease tissue. (9) Hyperparameter explosion (VAE architecture/latent dim, kNN k, MS time grid, filtration scale, ensemble size K, conformal alpha, divergence family) creates overfitting and reproducibility exposure. (10) VAE misspecification propagates: if the NB decoder is wrong (ZINB/Poisson regimes, batch structure), the Fisher information and hence the entire downstream geometry, topology, and coverage are biased.
recommended_experiments: Figure 1 (schematic + thesis): one Fisher-Rao pullback object feeding four roles (distance, Markov-stability functional, persistent-homology filtration, conformal score), making the 'one geometry, not four tools' claim visually explicit.

Metric-ablation on simulated ground truth (splatter/dyngen/dyntoy with planted discrete+continuous mixtures and rare populations at 0.1-5% abundance): rare-population recall/macro-F1 as a function of abundance for our Fisher-Rao pullback vs (a) Euclidean pullback M=J^T J, (b) closed-form simplex FR (GAIA), (c) plain L2 on scVI latent, (d) log1p+PCA+Leiden, (e) PHATE, (f) Markov stability on a Euclidean graph — the causal test that the NB Fisher geometry, specifically, is what helps.

Identifiability experiment: decoder ensemble size K=1,5,10,20; report variance of the metric tensor and stability (ARI/VI) of geodesic distances and cluster assignments with vs without marginalization — the direct answer to the Hauberg/Syrota objection.

Calibration audit with leave-one-donor-out on GSE84133 (Baron) and E-MTAB-5061 (Segerstolpe): empirical vs nominal coverage, mean/median set size, size-stratified coverage, and per-cell-type coverage; compare split vs Mondrian vs clustered conformal (Ding) and the geometry-native score vs THR/APS/RAPS; explicitly plot per-rare-type coverage for epsilon/delta/stellate.

Covariate-shift stress test: healthy->T2D and healthy->HNF1A-perturbed calibration; show marginal coverage degrades under naive conformal and is restored by weighted/covariate-shift conformal; report conformal OOD detection of perturbed cells at controlled FPR.

Discrete-vs-continuous validation: on data known discrete (PBMC immune types) vs known continuous (a differentiation trajectory), show the persistent H0/H1 statistic separates the two regimes with Fasy significance; then apply to the beta-cell compartment to adjudicate HNF1A discrete-switch vs continuous-dedifferentiation.

Full scIB benchmark (Luecken 2022): 14-metric battery with isolated-label F1/ASW, cLISI, graph connectivity, overall=0.4*batch+0.6*bio, against the scIB leaderboard (Harmony, scVI, scANVI, Scanorama); must be competitive on bio-conservation while uniquely adding calibrated coverage and average set size stratified by cell-type frequency.

Scalability curve: wall-clock and memory vs N (1k->250k cells) with the landmark/graph-geodesic and sparse-filtration approximations, plus a certified approximation-error report; demonstrate a run on the 2025 48-donor / 245,878-cell atlas.

Component ablation table: marginal contribution of each module (pullback metric, information-geometric MS, PH discrete/continuous test, conformal layer) to rare-population F1 and to coverage — proving no module is dead weight.

Biological result with orthogonal validation: a conformally-certified aberrant/intermediate beta-cell state along the HNF1A axis, characterized by FXYD2/SLC2A2/PDX1/TMEM27 loss and ER-stress up (DDIT3/HSPA5/ATF4), validated by AUCell module scores and, ideally, smFISH or an independent MODY/perturbation cohort — the discovery needed to lift the paper above a pure methods contribution.

Robustness to decoder misspecification: NB vs ZINB vs Poisson emission, showing the geometry/coverage are stable when the likelihood is correctly specified (per Svensson) and degrade gracefully otherwise.
